question: A data science team has a large dataset of customer records with no labels. They want to automatically group customers based on how similar or different their behaviors are, without predefining any output categories. Which type of machine learning task does this scenario describe?

options.text: Supervised learning using labeled training data
Reinforcement learning with a reward signal
Regression to predict a continuous value

explanation: The notes state that Clustering is the process of grouping UNLABELED data based on similarities and differences, and this is an Unsupervised Learning task. So B is correct. A is wrong because the data has no labels, so it isn't supervised. C (reinforcement learning) and D (regression) are not mentioned and don't fit grouping unlabeled data.

question: A retail company wants to run a clustering algorithm on its dataset. A key requirement is that the team must decide the number of groups in advance, and they have chosen a fast, widely used algorithm that groups data around the mean point (centroid) of each cluster. Which algorithm best matches this description?
explanation: According to the notes, K-means splits data into K clusters around a centroid (mean point), is fast and popular, but requires choosing K in advance. This matches the description exactly, so D is correct. A (Density Based/DBSCAN) finds the number of clusters on its own, so it doesn't need K chosen beforehand. B (Hierarchical) doesn't need K chosen beforehand either. C (K-medoids) uses an actual data point (medoid) as the cluster center rather than a mean centroid.

question: A dataset contains many outliers, and an analyst is concerned that the cluster centers should be actual data points rather than computed averages to make the algorithm more robust to those outliers. Which clustering algorithm should the analyst choose?
explanation: The notes state K-medoids is similar to K-means but the cluster center is an actual data point (medoid) and it is more robust to outliers than K-means. This matches the requirement that the center be a real data point and robust to outliers, so B is correct. A (K-means) uses a centroid that is a mean point and is sensitive to outliers. C (Hierarchical) and D (Density Based) are not described in the notes as using a real data point as center to resist outliers.

question: A team is exploring an unlabeled dataset where they do not know how many clusters exist, and the clusters may have arbitrary (non-spherical) shapes. They also want the algorithm to identify noise points. Which clustering approach, as described, is most appropriate?

options.text: K-means, because it is fast and popular
K-medoids, because it is robust to outliers

explanation: According to the notes, Density Based methods (e.g. DBSCAN) group by point density, automatically determine the number of clusters, and can detect noise and clusters of arbitrary shape. This matches all three requirements (unknown number of clusters in advance, arbitrary cluster shapes, noise detection), so C is correct. A (K-means) requires choosing K in advance and isn't described as detecting noise or arbitrary shapes. B (K-medoids) is robust to outliers but still requires choosing the number of clusters in advance and isn't described as detecting noise. D is supervised, which doesn't fit unlabeled data.

question: A data science team has a large dataset of historical loan applications, each already tagged with whether the applicant defaulted or repaid. They want to train a model to predict the outcome for new applicants. Which type of machine learning task does this scenario describe?

options.text: Unsupervised learning, because the model discovers patterns and applies its own labels
Supervised learning, because the model is trained on labeled data containing the correct answers
Unsupervised learning, because no upfront work is required to prepare the data
Supervised learning, because it needs no existing training data

explanation: According to the notes, Supervised Learning is an ML task that needs training data (labeled data / correct answers) for the machine to learn from. A dataset already labeled default/repaid is exactly labeled data, so this is supervised learning -> B is correct. A and C are wrong because unsupervised learning doesn't use existing labeled training data and assigns its own labels, which doesn't match the scenario. D is wrong because it describes the opposite: supervised learning NEEDS training data, not 'no existing training data'.

question: A retailer has a large collection of unlabeled customer transaction records and wants to automatically group customers into segments with similar purchasing behavior, without providing any predefined categories. Which learning approach and model type best fit this need?
explanation: The notes state Unsupervised Learning receives unlabeled data, discovers patterns on its own, and assigns its own labels; UL models include Clustering, Association, and Dimensionality Reduction. Grouping customers with no predefined labels is a clustering problem -> C is correct. A and B are supervised learning, which requires labeled data, so they don't fit unlabeled data. D is wrong because regression is not a model of unsupervised learning per the notes.

question: Which of the following are listed as supervised learning models? (Choose TWO.)
explanation: According to the notes, Supervised Learning models include Classification and Regression -> A and C are correct. Clustering (B), Association (D), and Dimensionality Reduction (E) are all listed as models of Unsupervised Learning, so they are not supervised.

question: A team is weighing supervised learning against unsupervised learning for an upcoming project. According to the key comparison of these two approaches, which statement is accurate?

options.text: Supervised learning tends to be more accurate than unsupervised learning but requires more upfront work
Unsupervised learning tends to be more accurate than supervised learning and requires more upfront work
Unsupervised learning is fully automated and requires no human intervention to validate its results
Supervised learning requires no labeled data, which makes it less accurate but faster to set up

explanation: The notes state clearly: SL is usually more accurate than UL but requires more upfront preparation effort -> A is correct. B is wrong because it reverses the accuracy comparison. C is wrong because the notes say UL still needs human intervention to validate results. D is wrong because supervised learning NEEDS labeled data (training data), and preparing those labels is exactly what costs upfront effort.

question: A data science team is building a model to forecast next week's average temperature in degrees Celsius based on a historical labeled dataset. Which type of machine learning task best describes this problem?

options.text: Classification, because the output is a discrete category
Regression, because the output is a continuous numeric value
Clustering, because the data points are grouped by similarity
Dimensionality reduction, because the features are plotted in multiple dimensions

explanation: According to the notes, Regression is the process of finding a function to correlate a labeled dataset into a continuous variable/number, e.g. predicting next week's temperature (20°C). Since the output is a continuous numeric value, this is a Regression problem. A is wrong because classification produces discrete categories, not a continuous number. C is wrong because clustering is grouping and doesn't use labeled data. D is wrong because dimensionality reduction reduces feature dimensions rather than predicting a continuous variable.

question: In a regression model, data points (vectors) are plotted on a graph and a regression line is drawn through the dataset. What does the distance of a data point from the regression line represent?

options.text: The label of the data point

explanation: According to the notes, the distance of a vector (dot) from the regression line is called Error. Regression algorithms use this error to predict future variables. A is wrong because a label is the known target value, not a distance. B is wrong because dimension refers to the number of axes in the plotted space. D is wrong because the notes don't describe correlation coefficient as this distance.

question: A machine learning team is evaluating a regression model and notices the training dataset contains many outliers. They want an error metric that is more robust to these outliers. Which metric should they choose?
explanation: According to the notes, MAE (mean absolute error) is more robust to outliers than MSE/RMSE and keeps the original units; the exam notes state that when there are many outliers, MAE should be chosen. A and B are wrong because MSE and RMSE square the error, so they penalize large errors heavily and are very sensitive to outliers. D is wrong because accuracy is a classification metric, not used for regression.

question: A team wants a regression error metric that penalizes large errors heavily but is also expressed in the same unit as the target variable so business stakeholders can interpret it easily. Which metric best meets both requirements?
explanation: According to the notes, RMSE is the square root of MSE, has the same units as the target variable so it's easy to interpret, and is still sensitive to outliers / heavily penalizes large errors — satisfying both requirements. A is wrong because although MSE heavily penalizes large errors, its units are squared, making it hard to interpret. C is wrong because MAE is robust to outliers and doesn't heavily penalize large errors. D is wrong because slope is not an error metric.

question: An engineer is describing the Mean Squared Error (MSE) metric used in regression. Which TWO statements about MSE are correct according to its characteristics? (Choose TWO.)

options.text: It penalizes large errors heavily, making it very sensitive to outliers
It is more robust to outliers than MAE
Its unit is squared, which makes it harder to interpret
It is only used for classification tasks
It removes the need for a labeled dataset

explanation: According to the notes, MSE is the mean of the squared errors, which heavily penalizes large errors, making it very sensitive to outliers (A correct), and its squared units make it hard to interpret (C correct). B is wrong because it's actually MAE that is more robust to outliers than MSE, not the other way around. D is wrong because MSE is used for regression, not classification. E is wrong because regression is Supervised Learning and always requires labeled data.

question: A retail company has a large historical dataset in which every past transaction is already tagged as either "fraud" or "legitimate." The team wants to build a model that returns a precise prediction for whether each new transaction is fraudulent. Which type of machine learning is the BEST fit for this scenario?

options.text: Supervised learning, because the data is labeled and the goal is to return a specific predicted value
Unsupervised learning, because the model should discover structure in the data on its own
Reinforcement learning, because the model must interact with an environment to reach a goal
Unsupervised learning, because the outcome does not need to be precise

explanation: According to the notes, Supervised Learning is used when the data is labeled and you want to return an accurate/specific predicted value (task-driven, make a prediction). The transaction data is already labeled fraud/legitimate and requires an accurate prediction, so this is Supervised. B and D are wrong because Unsupervised is used when there are NO labels and the outcome doesn't need to be precise. C is wrong because Reinforcement Learning has no available dataset but learns through interacting with an environment to achieve a goal.

question: A data science team receives a large set of customer records with NO labels. They do not have a specific target value to predict; instead, they want to make sense of the data by grouping similar customers together to reveal underlying patterns. Which machine learning approach and example task align with this need?
explanation: The notes describe Unsupervised Learning: data is unlabeled, the model assigns its own labels, data-driven — recognizing structure/patterns, used when labels are unknown and you want to understand the data; examples include Clustering. Grouping similar customers is exactly Clustering, so C is correct. B and D are Supervised (require labeled data and predict a specific value), so they are wrong. A is Reinforcement Learning (learning through an environment), which doesn't fit grouping existing data.

question: A robotics team is building an AI agent for robot navigation. There is no pre-existing dataset; instead, the agent generates its own data through many attempts within an environment as it learns to reach a goal. Which characteristic BEST describes this machine learning paradigm according to the described approaches?

options.text: It is data-driven and focuses on recognizing structure in unlabeled data
It is task-driven and returns precise predictions from labeled data
It relies on a fully labeled training dataset prepared in advance
It is decisions-driven, with the model generating data over many attempts in an environment to reach a goal

explanation: The notes define Reinforcement Learning: no available dataset, there is an environment and the model generates data through repeated trials to achieve a goal, decisions-driven; examples include Robot Navigation. So D is correct. A describes Unsupervised (data-driven, structure in unlabeled data). B describes Supervised (task-driven, accurate prediction from labeled data). C is wrong because RL doesn't rely on a pre-prepared labeled dataset.

question: An ML lead is categorizing techniques by the learning paradigm they belong to. Which of the following are example tasks specifically associated with Unsupervised Learning? (Choose TWO.)
explanation: According to the notes, examples of Unsupervised Learning include Clustering, Dimensionality Reduction, and Association, so A and C are correct. B (Regression) and E (Classification) are examples of Supervised Learning. D (Game AI) is an example of Reinforcement Learning. So only A and C belong to Unsupervised.

question: A data science team is building a model to predict whether an email message is spam or not spam. They have a large labeled dataset of past emails and want a fast algorithm that works well with text features. Based on classification fundamentals, which algorithm is most suitable for this text/spam classification task?
explanation: According to the notes, Naive Bayes is based on Bayes' theorem, assumes independent features, is fast, and is especially suited to text/spam classification, making it the most fitting choice. K-NN is a lazy learner sensitive to scale and K, and isn't noted as optimal for text. Neural Networks learn complex nonlinear relationships but need lots of data and computation, not a fast choice. Random Forest is more accurate/stable than a Decision Tree, but the notes don't tie it to text/spam problems.

question: A machine learning engineer trains a single Decision Tree classifier and observes that it overfits the training data. A colleague recommends switching to an ensemble method that combines many trees to improve accuracy and stability. Which algorithm is being recommended?
explanation: The notes state Decision Trees easily overfit, while Random Forest is an ensemble of many trees (bagging) that improves accuracy and stability, matching the scenario exactly. Logistic Regression is a linear model using sigmoid, not a tree ensemble. Naive Bayes is based on Bayes' theorem. K-NN classifies by the K nearest points, not by an ensemble of decision trees.

question: An analyst needs a simple, interpretable baseline model for a binary classification problem and wants the model to output a probability using a sigmoid function. Which classification algorithm best matches these requirements?
explanation: The notes describe Logistic Regression as using sigmoid to output probabilities, being linear and easy to interpret, and serving as a baseline for binary classification — matching the requirement exactly. Neural Networks learn complex nonlinear relationships and need lots of data/computation, not a simple baseline. Random Forest is a tree ensemble. K-NN is a lazy learner using majority vote, not sigmoid probability output.

question: A team is categorizing a labeled dataset by finding a function that separates the data into distinct categories such as "Sunny" and "Rainy", where a boundary places data on one side into one class and data on the other side into another. Which type of machine learning task does this describe?
explanation: The notes define Classification as the process of finding a function to split a labeled dataset into classes/categories, with a classification line separating the data (e.g. Sunny/Rainy), and state that Classification is a Supervised Learning task. Since the data is already labeled, this isn't clustering (unsupervised). Reinforcement Learning and dimensionality reduction aren't mentioned and don't describe classifying labeled data.

question: A team is evaluating K-Nearest Neighbors (K-NN) for a classification task. According to classification fundamentals, which TWO statements correctly describe K-NN? (Choose TWO.)

options.text: It is a lazy learner that does not perform real training.
It uses a sigmoid function to output class probabilities.
Its performance is sensitive to feature scaling and the choice of K.
It is an ensemble of many decision trees built with bagging.
It assumes all features are conditionally independent.

explanation: The notes describe K-NN as classifying by the K nearest points via majority vote, being a lazy learner that doesn't really 'train' (answer A), and being sensitive to scale and the value of K (answer C). Outputting probabilities via sigmoid is a feature of Logistic Regression (B is wrong). Combining many trees via bagging is Random Forest (D is wrong). Assuming independent features belongs to Naive Bayes (E is wrong).

question: A data science team is studying the historical foundations of neural networks. They want to identify the earliest single-neuron model that served as a precursor to modern, larger neural networks and was used for supervised learning of binary classifiers. Which model matches this description?
explanation: Theo notes, perceptron la mot mo hinh don neuron (single-neuron model), duoc dung cho supervised learning of binary classifiers, va la tien than (precursor) cua cac neural network lon hon. Cac phuong an B, C, D deu khong duoc de cap trong notes va khong phai la mo hinh don neuron so khai duoc mo ta.

question: During a briefing on early AI history, an engineer needs to state what the Mark 1 perceptron was originally designed to do. According to the historical description, what was its intended task?
explanation: Notes ghi ro Mark 1 perceptron duoc thiet ke cho image recognition (nhan dang hinh anh). Cac phuong an con lai (speech-to-text, dich may, du bao chuoi thoi gian) khong duoc neu trong notes va khong phai muc dich cua Mark 1 perceptron.

question: A trainer is preparing a timeline slide about the perceptron. Based on the historical notes, which statements correctly describe key facts about the perceptron? (Choose TWO.)

options.text: The perceptron algorithm was invented in 1943.
The perceptron was invented specifically for unsupervised clustering.
The physical perceptron machine was built in 1957.
The perceptron was a multi-layer deep network from its inception.
The perceptron replaced all later neural network research.

explanation: Theo notes: thuat toan perceptron duoc phat minh nam 1943 (dung A), va co may (machine) duoc xay dung nam 1957 (dung C). B sai vi perceptron dung cho supervised learning of binary classifiers, khong phai unsupervised clustering. D sai vi perceptron la mo hinh don neuron (single-neuron), khong phai mang deep nhieu lop. E sai va khong co trong notes; nguoc lai perceptron la tien than cho cac neural network lon hon.

question: A data scientist is explaining how a single node in a neural network processes information. According to the standard sequence, what happens first when data arrives at a node, immediately before any further processing occurs?

options.text: All of the arriving input data is summed together
The activation function is triggered to produce the output
The output is passed directly to the next layer
The node is marked as inactive by default

explanation: According to the notes, when data reaches a node capable of computation, the first step is that all input data is summed, and ONLY AFTER THAT is the activation function triggered. B is wrong because the activation function is only triggered after the sum is computed. C is wrong because passing the output to the next layer only happens after the activation function. D is wrong because the active/inactive state is determined based on the output, not by default.

question: An engineer wants to describe the role of an activation function within a neural network node. Which description best matches its function?

options.text: It acts as a gate between nodes that determines whether output proceeds to the next layer
It sums all the incoming input values before any computation
It permanently stores the weights learned during training
It converts raw text prompts into numerical embeddings

explanation: According to the notes, the activation function acts as a 'gate between nodes' and decides whether the output is passed on to the next layer, i.e. it determines whether the node is active or inactive. B is wrong because summing the inputs happens BEFORE the activation function, not as its function. C and D are concepts not mentioned in the notes at all.

question: During a review of neural network fundamentals, a team asks how an activation function decides whether a node is active or inactive. On what basis is this decision made?

options.text: Based on the total number of nodes in the previous layer
Based on the node's own output value
Based on the size of the training dataset
Based on the learning rate configured for the model

explanation: According to the notes, the activation function 'determines if a node is active or inactive based on its own output'. So B is correct. A, C, D refer to the number of nodes, dataset size, and learning rate — factors not mentioned in the notes as the basis for determining active/inactive state.

question: A practitioner is documenting the possible output ranges that an activation function can produce for a node. According to the notes, which of the following are valid output ranges? (Choose TWO.)
explanation: According to the notes, the output of an activation function 'could be a range between 0 to 1 or -1 or 1'. So A and B are both correct and independent of each other. C (0 to 100), D (1 to 1000), and E (-255 to 255) are ranges not mentioned in the notes at all.

question: A financial services company wants to build a model that flags whether a submitted transaction is fraudulent or legitimate. The training data is fully labeled into these two categories. Which type of classical machine learning task best fits this use case?

options.text: Regression, because the model predicts a variable in the future
Classification, a supervised learning task that predicts what category the data belongs to
Clustering, an unsupervised learning task that groups data by similarities
Association, an unsupervised learning task that finds relationships between variables

explanation: According to the notes, Classification is a Supervised Learning task: data is labeled into categories and the model predicts which category the data belongs to, with the main use case being Identity Fraud Detection. So B is correct. A is wrong because Regression is used to predict a future variable (Market Forecast), not to classify. C and D are wrong because Clustering and Association belong to Unsupervised Learning (data is NOT labeled), whereas in this problem the data is already labeled.

question: A retail analytics team has customer data that is NOT labeled. They want the model to automatically recognize a structure or pattern in the data rather than make a specific prediction. Which characterization of this approach is correct?

options.text: It is supervised learning, which is task-driven and makes a prediction
It is regression, which predicts a variable in the future
It is unsupervised learning, which is data-driven and recognizes a structure or pattern
It is classification, which predicts what category the data belongs to

explanation: The notes define Unsupervised Learning as data that is NOT labeled, being data-driven — recognizing a structure or pattern. This matches the scenario exactly, so C is correct. A is wrong because Supervised Learning is task-driven (make a prediction) and requires labeled data. B (Regression) and D (Classification) are both Supervised Learning tasks, requiring labeled data and aiming to predict, which contradicts the unlabeled data scenario.

question: A data scientist is preparing a very large dataset before feeding it into a downstream model. She wants to reduce the amount of data as a pre-processing step. According to classical ML concepts, which technique matches this goal?
explanation: According to the notes, Dimensionality Reduction belongs to Unsupervised Learning and is aimed at reducing the amount of data as a pre-processing step — matching the scenario exactly, so D is correct. A (Association) is used to find relationships between variables (Customer Recommendation). B (Clustering) is used to group data by similarities/differences (Targeted Marketing). C (Regression) is a supervised task predicting a future variable. No other option describes reducing data for pre-processing.

question: A company is reviewing classical machine learning approaches and lists several tasks. Which TWO of the following are unsupervised learning tasks as described for classical ML? (Choose TWO.)

options.text: Clustering, used for targeted marketing by grouping data by similarities and differences
Classification, used for identity fraud detection
Association, used for customer recommendation by finding a relationship between variables
Nearest Neighbor prediction of a labeled category

explanation: The notes list the tasks of Unsupervised Learning as including Clustering (grouping data by similarities/differences, use case Targeted Marketing) and Association (finding relationships between variables, use case Customer Recommendation), so A and D are correct. B (Classification) and C (Regression) are Supervised Learning tasks. E is wrong because Nearest Neighbor is mentioned as an example of a Classical ML algorithm from 1967, not classified by the notes as an unsupervised task; moreover, predicting a labeled category is supervised in nature.

question: A data science team needs to build a model that processes sequential data such as text, time-series, and audio, where earlier inputs influence the interpretation of later ones. According to the ML division framework, which neural network type is most appropriate?
explanation: According to the notes, RNN (Recurrent) has loops/memory, so it processes sequential data (text, time-series, audio) — matching the requirement. CNN uses convolutional filters for images/computer vision, not sequential data. GAN consists of a generator vs. discriminator to produce realistic fake data. MLP is a basic feed-forward fully-connected network, suited to tabular data.

question: A team wants to reduce variance and combat overfitting by training many models in parallel on bootstrap samples and then averaging or voting their predictions. Which ensemble method does this describe?
explanation: According to the notes, Bagging trains many models in parallel on bootstrap samples and then averages/votes, reducing variance (fighting overfitting), e.g. Random Forest — matching exactly. Boosting trains sequentially, with each model correcting the previous model's errors, reducing bias. Stacking combines multiple different model types via a meta-model. Reinforcement Learning is not an ensemble method but a type of ML used when there is no data.

question: According to the ML division framework, which category of machine learning is described as being used when there is no data, covering use cases like real-time decisions, game AI, and robot navigation?
explanation: According to the notes, Reinforcement Learning is used when there is no data, with use cases such as Real-time Decisions, Game AI, Learning Tasks, Robot Navigation — matching the description. Classical ML is used for simple data with clear features. Ensemble Methods are used when data quality is an issue. Neural Networks/Deep Learning are used when data is complex and/or features are unclear.

question: A team needs high accuracy on a tabular dataset and plans to train models sequentially, where each new model corrects the errors of the previous one to reduce bias. Which ensemble technique and example algorithm align with this approach?
explanation: According to the notes, Boosting trains sequentially, with each model correcting the previous model's errors, reducing bias and giving high accuracy, e.g. XGBoost, AdaBoost — matching exactly. Bagging (e.g. Random Forest) trains in parallel on bootstrap samples to reduce variance, not by sequential error correction. Stacking combines multiple different model types via a meta-model. Option D is mismatched because XGBoost belongs to Boosting, not Bagging.

question: An engineer chooses Auto Encoders for a project. Based on the ML division notes, which TWO statements correctly describe Auto Encoders? (Choose TWO.)

options.text: They are a supervised learning method requiring labeled targets.
They encode (compress) data and then decode (reconstruct) it.
They are commonly used for dimensionality reduction and anomaly detection.
They use convolutional filters specialized for computer vision.
They pit a generator against a discriminator to produce realistic fake data.

explanation: According to the notes, Auto Encoders compress (encode) then reconstruct (decode) data (B correct) and are unsupervised, used for dimensionality reduction and anomaly detection (C correct). A is wrong because Auto Encoders are unsupervised, not supervised. D describes CNN (convolutional filters for computer vision). E describes GAN (generator vs. discriminator producing realistic fake data).

question: A data scientist is designing a basic perceptron to process feature vectors that each contain an X coordinate and a Y coordinate. How many nodes should the input layer contain?

options.text: One node, because the network performs a single classification
Two nodes, because the input vectors have two dimensions
Four nodes, because both inputs and outputs must be represented
The number of nodes is determined during training as the weights are adjusted

explanation: Theo notes, so node o input layer duoc xac dinh boi so chieu (dimensions) cua vector dau vao; mot vector co X va Y = 2 chieu nen can 2 input node. A sai vi so output node (khong phai input node) moi phu thuoc vao ung dung/kieu phan loai. C them du kien khong co trong notes. D sai vi chi co weights duoc dieu chinh trong training, con so input node do so chieu du lieu quyet dinh, khong phai do training.

question: A team builds a perceptron for a simple Yes/No classification task using input vectors that have many dimensions. How many nodes will the output layer contain?

options.text: The same number of nodes as the input layer
Two nodes, one for Yes and one for No
One node, because the application is a Yes/No classification
As many nodes as there are weighted connections in the network

explanation: Notes neu ro so node o output layer duoc xac dinh boi ung dung cua mang; vi du phan loai Yes/No chi can mot output node, bat ke co bao nhieu input node. A sai vi output khong nhat thiet bang input. B la bay truc giac nhung notes ghi ro Yes/No = mot output node. D them du kien khong co trong notes.

question: During the training process of a perceptron network, which element is modified to produce a better outcome?

options.text: The number of nodes in the input layer
The weights on the connections between nodes
The number of dimensions of the input vectors
The number of nodes in the output layer

explanation: Notes ghi: du lieu di chuyen giua cac node duoc nhan voi weights, va weights duoc dieu chinh trong qua trinh training de cho ket qua tot hon. A va D sai vi so node input/output do so chieu du lieu va ung dung quyet dinh, khong thay doi khi training. C sai vi so chieu cua vector dau vao la thuoc tinh cua du lieu, khong phai thu duoc huan luyen.

question: An engineer is reviewing how data flows through a basic perceptron. Which statements correctly describe the roles of the layers according to the network's design? (Choose TWO.)

options.text: The input layer acts only as connection points and does not modify the data
The input layer multiplies incoming data by weights before passing it on
Output nodes can modify or compute new values based on the inputted data
The output layer only forwards data without performing any computation
Every layer must contain the same number of nodes as the input vector dimensions

explanation: Notes neu hai du kien doc lap: (A) input layer chi la diem ket noi va khong sua doi du lieu; (C) output node (va cac layer khac) co the sua doi/tinh toan gia tri moi dua tren du lieu dau vao. B sai vi viec nhan weights xay ra khi du lieu di chuyen giua cac node, con input layer ban than khong sua doi du lieu. D trai nguoc voi (C). E them du kien khong co trong notes - chi input layer moi phu thuoc so chieu, con output layer phu thuoc ung dung.

question: A machine learning team is documenting their model architectures. One architecture is a neural network that contains an input layer, four hidden layers, and an output layer. According to the standard definition, how should this network be classified?

options.text: It is a deep learning network because it has 3 or more hidden layers.
It is a shallow network because it only has one output layer.
It is a recurrent network because it contains multiple hidden layers.
It is not a valid neural network because it has more than three hidden layers.

explanation: According to the notes, Deep Learning is a neural network with 3 or more hidden layers. This network has 4 hidden layers, so it is deep learning (A correct). B is wrong because the number of output layers doesn't define 'shallow'. C is wrong because having many hidden layers doesn't make it recurrent (RNN is characterized by memory/loops). D is wrong because there is no upper limit on the number of hidden layers.

question: During training of a neural network, a process moves backwards through the network and adjusts the weights so the model performs better on the next iteration. Which mechanism does this describe?
explanation: The notes define Backpropagation as the process of passing back through the network, adjusting weights to improve results in the next iteration — this is how a neural network learns (B correct). A (activation function) is an algorithm applied at hidden layer nodes affecting output, and doesn't adjust weights backward. C (loss function) only compares ground truth with the prediction to compute error, without adjusting weights itself. D (feed forward) is data moving forward, not backward.

question: A data scientist needs to describe the component of a neural network that compares the ground truth to the model's prediction in order to determine how badly the network performed. Which component is being described?
explanation: According to the notes, the Loss Function compares ground truth with the prediction to determine the error rate (how poorly the network is performing) — exactly C. A (weight) is the strength of connections between neurons, and doesn't compare prediction with reality. B (activation function) is an algorithm applied at hidden layer nodes, e.g. ReLU. D (hidden layer) refers to the layers between input and output, not an error-measuring mechanism.

question: An engineer is building a model for an image classification task and wants a deep learning algorithm designed to capture spatial features from images. Which supervised deep learning architecture is the standard choice for this scenario?
explanation: The notes state CNN uses convolutional filters to capture spatial features and is the standard for images (B correct). A (RNN) has memory/loops, suited to sequential data like text/time-series, not images. C (RBM) and D (DBN) belong to the unsupervised group, not the standard choice for supervised image classification.

question: A team is processing sequential data such as text and time-series and needs a supervised deep learning architecture that maintains memory across steps. Which architecture fits this requirement?
explanation: The notes describe RNN as having memory/loops for sequential data (text, time-series) — matching the requirement exactly (C). A (CNN) uses convolutional filters for spatial features, standard for images. B (SAE) is unsupervised, stacking autoencoders to learn compressed representations. D (FNN) is a fully-connected network that only moves forward, with no loop/memory, suited to tabular data.

question: A researcher is reviewing unsupervised deep learning building blocks. Which of the following statements are accurate according to the reference material? (Choose TWO.)

options.text: A Restricted Boltzmann Machine (RBM) is a two-layer network (visible/hidden) that learns a probability distribution and serves as a building block of a DBN.
A Deep Belief Network (DBN) stacks multiple RBMs to learn hierarchical features.
A Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) is an unsupervised algorithm used for pre-training.
A Feed Forward Neural Network (FNN) forms cycles between its nodes to retain memory.
A Loss function adjusts the network weights by moving backwards through the network.

explanation: The notes state RBM is a 2-layer network (visible/hidden) that learns a probability distribution and is the building block of DBN (A correct); DBN stacks multiple RBMs to learn hierarchical features (B correct). C is wrong because CNN is a supervised algorithm for images, not unsupervised pre-training. D is wrong because FNN doesn't create loops — connections always move forward, with no cycle. E is wrong because a loss function only computes error; adjusting weights backward is backpropagation.

question: A machine learning team is experimenting with an early neural network built from scratch. They configure a neuron to use a binary step activation function. Which type of task is this activation function suited for?

options.text: Multi-class image classification across hundreds of categories
Binary classification such as On/Off or True/False decisions
Continuous numeric regression to predict house prices
Time-series forecasting with smooth probability outputs

explanation: According to the notes, the binary step activation function only returns 0 or 1 and 'Can only handle binary classification (On/Off, True/False, 0/1)'. So B is correct. A is wrong because this is multi-class classification, not binary. C is wrong because this function doesn't produce a continuous numeric output for regression. D is wrong because the notes don't mention forecasting or smooth probabilities; the output is only 0 or 1.

question: An engineer passes different input values through a binary step activation function to understand its output behavior. According to how the function is defined, what does it return for an input value of exactly 0.0 versus an input value greater than 0.0?

options.text: An input of 0.0 returns 1, and any input greater than 0.0 returns 0
Both an input of 0.0 and inputs greater than 0.0 return 1
An input of 0.0 returns 0, and any input greater than 0.0 returns 1
An input of 0.0 returns a value between 0 and 1 depending on its magnitude

explanation: According to the notes: 'value 0.0 or less → 0' and 'value greater than 0.0 → 1'. So an input equal to 0.0 returns 0, while an input greater than 0.0 returns 1, matching C. A reverses the rule, so it's wrong. B is wrong because 0.0 must return 0, not 1. D is wrong because the function only returns 0 or 1, with no intermediate value (range {0,1}, bound).

question: A data scientist is reviewing the properties of the binary step activation function before deciding whether to use it in a modern deep learning model. Which two statements accurately describe this function according to its documented characteristics? (Choose TWO.)

options.text: Its output range is bounded to the set {0,1} rather than being infinite
It is one of the earliest activation functions and is not used much today
It produces an unbounded, infinite range of output values
It is the most widely used activation function in current deep networks
It outputs smooth continuous probabilities between 0 and 1

explanation: According to the notes, this function has 'Range {0,1} — bound (not infinite)', so A is correct, and 'One of the earliest activation functions; not used much today', so B is correct. C is wrong because the range is bounded, not infinite. D is wrong because the notes say the opposite — it is not used much today. E is wrong because the output is only 0 or 1, not a smooth continuous probability.

question: A retail company wants to predict whether tomorrow's weather will be classified as "Cold" or "Hot" so it can plan inventory. Which type of supervised learning model is most appropriate for producing this kind of output?

options.text: A regression model, because it outputs a continuous number
A classification model, because it divides the dataset into discrete classes or categories
A regression model, because it fits a curve using exponents
A classification model, because it always predicts a continuous variable

explanation: According to the notes, Classification is the process of finding a function to split a dataset into classes/categories (e.g. exactly 'Will it be Cold or Hot tomorrow?'). Since the output is two discrete classes, this is a classification problem → B correct. A and C are wrong because regression outputs a continuous variable (a number), which doesn't fit classifying Cold/Hot. D is wrong because classification does NOT predict a continuous variable — that's a feature of regression.

question: A data science team must build a model to estimate the exact temperature (in degrees) that will occur tomorrow. According to supervised learning definitions, which statement correctly describes the required approach?

options.text: Regression is required because the target is a continuous variable/number
Classification is required because the target divides data into categories
Classification is required because the output is a straight line y = ax + b
Regression is required because it separates the data into discrete classes

explanation: The notes define Regression as the process of finding a function to correlate a dataset into a continuous variable/number (e.g. 'What is the temperature going to be tomorrow?'). Predicting an exact temperature is a continuous variable → A correct. B and C are wrong because classifying into categories is classification, not a continuous-number prediction problem. D mischaracterizes regression (regression doesn't split data into discrete classes).

question: An ML engineer is working with a dataset that has many dimensions (features) and needs a classification algorithm that separates the classes by finding the separating hyperplane with the largest possible margin. Which algorithm matches this description?
explanation: According to the notes, Support Vector Machines (SVM) find the hyperplane that separates classes with the maximum margin and are strong with high-dimensional data — matching the description exactly → C correct. A and D are regression algorithms (continuous variable), not classification. B (Naive Bayes) is a classification algorithm, but the notes don't describe it via the hyperplane/margin mechanism, so it doesn't match the question's description.

question: A team has data whose classes are NOT linearly separable. They want a technique that maps the data into a higher-dimensional space using the "kernel trick" so the classes can be separated. Which algorithm should they use?
explanation: The notes state Kernel SVM is SVM using the 'kernel trick' to separate NON-linear data by mapping it into a higher-dimensional space — matching the description → A. B is multivariate linear regression, and doesn't handle nonlinear classification via the kernel trick. C (KNN) is a classification algorithm, but the notes don't tie it to the kernel trick. D (SVR) applies the SVM idea to regression (predicting a number), not to separating nonlinear classes.

question: A modeling team is choosing regression techniques. They want to (1) model a target using a single input variable that produces a straight line of the form y = ax + b, and separately (2) capture a non-linear relationship by adding exponents to fit a curve. Which TWO algorithms from the notes match these needs? (Choose TWO.)
explanation: According to the notes: Simple Linear Regression uses 1 input variable to form a straight line y = ax + b (matches requirement 1) → A correct; Polynomial Regression adds exponents to fit a curve, modeling nonlinear relationships (matches requirement 2) → C correct. B (Kernel SVM), D (Naive Bayes), and E (Random Forest Classification) are all classification algorithms, not regression, so they don't fit the requirement of predicting a continuous variable.

question: A data scientist is building a neural network that classifies product images into one of ten distinct categories. The final layer must output a probability distribution across all ten classes so the model can select the most likely category. Which activation function is designed for this output requirement?
explanation: According to the notes, Softmax 'converts output to probabilities for multiple classifications' — correct for a multi-class problem (10 classes) at the output layer. Sigmoid is used for binary classification, not multi-class. ReLU only zeroes out negative values and doesn't produce a probability distribution. Binary Step only activates when a threshold is crossed and doesn't produce probabilities.

question: During training of a deep neural network, an ML engineer notices that gradients become extremely small in the earlier layers, slowing learning. According to activation function characteristics, which activation functions are known to be susceptible to the vanishing gradient problem? (Choose TWO.)
explanation: The notes clearly state Sigmoid is 'susceptible to the vanishing gradient problem' and Tanh (a rescaled version of Sigmoid) is 'still susceptible to vanishing gradient'. These are two independently correct facts. ReLU is the most popular function and zeroes negative values (associated with dying ReLU rather than vanishing gradient in the notes). Softmax is used for multi-class probabilities, and Maxout selects the max of the inputs — neither is tied to vanishing gradient in the notes.

question: An engineer wants to stack multiple layers in a neural network and train them using backpropagation. Which statement about activation function categories is correct?

options.text: Linear activation functions can perform backpropagation, while non-linear ones cannot.
Linear activation functions cannot perform backpropagation, whereas non-linear activation functions can and allow stacking many layers.
Both linear and non-linear activation functions equally support stacking many layers.
Non-linear activation functions cannot be stacked into multiple layers.

explanation: The notes state Linear Activation Functions 'can't do backpropagation', while Non-Linear Activation Functions 'can do backpropagation, can stack (have many) layers'. So B is correct. A reverses this. C is wrong because linear can't backprop, so it doesn't support this like non-linear does. D directly contradicts the notes (non-linear can stack many layers).

question: A team is comparing variants of ReLU. They want an activation where the slope applied to negative values is not fixed but is instead learned during training, rather than being fixed at a small value such as 0.01x. Which activation function matches this description?
explanation: According to the notes, Parameterised ReLU is a 'type of Leaky ReLU where the negative slope is a learnable parameter (not fixed at 0.01x like Leaky ReLU)' — matching the requirement of a learned negative slope. Leaky ReLU uses a small fixed slope (0.01x), which isn't learnable. ELU is similar to ReLU and saturates large negative values, with no mention of a learnable slope. Standard ReLU simply zeroes negative values.

question: An ML practitioner is documenting activation functions for a knowledge base. Which pairing of an activation function with its described behavior is correct?

options.text: Swish — an alternative to ReLU introduced by the Google Brain Team.
Maxout — converts outputs into probabilities for multiple classifications.
ReLU — counters the dying ReLU problem using a small slope for negative values.
Sigmoid — treats any negative value as 0.

explanation: The notes describe Swish as an 'alternative to ReLU by the Google Brain Team', so A is correct. B is wrong because Maxout 'chooses output to be the max of the inputs'; converting output into multi-class probabilities is Softmax's role. C is wrong because countering dying ReLU with a small negative slope is Leaky ReLU's role, not ReLU's. D is wrong because 'treats any negative value as 0' describes ReLU, not Sigmoid (Sigmoid is used for binary classification).

question: A machine learning engineer configures a neural network using only a linear activation function (the identity function) in every layer. Despite adding more hidden layers, the model fails to capture the non-linear relationships in the dataset. Which characteristic of the linear activation function best explains this behavior?

options.text: The linear activation function outputs values only between 0 and 1, clipping the signal.
Because the model behaves as if it is linear, stacking layers has no effect and it cannot handle complex, non-linear data.
The function introduces excessive non-linearity, causing the gradients to explode.
The derivative changes at every layer, preventing the network from converging.

explanation: According to the notes, a model using linear activation behaves as if it were linear and cannot handle complex, nonlinear data; it also cannot stack layers (it's equivalent to just one layer). So B is correct. A is wrong because the range is (-∞, ∞), not bounded to 0-1. C is wrong because a linear function doesn't create non-linearity — quite the opposite, it lacks non-linearity. D is wrong because the derivative of a linear function is always 1 (constant).

question: A data scientist reviews the mathematical properties of the linear (identity) activation function to understand its limitations during model training. What is the derivative of the linear activation function, and what does this imply?

options.text: The derivative is 0, so no signal passes through the neuron.
The derivative equals the input value, scaling the gradient dynamically.
The derivative is 1, meaning what you put in is what comes out.
The derivative is bounded between -1 and 1, similar to tanh.

explanation: The notes state clearly: Derivative is 1 — what you put in is what comes out (since f(x)=x). So C is correct. A is wrong because the derivative equals 1, not 0. B is wrong because the derivative is the constant 1, not dependent on the input. D is wrong because that describes tanh, not the linear function.

question: An engineer must justify why a neural network built entirely on a linear activation function is unsuitable for deep learning tasks. Which TWO characteristics of the linear activation function support this conclusion? (Choose TWO.)

options.text: It cannot stack layers and effectively only ever has one layer.
It outputs a bounded range between 0 and 1.
It aggressively improves the error term after each epoch.
It has a derivative that varies with the input magnitude.

explanation: The notes list two independent characteristics: (1) Cannot perform Backpropagation, and (2) Cannot stack layers — only ever has one layer. So A and B are correct. C is wrong because the range is (-∞, ∞), unbounded. D is wrong because the notes say the model doesn't really learn and doesn't improve the error term. E is wrong because the derivative is always 1, regardless of the input.

question: While selecting an activation function, a team notes that one candidate produces outputs across the entire real-number line. Which activation function has an unbounded range of (-∞, ∞)?
explanation: According to the notes, the linear (identity) function has Range (-∞, ∞) — unbound (infinite). So B is correct. A, C, D are other functions not described with an infinite range in the notes (sigmoid and softmax give values between 0-1, tanh gives -1 to 1); only the linear function is stated to be unbound.

question: A data science team needs to segment customers using an unlabeled dataset. They do not know in advance how many segments exist, and they also want the algorithm to automatically flag outliers as noise. Which clustering algorithm best fits these requirements?
explanation: DBScan clusters by density, automatically determines the number of clusters, and detects noise/outliers, matching the requirement of not knowing the number of clusters in advance and automatically flagging outliers. K-Means (A) requires choosing K in advance, so it doesn't fit. K-Modes (C) is a variant for categorical data and doesn't address the cluster-count or outlier issue. Apriori (D) is an association algorithm (finding frequent itemsets), not clustering.

question: An analyst is clustering a dataset composed entirely of categorical attributes (such as color, region, and product type). Which algorithm is specifically designed to handle this type of data?

options.text: K-Means, because it groups data around centroids
PCA, because it reduces the number of dimensions
K-Modes, because it uses the mode instead of the mean
t-SNE, because it visualizes clusters in 2D

explanation: K-Modes is a variant of K-Means for categorical data, using mode instead of mean. K-Means (A) is suited to numeric data, so it doesn't fit categorical data. PCA (B) is dimensionality reduction, not a clustering algorithm. t-SNE (D) is only for visualizing clusters in 2D/3D, not a clustering algorithm for categorical data.

question: A retail company wants to build a recommendation feature that suggests complementary products, for example: "If someone buys bread, suggest butter." Which category of unsupervised learning addresses this market-basket style problem?
explanation: Association finds relationships between variables (e.g. buying bread suggests butter — items frequently bought together), matching a market-basket problem. Clustering (A) groups unlabeled data by similarity, not by finding co-purchase relationships. Dimensionality Reduction (C) reduces data dimensions. Linear Discriminant Analysis (D) is a supervised dimensionality-reduction technique and is unrelated.

question: A team is comparing association rule mining algorithms for performance. Which statements accurately describe how these algorithms differ? (Choose TWO.)

options.text: Apriori is fast because it scans the dataset only once
FP-Growth compresses data into an FP-tree and avoids candidate generation, making it much faster than Apriori
Euclat uses a vertical (tidset) representation to intersect sets faster than Apriori
Apriori uses an FP-tree to avoid multiple scans of the data
FP-Growth is a clustering algorithm that groups items by density

explanation: FP-Growth compresses data into an FP-tree and doesn't generate candidates, so it's much faster than Apriori (B correct); Eclat uses a vertical (tidset) representation for faster set intersection than Apriori (C correct). A is wrong because Apriori is slow due to scanning the data multiple times, not just once. D is wrong because the FP-tree is a feature of FP-Growth, not Apriori. E is wrong because FP-Growth is an association algorithm, not density-based clustering.

question: A machine learning engineer needs a dimensionality reduction technique that uses the class labels to maximize the separation between classes. Which technique meets this requirement?
explanation: Linear Discriminant Analysis is a supervised dimensionality-reduction technique that uses labels to maximize separation between classes. PCA (A) is unsupervised, projecting data onto axes that retain the most variance, without using labels. Latent Dirichlet Allocation (C) shares the LDA acronym but is a topic-modeling technique for text. t-SNE (D) is nonlinear dimensionality reduction purely for visualization, not using labels to separate classes.

question: A team wants to project high-dimensional embeddings into a 2D plot so that analysts can visually inspect how data points cluster. They do not intend to use the output as input features for a downstream model. Which technique is the most appropriate choice?

options.text: SVD, because it decomposes matrices and compresses data
PCA, because it retains the most variance for modeling
t-SNE, because it is a nonlinear technique intended for visualizing clusters in 2D/3D
K-Means, because it assigns points to centroids

explanation: t-SNE is a nonlinear dimensionality-reduction technique used to visualize clusters in 2D/3D, and per the notes it's only used for visualization, not as a feature — matching the requirement exactly. SVD (A) is matrix decomposition/data compression, the foundation for PCA and recommender systems, not for cluster visualization. PCA (B) preserves variance and is typically used for pre-processing/features, not purely for visualization. K-Means (D) is a clustering algorithm, not a projection technique for visualization.

question: A machine learning team is comparing activation functions for a deep neural network. They need an activation function whose output values are bounded within the range (-1, 1). Which activation function meets this requirement?
explanation: According to the notes, Tanh has Range (-1,1), matching the requirement that output lie within (-1,1), so B is correct. The notes describe Tanh as a rescaled Sigmoid but only assign the range (-1,1) to Tanh, not to Sigmoid, so A is wrong. ReLU and Softmax are not mentioned in the notes with a range of (-1,1), so C and D are wrong.

question: A data scientist observes that a network using the Sigmoid activation function suffers from gradient bias and weaker gradients during training. A colleague suggests switching to Tanh. According to the properties of Tanh, which statements correctly describe how Tanh compares to Sigmoid? (Choose TWO.)

options.text: Tanh produces a stronger (steeper) gradient than Sigmoid.
Tanh completely eliminates the vanishing gradient problem.
Tanh can assist in avoiding bias in gradients and can outperform Sigmoid.
Tanh cannot be used for multi-classification tasks.
Tanh outputs values in the range (0, 1).

explanation: The notes state clearly: Tanh's gradient is steeper than Sigmoid's (A correct), and Tanh helps avoid bias in gradients and can outperform Sigmoid (C correct). B is wrong because the notes say Tanh still has the vanishing gradient problem like Sigmoid, not that it's eliminated entirely. D is wrong because Tanh handles both binary and multi-classification. E is wrong because Tanh's range is (-1,1), not (0,1).

question: An engineer is documenting the capabilities of the Tanh activation function for a design review. Which of the following is a known limitation of Tanh that it shares with the Sigmoid function?

options.text: It can only handle binary classification and not multi-classification.
It prevents stacking multiple layers in a network.
It still has the vanishing gradient problem.
Its output range is limited to (0, 1).

explanation: The notes state clearly that Tanh 'Still has the vanishing gradient problem like Sigmoid', a limitation shared with Sigmoid, so C is correct. A is wrong because Tanh handles both binary and multi-classification. B is wrong because the notes say Tanh 'can now stack layers'. D is wrong because Tanh's range is (-1,1), not (0,1).

question: A data science team has a small number of labeled images and a very large collection of unlabeled images of the same type. They want to build a single model that leverages both the labeled and unlabeled data during training. Which learning problem does this scenario describe?
explanation: According to the notes, Semi-Supervised is the case where the training data has VERY FEW labeled examples and A LARGE NUMBER of unlabeled examples — matching the scenario. Supervised only learns a mapping from input to target (assuming fully labeled data), so it's wrong. Unsupervised only describes/extracts relationships in the data, without using any labels. Reinforcement is an agent learning through feedback in an environment, unrelated to labeled/unlabeled data.

question: A robotics company is building a system where a software agent operates within an environment and must learn the best actions to take based on feedback it receives. Which type of machine learning best fits this description?
explanation: The notes define Reinforcement as an agent operating in an environment that must learn how to behave through feedback — matching exactly. Unsupervised only describes/extracts relationships in the data. Supervised learns an input-target mapping. Self-supervised is framed as a supervised problem in order to apply supervised algorithms, not an agent-environment-feedback setup.

question: An organization has a model already trained on a large task and wants to use it as the starting point to build a solution for a different but related task, rather than training from scratch. Which learning technique does this represent?
explanation: According to the notes, Transfer is a model trained on one task that is used as the starting point for a related task — matching the description. Multi-Task is fitting ONE model on a dataset to solve multiple related problems at once, which differs from reusing a model for a new task. Ensemble combines predictions from two or more models on the same data. Online updates a model with new data before or after each observation.

question: A machine learning practitioner is reviewing definitions of learning techniques from their study notes. Which statements correctly match the technique to its definition? (Choose TWO.)

options.text: Active learning allows the model to query a human operator during learning to resolve ambiguity.
Ensemble learning fits a single model on one dataset that addresses multiple related problems.
Online learning uses available data and updates the model before a prediction is required or after the last observation.
Transfer learning combines the predictions of two or more models fit on the same data.
Multi-task learning uses a model trained on one task as the starting point for a related task.

explanation: According to the notes: Active — the model can query a human operator during learning to resolve ambiguity (A correct); Online — uses available data and updates the model before predictions are needed or after the last observation (C correct). B is wrong because that's the definition of Multi-Task, not Ensemble. D is wrong because combining predictions from multiple models is Ensemble, not Transfer. E is wrong because using a model from one task as the starting point for a related task is Transfer, not Multi-Task.

question: In a study of statistical inference approaches, an analyst applies general rules to determine specific outcomes. According to the categories of machine learning, which form of statistical inference is being used?
explanation: The notes define Deductive as using general rules to determine specific outcomes — matching the description. Inductive is the opposite: using evidence to determine outcomes. Transductive is used in statistical learning theory to predict specific examples for a specific domain. 'Semi-supervised inference' is not a type of statistical inference in the notes (Semi-Supervised belongs to the Hybrid Learning Problems group).

question: A machine learning team is training a deep neural network with several stacked layers that use the sigmoid activation function. As they add more layers, they notice that training slows dramatically and the network appears to stop learning in the earlier layers. Based on the behavior of the sigmoid function, what is the most likely cause?

options.text: The sigmoid function has a range of (0,1), which prevents any negative signals from propagating.
Near the ends of the curve, Y responds less to changes in X, causing vanishing gradients.
The sigmoid function cannot be used to stack multiple layers together.
The sigmoid function only supports binary classification, so multi-layer networks fail.

explanation: According to the notes, near the ends of the sigmoid curve, Y responds very little to changes in X, causing 'vanishing gradients' which stalls the network or makes it learn very slowly — matching the described phenomenon. A is wrong because the range (0,1) is not the cause of stalled learning. C is wrong because the notes say sigmoid CAN stack layers. D is wrong because the notes say sigmoid handles both binary AND multi-classification.

question: A data scientist is describing the shape and output range of the sigmoid activation function to a colleague. Which statement correctly characterizes the sigmoid function?

options.text: It is a linear function with an output range of (-1,1).
It is a step function that outputs only 0 or 1.
It is a logistic curve resembling an S-shape with an output range of (0,1).
It is an exponential curve with an unbounded output range.

explanation: The notes state clearly that sigmoid is a 'Logistic curve' shaped like an S with a range of (0,1). C matches exactly. A is wrong because sigmoid is not linear and its range is (0,1), not (-1,1). B is wrong because sigmoid is a continuous (analog) curve, not a step function outputting only 0 or 1. D is wrong because the output is bounded within (0,1), not unbounded.

question: An engineer is evaluating the computational cost of using the sigmoid activation function in a large neural network. According to the characteristics of sigmoid, why does its activation tend to be dense, slower, and more costly?

options.text: Sigmoid is analog, so almost all neurons fire (become active).
Sigmoid outputs are restricted to the range (0,1), which requires extra normalization.
Sigmoid can only be applied to a single layer at a time.
Sigmoid pushes all activations to exactly zero, requiring sparse recomputation.

explanation: The notes state: sigmoid is analog, so almost all neurons fire (are active), making activation dense, slower, and more costly. A is correct. B is wrong because the range (0,1) is not the reason for the costly dense activation. C is wrong because sigmoid can stack multiple layers. D is wrong because sigmoid doesn't push everything to exactly 0; on the contrary, it causes most neurons to be active.

question: A team is reviewing why the sigmoid function is considered one of the most widely used activation functions. Which of the following are accurate characteristics of the sigmoid function according to its documented properties? (Choose TWO.)

options.text: It can handle both binary and multi-classification tasks.
It tends to bring activations to either side of the curve, producing clear distinctions on predictions.
It produces sparse activations because very few neurons fire.
It has an output range of (-1,1) centered at zero.
It cannot be used to stack layers in a neural network.

explanation: The notes state sigmoid handles both binary and multi-classification (A correct) and tends to push activations toward the two ends of the curve, creating clear distinction in predictions (B correct). C is wrong because sigmoid is analog, causing almost every neuron to fire → DENSE activation, not sparse. D is wrong because the range is (0,1). E is wrong because the notes say sigmoid CAN stack layers.

question: A machine learning team is comparing activation functions for a deep neural network. They note that Sigmoid and Tanh tend to fire almost all neurons, producing dense and computationally costly activations. They want an activation function that instead triggers activations sparsely to improve efficiency and speed. Which activation function meets this requirement?

options.text: Sigmoid, because it bounds outputs between 0 and 1
Tanh, because it centers outputs around zero
ReLU, because its negative-axis gradient is zero, so it sparsely triggers activations
Softmax, because it normalizes outputs into a probability distribution

explanation: According to the notes, ReLU sparsely triggers activations because the gradient on the negative axis is 0, making it less costly and more efficient (faster). In contrast, Sigmoid and Tanh activate almost all neurons, making them dense, slow, and costly, so A and B are wrong. Softmax isn't mentioned in the notes, so D isn't valid.

question: During a review of a neural network's activation functions, an engineer is asked to describe the output range of the ReLU (Rectified Linear Unit) function. According to its definition, what is the range of ReLU?

options.text: [0, ∞), because the positive axis is unbound (infinite) and the negative axis returns 0
(-∞, ∞), because both axes are linear and unbounded
[0, 1], because outputs are squashed into a probability
[-1, 1], because outputs are centered around zero

explanation: The notes state clearly that ReLU has range [0, ∞): the positive axis is linear and unbounded (infinite), while the negative axis always returns 0, so A is correct. B is wrong because the negative axis is not linear — it's 0. C ([0,1]) describes Sigmoid and D ([-1,1]) describes Tanh, not ReLU.

question: A data scientist observes that in a trained network using ReLU, some nodes stop contributing to learning: their gradients trend toward 0 and get stuck at 0 with nothing left to adjust, so the nodes essentially "die." What is this side effect called and why does it occur?

options.text: Vanishing softmax, caused by normalizing outputs into probabilities
The ReLU dying gradient, caused by the zero gradient on the negative axis
Dense activation, caused by firing almost all neurons at once
Unbounded range, caused by the positive axis being infinite

explanation: The notes call this phenomenon the ReLU dying gradient: because the gradient on the negative axis is 0, gradients trend toward 0 and get stuck at 0, causing the node to 'die'. So B is correct. A is not in the notes. C (dense activation) is a feature of Sigmoid/Tanh, not the cause of a dying node. D (unbounded range) is a feature of the positive axis and doesn't cause this phenomenon.

question: An ML engineer is documenting the characteristics of the ReLU activation function to justify choosing it over Sigmoid and Tanh. Which TWO statements accurately describe ReLU according to its definition? (Choose TWO.)

options.text: On the positive axis it is linear, while the negative axis always returns 0
It fires almost all neurons, producing dense activations
It sparsely triggers activations, making it less costly and more efficient
Its output range is [-1, 1], centered around zero
Its negative axis is linear and unbounded

explanation: According to the notes: (A) ReLU is linear on the positive axis and always returns 0 on the negative axis — correct; (C) ReLU activates sparsely, making it less costly and more efficient — correct. B is wrong because firing almost all neurons (dense) is a feature of Sigmoid/Tanh, not ReLU. D is wrong because ReLU's range is [0, ∞), not [-1, 1]. E is wrong because ReLU's negative axis is 0 (gradient 0), not linear or infinite.

question: A data science team is training a deep neural network with standard ReLU activations. They observe that a growing number of neurons stop activating entirely and no longer contribute to learning, a problem often called the "dying ReLU" issue. Which activation function is specifically designed to reduce this effect by allowing a small, non-zero gradient on the negative axis?
explanation: Theo notes, Leaky ReLU cho phep truc am co gradient nhe gan bang 0, lam giam hieu ung 'dying gradient' cua ReLU vi truc am bi 'ro ri' nen mot so node khong bi chet. B (ReLU6) chi gioi han tren truc duong, khong xu ly van de node chet. C la ReLU tieu chuan - chinh la nguyen nhan gay ra van de. D khong duoc de cap nhu giai phap cho van de nay.

question: An ML engineer wants to use a Leaky ReLU variant where the slope of the negative axis is not fixed but is instead learned during training. Which activation function meets this requirement?

options.text: Leaky ReLU with a fixed 0.01x slope

explanation: Theo notes, Parameterised ReLU la Leaky ReLU trong do do doc truc am la mot tham so co the hoc duoc (learnable parameter), khong co dinh o muc 0.01x. A (ReLU6) gioi han truc duong chu khong lien quan do doc am hoc duoc. B (ReLU tieu chuan) dat truc am bang 0. D mo ta Leaky ReLU voi do doc co dinh, dung la cai ma PReLU thay the bang tham so hoc duoc.

question: A mobile ML team needs an activation function whose positive axis is capped at a maximum value rather than being unbounded/infinite. Based on activation function definitions, which variant provides this bounded positive output?
explanation: Theo notes, ReLU6 la ReLU ma truc duong co gioi han tren nen khong vo han (bi chan o mot gia tri toi da). A (Leaky ReLU) chi them gradient nhe o truc am, truc duong van tuyen tinh vo han. C (PReLU) chi lam do doc am hoc duoc. D (ReLU tieu chuan) co truc duong tuyen tinh khong gioi han.

question: During a design review, a colleague asks how Leaky ReLU differs from standard ReLU in terms of its shape. Which statement correctly describes the Leaky ReLU function according to its definition?

options.text: Both the positive and negative axes are capped at a fixed maximum value.
The positive axis is set to zero and the negative axis is linear.
The positive axis is linear and the negative axis has a gentle gradient closer to zero.
The negative slope must always be learned during training.

explanation: Theo notes, Leaky ReLU co truc duong tuyen tinh va truc am co gradient nhe gan bang 0. A mo ta sai (gioi han tren la dac diem cua ReLU6, va khong ap cho truc am). B dao nguoc hanh vi - ReLU tieu chuan dat truc am bang 0, khong phai truc duong. D mo ta Parameterised ReLU (do doc am hoc duoc), khong phai Leaky ReLU noi chung von thuong dung do doc co dinh nhu 0.01x.

question: An ML team is designing a deep neural network and wants an activation function that provides the benefits of a ReLU neuron while avoiding the dying ReLU problem. Which activation function is described as a generalization of both ReLU and Leaky ReLU that meets this need?
explanation: According to the notes, Maxout is a generalization of ReLU and Leaky ReLU, and a Maxout neuron has all the benefits of a ReLU neuron without dying ReLU. So B is correct. Sigmoid (A), Tanh (C), and Softmax (D) are not described in the notes as generalizations of ReLU/Leaky ReLU, nor are they mentioned as a solution to dying ReLU.

question: A data scientist is evaluating the Maxout activation function for a large network but is concerned about resource cost. According to how Maxout works, what is the main downside they should consider?

options.text: It causes the dying ReLU problem for every neuron
It can only be used in the output layer of a network
It is expensive because it doubles the number of parameters for each neuron
It always returns the minimum of its inputs, reducing accuracy

explanation: The notes state clearly that the downside of Maxout is that it's expensive because it doubles the number of parameters for each neuron, so C is correct. A is wrong because Maxout avoids dying ReLU (that's an advantage). B is not mentioned in the notes. D is wrong because Maxout selects the maximum value, not the minimum.

question: An engineer needs to explain the core computation that a Maxout neuron performs. Based on its definition, what does a Maxout neuron do with its multiple inputs?

options.text: It sums all the inputs and applies a threshold
It looks at multiple inputs and returns the maximum value
It averages the inputs and normalizes them to a probability
It multiplies the inputs together and returns the product

explanation: According to the notes, Maxout looks at multiple inputs and selects the maximum value, returning that value; so B is correct. A (summing then thresholding), C (averaging and normalizing into a probability), and D (multiplying the inputs) are all behaviors not described for Maxout in the notes.

question: A machine learning team is training a deep neural network and observes that many neurons stop activating entirely and produce only zero outputs, halting their contribution to learning. A colleague recommends switching to the Exponential Linear Unit (ELU) activation function. What is the primary benefit ELU provides in this situation?

options.text: It avoids the dying ReLU problem by allowing negative values instead of clamping negatives to zero.
It guarantees that all activations remain strictly positive across the network.
It removes the need for any gradient computation in the positive axis.
It increases the output magnitude without bound for large negative inputs.

explanation: Theo notes, ELU 'Avoids the dying ReLU problem' vi no co do doc huong ve -1 o truc am, cho phep gia tri am thay vi ep ve 0 nhu ReLU. B sai vi ELU tao ra gia tri am chu khong phai luon duong. C sai vi ELU van co gradient tuyen tinh o truc duong. D sai vi ELU thuc ra bao hoa (saturates) voi so am lon, khong tang vo han.

question: During a design review, an engineer explains why the Exponential Linear Unit (ELU) tends to speed up learning and convergence compared to some other activation functions. Which explanation is consistent with how ELU behaves?

options.text: ELU saturates for large positive numbers, which stabilizes the gradient.
ELU's slope toward -1 produces negative values that push the mean of the activations closer to zero.
ELU forces every activation to exactly -1, standardizing the output range.
ELU eliminates the linear gradient in the positive axis to reduce computation.

explanation: Notes neu ro: ELU doc ve -1 tao ra gia tri am, day trung binh (mean) cua cac activation ve gan 0, dan den hoc va hoi tu nhanh hon. A sai vi notes noi ELU bao hoa voi so am lon, khong phai so duong. C sai vi ELU chi doc ve -1 (bao hoa huong toi), khong ep moi gia tri bang -1. D sai vi ELU giu gradient tuyen tinh o truc duong.

question: A practitioner is comparing activation functions and describes the Exponential Linear Unit (ELU). Which two statements correctly characterize ELU's behavior? (Choose TWO.)

options.text: ELU behaves like something between a ReLU and a Leaky ReLU.
ELU saturates for large negative numbers.
ELU has no gradient in the positive axis.
ELU pushes the mean of the activations away from zero to accelerate learning.
ELU introduces the dying ReLU problem more severely than ReLU.
