options.text: Traditional AI focuses on understanding and decision-making, while Generative AI focuses on creating new, original outputs.
Traditional AI can only process images, while Generative AI can only process text.
Traditional AI generates new, unseen data, while Generative AI only analyzes existing data.
Traditional AI is a subset of Generative AI that specializes in robotics.

explanation: Theo notes, ve Functionality: AI tap trung vao hieu va ra quyet dinh (understanding & decision-making), con GenAI tap trung tao ra output moi, nguyen ban. Vi vay A dung. B sai vi notes khong gioi han AI chi xu ly anh hay GenAI chi xu ly text (GenAI co nhieu modality: vision, text, audio, molecular). C dao nguoc vai tro: chinh GenAI moi tao du lieu moi chua thay. D sai vi GenAI la subset cua AI, khong phai nguoc lai.

question: A data science team needs a generative technique in which two neural networks compete during training: one network creates synthetic samples while the other tries to detect whether a sample is fake. Which technique matches this description?
explanation: Notes mo ta GANs gom hai mang canh tranh: generator tao ban gia va discriminator co phat hien, huan luyen doi khang (adversarial) tao anh/deepfake rat chan thuc — dung voi C. A (VAEs) ma hoa du lieu vao khong gian latent xac suat roi giai ma/lay mau, khong phai co che hai mang canh tranh. B (Transformers) dung self-attention xu ly chuoi song song, la kien truc cua LLM. D (expert systems) la ung dung AI truyen thong, khong phai ky thuat GenAI.

question: An engineer is explaining the mechanism behind modern large language models such as GPT. According to the notes, which architectural feature allows these models to process an entire sequence in parallel?

options.text: Adversarial training between a generator and a discriminator

explanation: Notes neu Transformers dung self-attention de xu ly toan bo chuoi song song va la kien truc dang sau cac LLM hien dai (GPT) — nen B dung. A (probabilistic latent space) la dac trung cua VAEs. C (adversarial training generator/discriminator) la cua GANs. D (speech recognition) la mot ung dung cua AI truyen thong, khong phai co che cua LLM.

question: A stakeholder asks how Generative AI relates to Artificial Intelligence and Large Language Models. Based on the notes, which statement is correct?

options.text: Generative AI is a superset that contains all of Artificial Intelligence.
Large Language Models and Generative AI are completely unrelated fields.
Generative AI is a subset of AI, and Large Language Models are a subset of Generative AI.
Artificial Intelligence is a subset of Large Language Models.

explanation: Notes khang dinh GenAI la mot subset cua AI, va LLM la mot subset cua GenAI (du thuong bi nham voi AI do pho bien). Do do C dung. A sai vi GenAI la subset chu khong phai superset cua AI. B sai vi LLM chinh la mot phan cua GenAI. D sai hoan toan ve thu bac.

question: A team is documenting the distinction between 'simulate' and 'emulate' as used in the context of AI. Which description aligns with the notes?

options.text: Simulate means to replicate exact processes and mechanisms; emulate means to mimic aspects or resemble behaviour.
Simulate means to mimic aspects or resemble behaviour; emulate means to replicate exact processes and mechanisms.
Simulate and emulate both mean generating entirely new content.
Simulate refers to GANs and emulate refers to VAEs.

explanation: Notes dinh nghia: Simulate = mimic aspects / resemble behaviour (mo phong, giong hanh vi); Emulate = replicate exact processes and mechanisms (tai tao chinh xac quy trinh va co che). B khop dung. A dao nguoc hai dinh nghia. C sai vi ca hai khong mang nghia tao noi dung moi. D gan sai hai thuat ngu nay cho ky thuat GenAI.

question: A product manager is sorting use cases into either traditional AI or Generative AI categories based on the notes. Which TWO use cases are described as Generative AI applications?
explanation: Theo phan Applications, GenAI mang tinh sang tao/doi moi: content creation, synthetic data generation, deepfakes, design — nen B (synthetic data generation) va D (deepfake) dung. A (recommendation systems), C (autonomous vehicles) va E (medical diagnosis) deu duoc liet ke la ung dung/nganh cua AI truyen thong, khong phai GenAI.

question: A machine learning team is preparing raw text to be processed by a large language model (LLM). Before the model can accept the input, the text must be broken into smaller parts, and each part must correspond to an entry in the model's internal vocabulary. What is this preprocessing step called?
explanation: The correct answer is A. According to the notes, tokenization is the process of splitting the input data (text) into smaller pieces (tokens), and input text must be tokenized into a sequence of tokens matching the model's internal vocabulary. B (vector embedding) isn't mentioned in the notes for this step. C (fine-tuning) is retraining the model, not splitting text. D (prompt engineering) is designing prompts, unrelated to splitting text into tokens.

question: A developer notices that after tokenizing a sentence, the word "Hello" is represented internally by the value 15496. Which statement best explains what this value represents?

options.text: The number of characters the model can process in a single request
A unique ID that maps the token to an entry in the model's vocabulary
The embedding dimension used to represent the token as a vector
The number of times the token appeared in the training data

explanation: The correct answer is B. The notes state clearly that each token is mapped to a unique ID within the model's vocabulary (e.g. "Hello" → 15496). A is wrong because this value isn't a character-count limit. C is wrong because this is an ID within the vocabulary, not the embedding dimension (the notes don't mention embedding dimension). D is wrong because this number is an identifying ID, not a frequency count in the training data.

question: A team is documenting the tokenization algorithm used by GPT-3. The algorithm iteratively merges the most frequent character or byte pairs into subword tokens and handles rare or unknown words by splitting them into pieces. Which tokenization algorithm is being described?
explanation: The correct answer is C. The notes state that BPE is used by GPT-3, iteratively merges the most frequent pairs of characters/bytes into subword tokens, and handles rare/unknown words by splitting them into multiple pieces. A (WordPiece) is used by BERT and chooses merges to maximize the likelihood of the training data. B (SentencePiece) is used by T5 and Llama, treating text as a raw stream. D (whole-word) is not subword-based and suffers from large vocabulary and unknown-word issues.

question: A multilingual application must tokenize text in several languages without relying on any whitespace-based pre-tokenization step. The engineering team wants an algorithm that treats the input as a raw stream (including spaces) so it is language-agnostic. Which tokenization algorithm meets this requirement, and which models are associated with it?

options.text: SentencePiece, used by Google T5 and Llama
Byte Pair Encoding (BPE), used by GPT-3
SentencePiece, used by GPT-3 and BERT

explanation: The correct answer is A. The notes describe SentencePiece as treating text as a raw stream (including whitespace), making it language-agnostic and not requiring pre-tokenization/whitespace splitting; it is used by Google T5 and Llama. B (BPE/GPT-3) and C (WordPiece/BERT) are other algorithms that don't have the characteristic of treating text as a raw stream without whitespace splitting. D is wrong because it mistakenly assigns SentencePiece to GPT-3 and BERT (those are BPE and WordPiece).

question: During a design review, an architect explains that subword tokenizers such as BPE, WordPiece, and SentencePiece are preferred as a middle ground over the two extremes. Which TWO problems do these extremes cause that subword tokenization is designed to balance? (Choose TWO.)

options.text: Whole-word tokenization leads to a huge vocabulary and an unknown-word problem
Per-character tokenization produces a tiny vocabulary but very long sequences
Whole-word tokenization always produces a vocabulary of exactly 30,000 tokens
Per-character tokenization eliminates the need for a model vocabulary entirely
Subword tokenization requires manual whitespace splitting for every language

explanation: The correct answers are A and B. The notes state that subword tokenizers are a middle ground between whole-word (large vocabulary, unknown-word problem) and per-character (small vocabulary, long sequences). A and B accurately describe these two extremes. C is wrong because 30,000–100,000 is a typical internal vocabulary size when an LLM is trained, not a fixed value for whole-word tokenization. D is wrong because per-character still needs a vocabulary (a small one), not none at all. E is wrong because it's exactly SentencePiece (a subword tokenizer) that is language-agnostic and doesn't need whitespace splitting.

question: A machine learning team wants to represent customer support tickets so that a model can identify which tickets are semantically related to one another. Which representation is specifically designed to let ML models find relationships between data?

options.text: Embeddings, which are vectors of data used by ML models to find relationships between data
A relational database schema that stores each ticket as a normalized row
A plain-text log file containing the raw ticket contents
A single scalar sentiment score assigned to each ticket

explanation: According to the notes, embeddings are data vectors used by an ML model to find relationships between data, matching the need to find semantically related tickets. B (relational schema), C (raw text log file), and D (a single scalar score) are not vector representations and aren't designed for a model to find relationships between data per the notes.

question: A data scientist describes an approach that represents text documents as vectors positioned within a high-dimensional space. Which term matches this description?
explanation: The notes define the Vector Space Model as a way of representing text documents (or other data) as vectors in a multi-dimensional space — matching the description in the question. B (multi-model pattern) is sharing embeddings across models; C (embedding algorithm) is the algorithm that generates embeddings; D (external memory) is a metaphor for the role of embeddings, not the concept of representing documents as vectors in a multi-dimensional space.

question: An engineer evaluates two different embedding algorithms on the same dataset and observes that each surfaces different types of relationships among the data points. According to embedding fundamentals, what best explains this outcome?

options.text: Embeddings can only be produced by non-ML statistical tools, so results vary randomly
Different embedding algorithms capture different kinds of relationships
An embedding is a scalar length only, with no direction, so results are identical
Embeddings cannot be shared or reused across any models

explanation: The notes state clearly that different embedding algorithms capture different kinds of relationships, explaining why two algorithms produce different relationships on the same data. A is wrong because the notes say an ML model can also generate embeddings. C is wrong because a vector has both length and direction (an arrow with a length and a direction). D is wrong because the notes state that embeddings can be shared across models.

question: A solutions architect is coordinating a task across several ML models and wants to leverage embeddings to help. Which two statements about embeddings are accurate according to the notes? (Choose TWO.)

options.text: Embeddings can act as external memory for an ML model's task
Embeddings can be shared across models to coordinate a task between models
Embeddings can only be created manually and never by an ML model
An embedding is a single number representing a document's length
Embeddings prevent any model from finding relationships between data

explanation: The notes describe embeddings as external memory for a model's task (A correct) and state they can be shared across models via the multi-model pattern to coordinate a task (B correct). C is wrong because the notes say an ML model can also generate embeddings. D is wrong because an embedding is a vector (with length and direction), not a single number. E is wrong because the purpose of embeddings is exactly to help a model find relationships between data.

question: A developer opens the Amazon Bedrock console and selects a foundation model they want to use in their application. When they attempt to invoke it, the call fails. According to the Bedrock model catalog workflow, what step must be completed before a model can be used?

options.text: Fine-tune the model on a custom dataset
Request model access for the model
Deploy the model to a dedicated Amazon EC2 instance
Provision a SageMaker endpoint for the model

explanation: The notes state clearly: 'Before using a model you must request model access.' So the mandatory step before using a model is requesting model access (B). Fine-tuning (A) is not a mandatory requirement to call a model. Bedrock is LLMaaS, so there's no need to deploy to EC2 yourself (C) or create a SageMaker endpoint yourself (D) — these aren't concepts in the notes.

question: An architect describes Amazon Bedrock's Model Catalog to a new team member. Which statement best characterizes what the Model Catalog provides?

options.text: A managed collection of LLM models ready to deploy through an LLM-as-a-Service offering
A repository of training datasets used to build foundation models
A set of pre-built Lambda functions for orchestrating model calls
A dashboard for monitoring the cost of running self-hosted models

explanation: The notes define the Model Catalog as 'a collection of LLM models ready to deploy with an LLMaaS' — matching A. B (training data repository), C (Lambda functions), and D (a cost-monitoring dashboard) are all concepts not appearing in the notes and mischaracterize the Model Catalog.

question: A team is reviewing the Amazon Bedrock model catalog table to compare foundation models before selecting one. Which attributes are shown as columns in the catalog to help them make this comparison? (Choose TWO.)

options.text: Modality (for example, Text or Text & vision)

explanation: The notes list the catalog's columns: Model name, Version, Provider, Modality (Text / Text & vision), Max tokens. So A (Modality) and B (Max tokens) are correct. C (hourly inference price), D (latency), E (number of regions) are not among the columns listed in the notes.

question: A company wants to use foundation models from multiple providers within Amazon Bedrock. A solutions architect needs to confirm which model providers are available on AWS. Which provider-and-model pairing is consistent with the Bedrock model catalog?
explanation: The notes list providers and example models, including 'Anthropic — e.g. Claude Sonnet', so A is correct. Google (Gemini) and OpenAI (GPT-4) aren't in the notes' provider list, ruling out B and C. SDXL 1.0 belongs to Stability AI, not Amazon (Amazon has Titan Premier), so D is mismatched.

question: A machine learning team is building a transformer-based model for a sentence-classification task. They want the model to learn how each word in an input sentence relates to every other word within that same sentence. Which mechanism is designed for this purpose?
explanation: According to the notes, Self-Attention computes attention weights WITHIN the same input sequence, with each element attending to all other elements of that same sequence (e.g. words in a sentence). This exactly matches the requirement. Cross-attention (A) is wrong because it computes attention between TWO different sequences. RNN (C) is not an attention mechanism and processes sequentially. Convolution (D) isn't mentioned and doesn't model word relationships in an attention-like way.

question: A team is developing a neural machine translation system where the decoder must focus on the relevant parts of the encoded source sentence while generating each output word. Which attention mechanism connects these two different sequences?

options.text: Self-attention, because each element attends to all other elements in the same sequence
Multi-head attention, because it processes the sequence in parallel
Cross-attention, because it lets the output sequence attend to the input sequence
Cross-attention, because it feeds a sequence back into itself

explanation: According to the notes, Cross-Attention computes attention weights between TWO different sequences, allowing one sequence to attend to the other; it's used in tasks like machine translation, where the output sequence (decoder) needs to focus on the input sequence (encoder). Answer C correctly describes the reason. A is wrong because self-attention operates within a single sequence, not two different sources. B is wrong because parallel computation isn't the reason for connecting two sequences. D is wrong because 'feeds back into itself' is a feature of self-attention, not cross-attention.

question: In a transformer architecture, an engineer wants the model to capture several different types of dependencies in the input at the same time, with multiple attention computations running in parallel, each focusing on a different aspect of the input. Which mechanism provides this capability?
explanation: According to the notes, Multi-head Attention combines multiple self-attention (or cross-attention) heads in parallel, with each head focusing on a different aspect of the input, improving performance and capturing multiple types of dependencies simultaneously. That's exactly what the question describes. A is wrong because a single head can't capture multiple aspects in parallel. B is wrong because cross-attention is about the relationship between two sequences, not multiple parallel heads. D is wrong because a recurrent layer processes sequentially, the opposite of the parallel idea.

question: A solutions architect is explaining to stakeholders why transformer-based models scale so well compared to older recurrent neural networks (RNNs). According to the role of the attention mechanism, which statements are correct? (Choose TWO.)

options.text: Attention lets transformers weigh long-range relationships between words in a sequence
Attention allows a sequence to be processed in parallel, unlike RNNs which are sequential
Attention removes the need to assign any weights to tokens in a sequence
Attention forces the model to process tokens strictly one after another
Attention converts text into images before processing

explanation: According to the Exam note: attention is the key innovation that lets a transformer weigh long-range word relationships and process sequences IN PARALLEL (unlike RNNs which are sequential) — that's why transformers scale well. So A and B are correct. C is wrong because attention is exactly what assigns weights to tokens. D is wrong because that describes sequential RNN processing, the opposite of attention. E is wrong and not found in the notes.

question: A data scientist reviews a description that reads: 'each element attends to all other elements, and the mechanism feeds back its own sequence.' Which type of attention is being described?

options.text: Cross-attention, which feeds sequence inputs from two different sources
Self-attention, which computes attention weights within the same input sequence
Multi-head attention, which receives multiple separate inputs
Cross-attention, used to model words within a single sentence

explanation: According to the notes, Self-Attention computes attention weights WITHIN the same input sequence, with each element attending to all other elements, and 'feeds back its own sequence' — matching the description. A and D are wrong because cross-attention takes input from TWO different sources, not within one sequence. C is wrong because multi-head attention is about multiple parallel heads, not the description of 'feeds back its own sequence'.

question: A machine learning team has fine-tuned an Amazon Titan model in Amazon Bedrock and now needs to serve it for inference in production. Which deployment model must they use to make this custom model available?

options.text: On-Demand (Serverless), paying per input and output token
Provisioned Throughput, purchasing Model Units with a commitment term
Batch Inference for offline bulk processing
Cross Region Inference to distribute the requests

explanation: According to the notes, custom/fine-tuned models are only available via Provisioned Throughput, which requires purchasing Model Units with a commitment term. A is wrong because On-Demand only serves base models like Titan/Cohere Command, not fine-tuned models. C is wrong because Batch Inference is bulk offline processing, not a mechanism for serving custom real-time models. D is wrong because Cross Region Inference only routes requests across multiple regions during high load and is unrelated to serving fine-tuned models.

question: A startup is testing several base models in Amazon Bedrock. Their traffic is low and highly variable, and they want to avoid any upfront capacity commitment. Which pricing behavior describes the On-Demand deployment model they should choose?

options.text: You pay based on the number of Model Units reserved for a fixed commitment term
You pay a flat monthly fee regardless of usage
You pay based on the number of input and output tokens, with no commitment
You pay only for offline batch jobs submitted during off-peak hours

explanation: The notes state clearly that On-Demand charges based on input and output tokens, has no commitment, and suits variable/low traffic and testing. A describes Provisioned Throughput (Model Units + commitment term), not On-Demand. B is wrong because there's no concept of a flat monthly fee in the notes. D describes Batch Inference (offline bulk processing), not On-Demand.

question: An enterprise application runs a steady, high-volume production workload against a base model in Amazon Bedrock and needs a dedicated, predictable level of throughput. Which deployment model best fits this requirement?
explanation: The notes indicate Provisioned Throughput provides dedicated throughput via Model Units, suited for large-scale, steady production (steady high-volume). A (On-Demand) suits variable/low traffic and testing rather than steady high-volume production. B (Batch Inference) is offline, non-real-time processing. D (Cross Region Inference) only routes requests across multiple regions during peak load, not a model for providing dedicated throughput.

question: A solutions architect wants to monitor token usage for an On-Demand model deployed in Amazon Bedrock. According to the notes, which AWS service provides logging and metrics for tracking this usage?
explanation: The notes state that token usage can be tracked in the Bedrock Playground, via code callbacks, or through Amazon CloudWatch logging/metrics. So A is correct. B, C, D (CloudTrail, CloudFront, Config) are not mentioned in the notes as tools for tracking token usage for On-Demand.

question: A team is evaluating Amazon Bedrock inference options and reviewing statements about Cross Region Inference and Batch Inference. According to the notes, which TWO statements are correct? (Choose TWO.)

options.text: Cross Region Inference routes requests to multiple AWS regions during peak utilization bursts
Cross Region Inference is limited to models from a single provider only
Batch Inference performs non-real-time (offline) bulk processing
Batch Inference is required to serve custom/fine-tuned models in real time
Cross Region Inference is used to pay per Model Unit with a commitment term

explanation: The notes state that Cross Region Inference routes requests to multiple AWS regions during periods of peak load (A correct) and is available across multiple providers (Anthropic, Meta, Amazon, Mistral, etc.), not a single provider, so B is wrong. Batch Inference is bulk offline/non-real-time processing, so C is correct; D is wrong because serving custom/fine-tuned models is the role of Provisioned Throughput, not Batch Inference. E is wrong because Model Units + commitment term belongs to Provisioned Throughput and is unrelated to Cross Region Inference.

question: A developer wants to quickly experiment with a foundation model's API and evaluate its outputs before building an application, but they do not want to write any code. Which Amazon Bedrock feature is designed for this purpose?
explanation: According to the notes, the Bedrock Playground allows interacting with the API of deployed models without writing code, serving rapid LLM development and evaluation. Guardrails is a safety-control mechanism (just a configuration option within the playground); Knowledge Bases and Provisioned Throughput are not mentioned as no-code interaction tools in this file.

question: A team lead is deciding how to use the Amazon Bedrock Playground within their organization. Which statement correctly describes the intended use of playgrounds?

options.text: They are meant for production workloads and daily end-user traffic.
They are meant for development and quick evaluation of LLMs, not for daily use or production.
They are meant only for fine-tuning foundation models on custom data.
They are meant to host models behind a public API endpoint for customers.

explanation: The notes state clearly that playgrounds are for development/rapid LLM evaluation, NOT for daily use or production. So A and D are wrong (not for production/end-users). Fine-tuning isn't mentioned as a purpose of the playground, so C is wrong.

question: An engineer is testing prompts in the Amazon Bedrock Chat playground and wants to review the resource consumption and performance of each request. Which metrics does the playground expose in its token usage view? (Choose TWO.)

options.text: Number of concurrent users on the endpoint
GPU memory utilization of the underlying host
Data transfer bandwidth to Amazon S3

explanation: The notes list token usage metrics including: Overall summary, Latency (e.g. 885 ms), Input token count, Output token count, Cost. So Latency (A) and input/output token counts (B) are both correct. Concurrent user count, GPU memory utilization, and S3 bandwidth are not mentioned, so C, D, E are wrong.

question: A data scientist needs a single-turn response to classify and categorize short pieces of text, using a model that simply predicts the next text rather than holding a conversation. Which Amazon Bedrock playground is most appropriate?
explanation: The notes describe the Text (Completion) playground as used for predicting next text, useful for single-turn responses such as Classification and Categorization. The Chat playground is for chat-completion models (multi-turn conversation), the Image playground is for generating images, and Bedrock Studio is just a Preview item in the Console Overview, not one of the 3 playgrounds. So C is correct.

question: While configuring a request in an Amazon Bedrock playground, a user is adjusting the settings exposed by the interface. Which of the following is a configuration option available in the playground?
explanation: According to the notes, the configuration options exposed in the playground include Temperature, Top P, Response length, Stop sequences, and Guardrail. Temperature (B) is on this list. Number of training epochs, VPC subnet selection, and IAM role ARN are not listed as playground configuration options, so A, C, D are wrong.

question: A company wants quick, fully managed access to foundation models from multiple providers through a single API, without provisioning or running any infrastructure. Which AWS service best meets this requirement?

options.text: Amazon SageMaker, to build, train, and host custom ML models end-to-end
Amazon Bedrock, a serverless service providing access to foundation models via one API
Amazon Bedrock Studio, so developers can sign in with company SSO
Amazon OpenSearch, to store and retrieve vector embeddings

explanation: According to the notes, Amazon Bedrock is a fully managed, serverless service that provides access to foundation models from multiple providers via a single API, without running any infrastructure. A is wrong because SageMaker is for building/training/hosting models yourself end-to-end, not for quickly accessing a third party's FM. C is wrong because Bedrock Studio is just a feature for developers to log in via SSO to build apps, not the nature of the described service. D is wrong because OpenSearch is only related to Knowledge Base/RAG and doesn't provide FM access.

question: An organization needs to improve a foundation model's fluency in a highly specialized medical domain. They have a very large corpus of unlabeled medical documents but no labeled task examples. Which Amazon Bedrock customization approach is most appropriate?

options.text: Fine-tuning, using the smaller labeled task dataset
Guardrails, to filter denied topics on input and output
Continued Pre-Training, using the large unlabeled domain text
Prompt Management, to store domain-specific prompt templates

explanation: The notes state clearly that Continued Pre-Training continues pretraining a base model on a large amount of UNLABELED domain text (self-supervised) to build domain proficiency. A is wrong because fine-tuning uses a smaller, labeled task-specific dataset, which doesn't fit when only large unlabeled data is available. B is wrong because Guardrails is a safety layer that filters content, not used to increase domain proficiency. D is wrong because Prompt Management only stores prompt templates and doesn't change the model's knowledge.

question: A development team wants to build a multi-step generative AI pipeline that chains prompts, Knowledge Bases, agents, and Lambda functions together using a visual drag-and-drop builder, without writing code. Which Amazon Bedrock feature should they use?
explanation: According to the notes, Prompt Flows is a visual drag-and-drop builder chaining prompts, KBs, agents, and Lambda into a workflow for multi-step GenAI pipelines without code. A is wrong because Prompt Management only stores prompt templates. C is wrong because the Model Catalog is where you browse/select models. D is wrong because Playgrounds (Chat, Completion, Image) are only for interactively testing models, not for workflow orchestration.

question: A financial services company deploying a customer-facing chatbot on Amazon Bedrock must block certain denied topics, filter harmful content, redact personally identifiable information (PII), and reduce hallucinations. On which points is the Bedrock Guardrails safety layer applied? (Choose TWO.)

options.text: Only on images produced by Titan Image Generator

explanation: The notes state Guardrails is a safety layer applied to BOTH input and output (pre and post filters) to block denied topics, filter harmful content, redact PII, and reduce hallucination. So A and B are correct. C and D are wrong because Guardrails isn't restricted by inference type (Batch/Provisioned/Serverless/Cross-Region). E is wrong because detecting images from Titan Image Generator is the function of Watermark detection, not Guardrails.

question: A media platform needs to determine whether a given image was generated by Amazon Titan Image Generator in order to support content authenticity. Which Amazon Bedrock capability provides this?
explanation: The notes state that Watermark detection detects whether an image was created by Amazon Titan Image Generator (based on an invisible watermark), supporting content authenticity. A is wrong because Guardrails filters content for safety. B is wrong because Model Evaluation is used to compare/score models. D is wrong because Knowledge Base serves RAG with OpenSearch, unrelated to watermark detection.

question: An ML team must select the most suitable foundation model for a new use case by comparing and scoring candidate models on accuracy, robustness, and toxicity, using either automatic metrics or human evaluation. Which Amazon Bedrock feature supports this?
explanation: According to the notes, Model Evaluation allows you to compare/score models on accuracy, robustness, toxicity, etc. using automatic metrics or human evaluation to choose the right FM. B is wrong because Agents is an agentic workflow that can plan and call tools/APIs, not used for evaluating and choosing a model. C is wrong because Bedrock Studio is an environment for developers to log in via SSO to build apps. D is wrong because Continued Pre-Training is a model-customization method, not model-selection evaluation.

question: A machine learning team has a base pretrained LLM and a small, high-quality dataset of input-to-expected-output pairs that have all been manually labeled for a specific customer-support task. They want to retrain the model's weights so it performs better on this task. Which fine-tuning approach are they using?
explanation: According to the notes, Supervised Fine Tuning (SFT) is fine-tuning when the dataset is already labeled, learning from input→expected-output pairs, used when you have quality labeled data for a specific task. This matches the scenario. B is wrong because self-supervised/unsupervised is pretraining, the opposite of SFT (no clear labels). C (RAG) is for adding new knowledge without retraining, not fine-tuning on labeled data. D (Pruning) is a model-compression technique that removes parameters, unrelated to training on labeled data.

question: A startup needs to fine-tune a large language model but has a very limited compute budget and cannot afford to update all of the model's weights. They want an approach that updates only a small set of parameters while freezing the rest. Which technique BEST fits this requirement?

options.text: Parameter Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT) such as LoRA
Domain-Specific Fine-tuning with full weight updates

explanation: The notes state clearly that PEFT updates only a small subset of parameters and freezes the rest, dramatically cutting cost; LoRA is a type of PEFT, and the exam tip: choose PEFT/LoRA when the question emphasizes limited compute/budget. A (Full fine-tuning) updates all weights, costing the most compute/memory/storage — the opposite of the requirement. B (Last-Layer) is just a middle ground between full and PEFT, not the most cost-optimal choice per the tip. D is still full weight updates, so it's expensive.

question: An organization wants to add fresh, proprietary knowledge to its LLM-powered assistant. The knowledge changes frequently and they want to avoid the cost of retraining the model each time it updates. According to the exam guidance, which approach should they prefer?

options.text: Full fine-tuning on the proprietary data

explanation: The Exam tip in the notes: to add new/proprietary knowledge without retraining, RAG should be preferred; fine-tuning is for style/behavior or deeply embedded domain expertise and is expensive to update. A (Full fine-tuning) is expensive and hard to update when knowledge changes frequently. C (Instruction Fine-tuning) teaches the model to follow instructions/format, not add new facts. D (Pruning) is model compression, unrelated to adding knowledge.

question: A team wants to convert a base pretrained model into a chat/assistant model that reliably follows user instructions and formats its responses well across many tasks, rather than injecting new factual knowledge. Which fine-tuning method matches this goal?
explanation: Notes: Instruction Fine-tuning uses a dataset of good and bad prompt examples, teaching the model to follow instructions/format (not new facts), and this is how base models become chat/assistant models. A (Domain-Specific) is what actually injects knowledge/vocabulary of a narrow domain — the opposite of the 'no new facts' requirement. B (Pruning) is model compression. D (Last-Layer) describes changing the training method (freezing all layers except the last), not the goal of teaching instruction-following.

question: A deployment engineer wants to shrink a trained model to achieve faster inference and lower memory usage by removing low-importance weights and neurons, accepting some accuracy cost. Which statements about this technique are correct? (Choose TWO.)

options.text: The technique is called pruning, a model compression technique like quantization or distillation
Post-Training Pruning involves manually changing the model weights file
Pruning injects new domain knowledge into the model's weights
It is a form of Parameter Efficient Fine-Tuning that freezes most parameters
It requires labeled input-to-output pairs to work

explanation: According to the notes: Pruning removes less important weights/neurons to shrink the model → faster inference, less memory, trading off some accuracy, and it's a model compression technique (like quantization/distillation) → A correct. Post-Training Pruning is manually modifying the weights file → B correct. C is wrong because adding domain knowledge is the job of Domain-Specific Fine-tuning, not pruning. D is wrong because pruning is model compression, distinct from PEFT (though both reduce resource use, the notes don't classify pruning as PEFT). E is wrong because pruning doesn't require labeled data pairs.

question: During an exam review, a candidate must recall specific model layer-count facts. Which of the following is correct according to the notes?

options.text: BERT base has 24 layers and BERT large has 12 layers
T5 base has 24 encoder and 24 decoder layers

explanation: The notes state clearly that GPT-3 has 96 layers at its largest configuration (175 billion parameters) → A correct. B is wrong because BERT base = 12 layers, large = 24 layers (this is reversed). C is wrong because GPT-2 has 12 to 48 layers depending on size, not always 96. D is wrong because T5 base = 12 encoder + 12 decoder layers (larger versions go up to 24 on each side).

question: A data science team is building an entity extraction feature. They need to pull entities that are not restricted to a fixed set of predefined classes, and they want to avoid the effort of labeling training data. According to the demo, which approach best meets these requirements?

options.text: Use a large language model on Amazon Bedrock and specify what to extract using natural language instructions
Train an Amazon Comprehend custom entity recognizer from scratch before any extraction can occur
Use a classic entity extractor limited to a fixed set of predefined entity classes
Manually label a large dataset first, then build a rule-based extractor

explanation: The notes state clearly: unlike classic extractors (limited to predefined classes), using an LLM lets you specify what to extract in natural language, more flexible/accurate and without needing to label data. So A is correct. C is wrong because that's exactly the limitation of classic extractors. B and D are wrong because the goal is to avoid labeling; moreover, an LLM can help assemble a dataset to later do custom entity recognition with Comprehend, not a prerequisite.

question: During the entity extraction demo, LLM output is used to help assemble a dataset that can later power a custom solution. Which AWS service is referenced for that custom entity recognition follow-up?
explanation: The notes state clearly that an LLM can help assemble a dataset for a custom solution, e.g. Amazon Comprehend custom entity recognition, so B is correct. Kendra (enterprise search), Textract (extraction from documents/images), and Rekognition (computer vision) are not mentioned in the notes for this scenario.

question: A developer is implementing the summarization sub-pattern from the text generation lab using an Amazon Titan model. They notice that for very large token counts the response feels slow. Which statement, based on the demo, correctly explains this behavior and the model used?

options.text: Summarization uses Anthropic Claude v3, which streams tokens one at a time and is therefore slow
Summarization uses Titan, which generates the whole summary in a single output and can be slow for large token counts
Summarization uses Titan, which returns partial streaming chunks, so latency is unrelated to token count
Summarization uses a classic extractor, so speed depends only on the number of predefined classes

explanation: The notes say the Summarization pattern uses Titan (depends on document size/context length) and Titan TTP generates the entire summary in a single output pass, so it can be slow with large token counts. So B is correct. A is wrong because summarization uses Titan, not Claude v3 (Claude v3 is used in the entity extraction demo via LangChain). C is wrong because Titan outputs in a single pass, not streaming piece by piece. D is wrong because summarization isn't a classic extractor.

question: An exam scenario asks about the QA prompt used in the Simplified Question Answering demo. The prompt instructs the assistant to answer concisely and to say "I am unsure" when it does not know the answer. What is the primary purpose of that instruction?

options.text: To reduce the maxTokenCount setting automatically
To increase the model temperature for more creative answers
To avoid hallucination when the model is uncertain
To enable streaming of partial responses to the user

explanation: The notes give a sample QA prompt asking the assistant to answer concisely and say "I am unsure" when uncertain, to avoid hallucination. So C is correct. A, B, D are unrelated to the purpose of this guidance and aren't mentioned in the notes for this scenario; the temperature in the Titan demo is even set to 0.

question: A developer is invoking an Amazon Titan text model with boto3 for zero-shot generation. Based on the Titan invocation shown in the demo, which TWO statements are correct? (Choose TWO)

options.text: The request body includes a textGenerationConfig with parameters such as maxTokenCount, temperature, and topP
The generated text is read from response_body.get('results')[0].get('outputText')
The prompt must be passed as a field named 'messages' containing role and content pairs
The invocation requires installing langchain-aws and using the ChatBedrock class
The output is returned as a list under the key 'entities' extracted by Amazon Comprehend

explanation: According to the notes, the Titan request body is json.dumps({"inputText": prompt, "textGenerationConfig": {"maxTokenCount":512, "stopSequences":[], "temperature":0, "topP":0.9}}), so A is correct; and the output is read via response_body.get('results')[0].get('outputText'), so B is correct. C is wrong because the prompt is passed via the 'inputText' field, not 'messages'. D describes the entity extraction demo using LangChain ChatBedrock with Claude v3, not this boto3 Titan call. E is fabricated and not in the notes.

question: A data scientist is designing a prompt for a foundation model. The prompt tells the model what task to perform, provides external reference information, includes the specific text to be processed, and specifies that the answer must be returned as JSON. Which element of the prompt structure specifies that the answer must be returned as JSON?
explanation: According to the notes, a prompt has up to 4 parts: Instructions (the task), Context (external information to guide it), Input data (the data to respond to), and Output indicator (the type/format of the output). Requiring the answer in JSON format is exactly specifying the output format, so the answer is Output indicator (D). Instructions (A) only describes the task, not the format; Context (B) is external reference information; Input data (C) is the text to be processed.

question: A team asks a large language model to classify customer sentiment by providing only the task description in the prompt, without including any worked examples. Which prompting technique are they using?
explanation: The notes define Zero-shot prompting as giving the LLM a task WITHOUT providing any additional examples ('without giving it further examples'), matching the scenario. Few-shot (A) and One-shot (C) both require accompanying examples (one or several shots). Chain-of-Thought (D) is breaking reasoning into intermediate steps, not what's described here.

question: A developer needs a model to solve multi-step math word problems more accurately. According to the notes, which prompting approach breaks complex reasoning into intermediary steps, and what trigger phrase is commonly used?

options.text: Zero-shot prompting, triggered by the phrase "Answer directly"
Few-shot prompting, triggered by the phrase "Here are examples"
Chain-of-Thought prompting, triggered by the phrase "Think Step-by-Step"
One-shot prompting, triggered by the phrase "Reason once"
Output indicator prompting, triggered by the phrase "Format as list"

explanation: The notes state clearly that Chain-of-Thought (CoT) breaks complex reasoning into intermediate steps and helps with math/logic/multi-step problems; the trigger phrase is 'Think Step-by-Step'. So C is correct. Zero-shot (A), Few-shot (B), One-shot (D) don't use that trigger phrase and don't describe step-by-step reasoning; 'Output indicator prompting' (E) isn't a prompting technique in the notes.

question: A developer calls the Amazon Bedrock InvokeModel operation with boto3 but has not set the body parameter. Based on the notes, what is true about invoking a Bedrock model this way? (Choose TWO.)

options.text: The body parameter is required for invoke_model.
The call requires the bedrock:InvokeModel IAM action.
The modelId parameter is optional and can be omitted.
The guardrailIdentifier parameter is required for every call.
The body parameter must always be plain text and never bytes.

explanation: The notes state client.invoke_model(...) and emphasize that 'body (bytes/file) is REQUIRED', so A is correct. The notes also say you need the IAM action 'bedrock:InvokeModel', so B is correct. C is wrong because modelId is a required parameter to identify the model (not noted as optional, and omitting it means you can't call a specific model). D is wrong because guardrailIdentifier is only an optional parameter, not required on every call. E is wrong because body is described as bytes/file, not always plain text.

question: A developer sends a request to a newer Claude model such as claude-3-haiku on Bedrock using the older InvokeModel text format and receives a ValidationException. According to the notes, what must the developer do to fix this?

options.text: Use the Messages API format with anthropic_version, max_tokens, and a messages array
Add the bedrock:InvokeModel IAM permission to the request
Switch the technique from zero-shot to few-shot prompting
Set a guardrailIdentifier and guardrailVersion on the call

explanation: The notes state that newer Claude models (e.g. claude-3-haiku) will raise a ValidationException with the old InvokeModel text format, and you must use the Messages API format with a body containing anthropic_version, max_tokens, messages: [{role, content:[{type:text, text:...}]}], temperature, top_p. So A is correct. B is about IAM permissions, unrelated to a format error; C is a prompting technique that doesn't fix a format error; D is a guardrail, unrelated.

question: When structuring a conversation with a Claude model, an engineer wants to define the model's overall behavior and persona, separate from the human's turn-by-turn input. According to the notes, which role defines the overall AI behavior/persona?
explanation: The notes describe Claude's Message roles: the System role defines the AI's overall behavior/persona; the User role is the human input driving the conversation; there are also advanced roles for simulating multiple personas. So the System role (B) is correct. The User role (A) is human input and doesn't define the overall persona. 'Output indicator role' (C) and 'Context role' (D) aren't message roles — they are elements within the prompt structure, not conversation roles.

question: A company needs its Amazon Bedrock chatbot to answer questions using proprietary internal documents that change frequently. The answers must cite the source content and stay current, and the team wants to avoid the cost and time of retraining the foundation model. Which approach best meets these requirements?

options.text: Fine-tune the foundation model on the internal documents each time they change
Use a Bedrock Knowledge Base with a Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) workflow
Increase the model's temperature so it produces more varied answers
Continuously pre-train a new foundation model from scratch on the documents

explanation: According to the notes, when you need current or company-specific information, source citations, and cheap updates, use RAG/Knowledge Base instead of fine-tuning. RAG retrieves relevant chunks from your data and inserts them into the prompt as context, adding knowledge without needing fine-tuning/retraining. A and D are expensive fine-tuning/retraining, exactly what the requirement wants to avoid. C (temperature) only affects output randomness, not adding knowledge or citations.

question: A team is configuring a Bedrock Knowledge Base and needs to ingest a set of PDF documents. In the console, what must they enable so that the PDF content can be analyzed and extracted?
explanation: The notes state clearly: to parse a PDF you must enable Advanced Parsing (using an LLM like Claude 3 Sonnet or Claude 3 Haiku to analyze and extract information). Semantic Chunking and No Chunking are chunking strategies, unrelated to PDF parsing. Web Crawler is a data source, not a PDF-parsing mechanism.

question: An engineer wants a chunking strategy for a Bedrock Knowledge Base where retrieval returns small, precise passages but can then expand to a larger surrounding passage for fuller context. Which chunking option provides this parent/child behavior?
explanation: According to the notes, Hierarchical Chunking uses parent/child chunks: retrieving a small chunk then expanding to the parent chunk. Fixed = equal token size with overlap. Semantic = splitting at semantic boundaries. No Chunking = one chunk for the whole file (for documents that are already small). Only Hierarchical accurately describes the retrieve-small-then-expand-to-parent behavior.

question: A solutions architect is setting up a Bedrock Knowledge Base and must select a supported vector store for storing the embeddings. Which of the following is a valid vector store option?
explanation: The notes list the supported vector stores: OpenSearch Serverless, Amazon Aurora, MongoDB Atlas, Pinecone, Redis Enterprise Cloud — so OpenSearch Serverless is correct. DynamoDB isn't in the list. Titan Text Embeddings is an embedding model (used to create vectors), not a place to store vectors. S3 is a data source, not a vector store.

question: After a Bedrock Knowledge Base has been created and populated, a team regularly adds, modifies, and removes files in the data source. What must they do so these changes are reflected in query results, and what is the nature of that operation?

options.text: Re-create the entire Knowledge Base from scratch each time files change
Nothing; the Knowledge Base automatically re-embeds every document in real time
Sync (ingest) the data source, which processes only the changed documents incrementally
Fine-tune the embeddings model on the new files

explanation: The notes state that every time you add/edit/delete a file, you must sync (ingest) the data source again to re-index, and the sync is incremental (only changed documents). So C is correct. A (recreating the whole KB) is unnecessary. B is wrong because manual sync is required, it's not automatic real-time. D is wrong because there's no step to fine-tune the embeddings model; embedding just converts a chunk into a vector.

question: A developer is enabling Advanced Parsing on a Bedrock Knowledge Base to extract information from documents using an LLM. Which foundation models can be selected for this advanced parsing task? (Choose TWO.)
explanation: The notes state clearly that Advanced Parsing uses an LLM, either Claude 3 Sonnet or Claude 3 Haiku, to analyze and extract information, so A and B are correct. Titan Text Embeddings and Cohere Embed Multilingual are embedding models (converting chunks into vectors), not used for parsing. Stable Diffusion is an image-generation model, unrelated and not mentioned in the notes.

question: A development team wants to standardize how they build prompts for a customer-service chatbot on Amazon Bedrock. They need to define a prompt once and reuse it across requests, inserting different customer names and ticket details at runtime without rewriting the prompt each time. Which Amazon Bedrock capability should they use?

options.text: Prompt Manager, which provides reusable prompt templates with variable injection
Model fine-tuning to embed the customer details into the model weights
A knowledge base to retrieve customer records at inference time
Provisioned Throughput to guarantee capacity for each request

explanation: According to the notes, Prompt Manager allows creating reusable prompt templates with variable injection — matching the need to define a prompt once and inject values at runtime. B is wrong because the notes clearly say 'fine-tune prompt engineering' is NOT model fine-tuning; injecting runtime variables is unrelated to retraining weights. C (knowledge base) and D (Provisioned Throughput) aren't mentioned in the notes and don't solve the reusable-template problem.

question: A prompt engineer is experimenting with a single prompt on Amazon Bedrock Prompt Manager. They create several alternative versions of the prompt — each using slightly different wording, and some pointing to a different model or inference parameters — so they can A/B compare them and pick the best-performing one before deployment. What is this feature called?
explanation: According to the notes, a 'variant' is an alternate version of the same prompt (different wording/model/params) that you A/B compare to find the best version before deployment — matching the description. B is wrong because creating a variant is 'fine-tune prompt engineering', not model fine-tuning. C (Test variables) is for filling in placeholder values, not creating an alternate prompt version. D (Prompt Flow nodes) isn't the variant concept; Prompt Manager only integrates with Prompt Flow, but a node isn't a prompt variant.

question: In Amazon Bedrock Prompt Manager, a template contains placeholders such as {{role}}, {{instruct}}, and {{format}}. During testing, how are these placeholders given concrete values?

options.text: By supplying Test variables, each defined as a variable name plus a value
By retraining the foundation model on the placeholder values
By attaching an IAM policy that maps each placeholder to a value
By configuring Provisioned Throughput for each variable

explanation: The notes state clearly that placeholders like {{variable}} are filled by 'Test variables' consisting of variable name + value. B is wrong because filling in variables is unrelated to retraining the model. C (IAM policy) and D (Provisioned Throughput) aren't mentioned and aren't the placeholder-filling mechanism in Prompt Manager.

question: A team building a multi-step generative AI application on Amazon Bedrock has created reusable prompt templates in Prompt Manager. They now want to orchestrate these prompts into a larger workflow. According to how Prompt Manager is designed to work, which Amazon Bedrock feature does it integrate with for this purpose?
explanation: The notes state clearly that Prompt Manager 'Integrates with Amazon Bedrock Prompt Flow' — used to orchestrate prompts into a workflow. B (Guardrails), C (fine-tuning), and D (Provisioned Throughput) aren't mentioned as Prompt Manager integrations in the notes, so all are wrong.

question: A development team wants the simplest possible way to answer user questions from a Bedrock Knowledge Base. They need retrieval, prompt augmentation, and answer generation all handled by Bedrock in a single call, and they want the response to include citations. Which Knowledge Base API should they use?

options.text: The Retrieve API, then manually pass the chunks to a model
The Retrieve API with overrideSearchType set to HYBRID

explanation: According to the notes, RetrieveAndGenerate is fully managed end-to-end RAG: Bedrock handles both retrieval (similarity search), prompt augmentation, and answer generation in ONE single call, returning a natural-language answer with citations — matching the requirement for 'the simplest, single call' approach. A and D use Retrieve, which only returns chunks, requiring you to augment the prompt and call the model yourself (multiple steps). C is the LangChain integration for a custom workflow, not a managed one-call approach. So B is correct.

question: An engineer is building a custom RAG pipeline with LangChain. They need raw text chunks — along with the location type, URI, and relevance scores — so that they can apply their own re-ranking and construct their own prompt template before choosing which model to call. Which Knowledge Base API meets this requirement?

options.text: The RetrieveAndGenerate API, because it returns citations
The InvokeModel API pointed at the Knowledge Base
The RetrieveAndGenerate API with a custom prompt template

explanation: The notes state clearly that the Retrieve API only returns retrieved text chunks (with location type, URI, and relevance scores); you augment the prompt and call the model yourself — suited for cases needing custom orchestration like LangChain, re-ranking, or a custom prompt template. A and D are RetrieveAndGenerate — managed one-call, which doesn't give you control over the prompt/model this way. B isn't mentioned as a KB retrieval method in the notes. So C is correct.

question: A company deploys RAG to connect Amazon Bedrock Foundation Models to its internal document repository. Which benefits does RAG provide according to the described approach? (Choose TWO.)

options.text: It lets the model produce relevant, context-specific answers without continuously retraining the FM
It provides source attribution that improves transparency and minimizes hallucinations
It permanently fine-tunes the FM weights with the company data
It removes the need for any embeddings or vector search
It guarantees the FM will never require a vector store

explanation: The notes state two benefits: (A) accessing external data helps the model generate more relevant, accurate answers WITHOUT needing continuous FM retraining; (B) retrieval results come with source attribution → increasing transparency and reducing hallucination. C is wrong because RAG is non-parametric, not modifying/fine-tuning the model's weights. D and E are wrong because RAG relies on similarity/semantic search (embeddings, vectors), not eliminating them.

question: When configuring the Retrieve API call, a developer notices that user queries often contain exact product IDs and acronyms that must be matched precisely, in addition to matching on meaning. Which value of overrideSearchType in the vectorSearchConfiguration is most appropriate?

options.text: SEMANTIC, because it uses pure vector similarity
HYBRID, because it combines semantic vectors with keyword matching
LEXICAL, because it only matches exact keywords
numberOfResults, set to a higher value

explanation: The notes: HYBRID combines semantic vectors + keyword (lexical) matching, which is better when exact terms/IDs/acronyms matter — matching this scenario. A (SEMANTIC) is pure vector/embedding based on meaning and doesn't handle exact keyword matching well. C 'LEXICAL' isn't a valid value listed (only HYBRID and SEMANTIC exist). D is a parameter for the number of results returned, unrelated to the search type. So B is correct.

question: A data scientist wants to build a custom workflow on top of a Bedrock Knowledge Base using LangChain, converting user queries into embeddings, searching the KB via semantic search, and returning relevant results to feed into a chat model. Which combination should they use?

options.text: AmazonKnowledgeBasesRetriever from langchain.retrievers.bedrock together with ChatBedrock
The RetrieveAndGenerate API called directly from boto3
The Retrieve API with overrideSearchType set to HYBRID only
InvokeModel with mistral.mistral-7b and no retriever

explanation: The notes' LangChain integration section states exactly: use AmazonKnowledgeBasesRetriever from langchain.retrievers.bedrock along with ChatBedrock — converting a query to embeddings, searching the KB, and returning results for a custom workflow built on semantic search. B is managed one-call, not a custom LangChain workflow. C is just a search-type configuration for Retrieve, not a LangChain integration. D has no retriever, so it doesn't query the KB. So A is correct.

question: An architect explains why RAG can improve answer accuracy without modifying the Foundation Model's parameters. Which statement correctly characterizes RAG as described?

options.text: RAG is parametric: it stores company data inside the model weights
RAG is non-parametric: it retrieves data from outside the model and augments the prompt with the relevant retrieved context
RAG replaces the Foundation Model with a keyword search engine
RAG requires continuous retraining of the FM to stay current

explanation: The notes state clearly that RAG is non-parametric — it retrieves data from OUTSIDE the model and augments the prompt with the retrieved relevant context. A is wrong (RAG doesn't store data in the weights — that would be parametric). C is wrong because RAG still uses the FM to generate answers, not replacing it with a keyword search engine. D is wrong because RAG lets you get up-to-date answers WITHOUT needing continuous FM retraining. So B is correct.

question: A data science team needs to build a generative AI workflow on Amazon Bedrock where all processing steps are known in advance and must execute in a fixed, predictable order from input to output. They want to wire the steps together visually rather than let the system decide routing at runtime. Which Amazon Bedrock capability best fits this requirement?

options.text: An Amazon Bedrock Agent that autonomously routes between actions
Amazon Bedrock Prompt Flow, a visually-built deterministic pipeline
A Bedrock Knowledge Base with automatic retrieval

explanation: According to the notes, Prompt Flow is a 'visually-built, deterministic pipeline' used when the steps are known in advance and connected into a fixed graph (input → steps → output). This exactly matches the requirement. A is wrong because Agent routes autonomously (autonomous routing), the opposite of deterministic; the notes state clearly that Prompt Flow contrasts with Agent. C and D are individual nodes/capabilities, not the mechanism for orchestrating the whole workflow.

question: An engineer building an Amazon Bedrock Prompt Flow needs to process a list of customer records: run the same prompt on each record individually, then combine all the individual results back into a single list for downstream use. Which two flow-control nodes should the engineer use? (Choose TWO.)

options.text: Iterator node to loop over each item in the list
Condition node to branch based on if/else logic
Collector node to gather iterated outputs back into a single list
S3 Storage node to write each result to a bucket
Lambda Function node to run custom code per item

explanation: The notes define Iterator = loop over each item in a list, and Collector = gather iterated outputs back into a single list. Combining these two nodes matches the scenario of looping through each record and then merging the results. B (Condition) is used for if/else branching, unrelated to looping/merging. D and E are other capability nodes, not the flow-control nodes serving the loop-and-merge purpose described in the question.

question: A developer wants to add conditional if/else routing inside an Amazon Bedrock Prompt Flow so that the workflow branches down different paths depending on a value produced by an earlier step. Which node type provides this capability?
explanation: According to the notes, the Condition node lets you 'branch the flow based on logic (if/else routing)', matching the need for branching. Collector gathers iterated output, Iterator loops through a list, Flow Output emits the final result, Knowledge base is for querying a KB — none of these perform if/else routing.

question: A platform team wants to define their Amazon Bedrock Prompt Flow programmatically as code so it can be version-controlled and deployed repeatably, instead of building it by hand each time in the console. According to the notes, which approach does Amazon Bedrock support for this?

options.text: Define the Prompt Flow using CloudFormation (CFN)
Export the flow only as an Amazon Lex bot definition
Prompt Flows can be created solely through the Visual Editor
Define the flow using an Iterator node configuration file

explanation: The notes state clearly, 'You can use CloudFormation (CFN) to programmatically define a Prompt Flow', matching the need to define it via code. C is wrong because although there's a Visual Editor, the notes confirm CFN can also be used, not only the Visual Editor. B and D aren't mentioned in the notes as ways to define a flow via code.

question: In the Amazon Bedrock Prompt Flow console builder, an engineer drags a flow from Flow input through an Agents node and out to Flow output. Which of the following accurately reflects how nodes and ports are described in the notes?

options.text: The Agents node accepts fields such as agentInputText, promptAttributes, and sessionAttributes, and each port is typed as String or Object
All ports are untyped and pass raw binary data between nodes
The Visual Editor is unavailable and flows can only be built via API calls
Agents nodes cannot be connected between Flow input and Flow output

explanation: The notes describe the console builder: dragging Flow input → nodes (e.g. an Agents node with agentInputText, promptAttributes, sessionAttributes) → Flow output, and 'each port is typed (String/Object)'. So A is correct. B is wrong because ports are typed (String/Object), not untyped/binary. C is wrong because Prompt Flow does have a Visual Editor (similar to Application Composer). D is wrong because the notes themselves illustrate an Agents node sitting between Flow input and Flow output.

question: A developer is building an Amazon Bedrock agent for a hotel platform. The agent must be able to actually create a room booking in the backend system when a customer confirms a reservation. Which Amazon Bedrock Agents capability enables the agent to take this real-world action?

options.text: Add an Action Group that maps the agent's decision to a Lambda function containing the booking business logic
Add a Knowledge Base so the agent can retrieve booking policy documents
Enable the Code Interpreter so the agent can run booking code in a sandbox
Enable Sessions Management so the booking is remembered across turns

explanation: According to the notes, an Action Group is how an agent performs a real action: it defines tasks/tools and maps the agent's decision to a Lambda (business logic) or an API schema, e.g. 'create a booking'. B is wrong because a Knowledge Base is only used to retrieve information, not to execute an action. C is wrong because Code Interpreter only runs code in a sandbox (math, data analysis, charts), not to call a backend for booking. D is wrong because Sessions Management only maintains conversation context, not creating a booking.

question: A team must choose between Amazon Bedrock Agents and Prompt Flow for a new feature. The workflow is open-ended: the exact tools and steps needed cannot be predetermined and must be decided dynamically based on the user's request. Which option best fits, and why?

options.text: Prompt Flow, because it lets the model decide which steps to run at runtime
Prompt Flow, because open-ended reasoning requires a fixed deterministic pipeline
Agents, because the model decides which tools and steps to run dynamically (autonomous, non-deterministic)
Agents, because they force a predefined, deterministic sequence of nodes
Either option works identically for open-ended reasoning

explanation: According to the Exam note: Agents = the model decides ON ITS OWN which tools/steps to run dynamically (autonomous, non-deterministic), suited for open-ended reasoning. Prompt Flow = YOU define a fixed pipeline in advance, deterministic — chosen for known steps. A is wrong because Prompt Flow doesn't let the model decide on its own. B is wrong because open-ended reasoning needs Agents, not a fixed pipeline. D is wrong because that describes Prompt Flow, not Agents. So C is correct.

question: An Amazon Bedrock agent is frequently asked to perform multi-step arithmetic and parse CSV data. The team notices the model sometimes fabricates computed results. Which agent feature directly addresses this by letting the agent compute answers reliably instead of hallucinating them?
explanation: According to the notes, Code Interpreter provides a sandboxed code execution environment (math, data parsing, charts), letting the agent write and run code instead of 'hallucinating computed answers' — matching the scenario of fabricated calculation results. A (Guardrails) is used for content safety controls, unrelated to computation. B (Action Groups) is used to call a Lambda/API to perform a business action, not a sandboxed computation environment. C (Sessions Management) only keeps conversation context. So D is correct.

question: A conversational Bedrock agent needs to remember prior messages from a customer across multiple turns within the same conversation. Which statement about Sessions Management in Amazon Bedrock Agents is correct?

options.text: It maintains conversation state/context across turns within a session_id, bounded by the model's context window
It stores unlimited conversation history independent of the chosen model
It maps the agent's decisions to a Lambda function to execute business logic
It provides a sandbox where the agent runs code to persist state

explanation: According to the notes, Sessions Management maintains state/context across turns within a session_id so the agent remembers previous messages, but it's limited by the model's context window ('limited based on the LLM'). B is wrong because it's not unlimited, and it depends on the model. C is wrong because that describes an Action Group. D is wrong because that's Code Interpreter. So A is correct.

question: Using the Amazon Bedrock Agent Builder, a team wants to enrich their agent so it can ground answers on internal documents and enforce content safety policies. Which TWO components can be added to the agent to meet these needs? (Choose TWO.)

options.text: A predefined deterministic Prompt Flow pipeline
An Amazon EC2 Auto Scaling group

explanation: According to the notes, Agent Builder lets you add a Knowledge Base (so the agent can retrieve/ground on internal documents) and Guardrails (to enforce content safety) — these are two independently listed features. C and E (GPU cluster, EC2 Auto Scaling) aren't mentioned in the notes. D is wrong because Prompt Flow is an alternative choice to Agents (a fixed pipeline), not an added component inside an agent. So A and B are correct.

question: A developer has finished building a restaurant assistant agent in Amazon Bedrock and now wants their application to send a customer query to the agent and receive a response at request time. Which boto3 client and API should the application use?

options.text: The bedrock-agent client with the invoke_agent API
The bedrock-agent-runtime client with the invoke_agent API
The bedrock-agent client with the prepare API
The bedrock-agent-runtime client with the retrieve_and_generate API

explanation: According to the notes, calling an agent (invoke_agent) is a runtime/data-plane action and belongs to the bedrock-agent-runtime client. B is correct. A is wrong because bedrock-agent is the control plane (build-time), not for running inference. C is wrong because prepare belongs to the control plane for configuring an agent, not for calling an agent. D is wrong because retrieve_and_generate is used to query a Knowledge Base, not to send a query to an agent.

question: A team is writing an automation script that needs to create, update, and prepare a Bedrock agent and its knowledge base as part of a build pipeline. Which boto3 client provides these control plane operations?
explanation: The notes state clearly that bedrock-agent is the control plane used to create, update, delete, prepare an agent or knowledge base (build-time), not for running inference. C is correct. A is wrong because bedrock-agent-runtime is the data/runtime plane (invoke_agent, retrieve, retrieve_and_generate). B and D aren't mentioned in the notes as agent-management clients, so they aren't correct per the material.

question: While configuring an action group in the Bedrock console, a developer chooses the 'Return control' invocation option instead of having Bedrock call a Lambda function. What behavior does this configuration produce?

options.text: Bedrock automatically creates a new Lambda function to execute the action
The agent hands the function call back to the developer's own application to execute the logic
The agent asks the user to confirm before running a sensitive action
The agent stores the reservation directly in the DynamoDB table without any code

explanation: According to the notes, 'Return control' means that instead of Bedrock calling Lambda, the agent hands the function call back to YOUR application to execute itself; used when the logic lives outside AWS/Lambda. B is correct. A describes the Quick-create new Lambda option, not Return control. C describes the 'Enable confirmation of action group function' feature (human-in-the-loop), not Return control. D isn't a mechanism described in the notes.

question: A restaurant assistant agent exposes a function that deletes a reservation. To reduce the risk that a prompt-injection attack triggers this sensitive operation without oversight, which action group setting should the developer enable?

options.text: Enable confirmation of action group function

explanation: The notes state that 'Enable confirmation of action group function' is a human-in-the-loop gate: the agent asks the user to confirm before running a sensitive action (e.g. delete/create), reducing the risk of an action being triggered by prompt injection. D is correct. A is a way of defining the action group (providing an OpenAPI schema), unrelated to protecting against prompt injection. B hands execution to an external application, not a confirmation mechanism. C is just a way to create the Lambda backend and doesn't reduce prompt-injection risk.

question: When associating a Menus Knowledge Base with the agent in the workshop, which two AWS resources make up the architecture described for the knowledge base? (Choose TWO.)
explanation: The notes describe the Knowledge Base architecture using an Amazon OpenSearch Serverless index plus a Documents S3 bucket. A and C are correct. B (DynamoDB) is the table storing reservations handled by Lambda in the agent-creation step, not a KB component. D (Lambda) processes bookings for the action group, not part of the KB architecture. E (API Gateway) is only mentioned as an optional backend for API schemas, not a KB component.

question: A developer sets up an action group and wants Bedrock to build the tool specification by having them describe each function and its parameters as JSON objects, rather than supplying an OpenAPI schema. Which action group type matches this approach?

options.text: Enable confirmation of action group function

explanation: The notes: 'Define with function details' means you describe the functions/params via JSON and Bedrock automatically builds the tool spec. A is correct. B 'Define with API schemas' requires providing an OpenAPI schema for a Lambda/API Gateway backend, the opposite of the scenario. C 'Return control' is an invocation option (who executes the function), not an action-group definition type. D is the human-in-the-loop confirmation feature, unrelated to how functions are defined.

question: A machine learning team is preparing a training dataset to fine-tune a custom model in Amazon Bedrock. According to the Bedrock documentation for preparing training and validation datasets, which file format must the data use?

options.text: CSV, with one column for the prompt and one column for the completion
JSONL, where each line is one complete data sample
A single JSON array containing all data samples
Parquet, with newline characters preserved inside each sample

explanation: According to the notes, Bedrock requires data in JSONL format, where each line is one complete data sample (one sample per line), and all \n characters within a sample must be removed. A is wrong because it's not CSV. C is wrong because it's not a single JSON array but individual JSON objects on each line. D is wrong because it's not Parquet, and the notes require removing (not keeping) \n characters within a sample.

question: When preparing a JSONL dataset for Bedrock fine-tuning using the single-turn messaging format, a data engineer must structure the messages array correctly. According to the rules described, which requirements apply to the messages array? (Choose TWO.)

options.text: The messages array must contain exactly 2 messages
The first message must have the role user
The first message must have the role assistant
The system field is required for every sample
The messages array can contain any number of messages as long as roles alternate

explanation: According to the notes, the rules of single-turn messaging are: the messages array must contain exactly 2 messages (A correct), the first message has role = user and the last message has role = assistant (B correct). C is wrong because the first message must be user, not assistant. D is wrong because the system field is optional, not required. E is wrong because the message count is fixed at 2, not arbitrary.

question: A developer is defining the fine-tuning single-turn messaging format for a Bedrock custom model. Which field is optional and used to set the conversation context as a string?
explanation: According to the notes, in the single-turn messaging fine-tuning format, the system field is optional and is a string used to set the context of the conversation. A (prompt) and D (completion) belong to the prompt/completion format from the Claude dataset-generation example, not a context field of the messaging format. B (role) is a required attribute of each message object (user or assistant), not an optional field for setting context.

question: A team used Claude to generate a training dataset of 100 unique animals as a JSONL artifact, where each line looks like {"prompt": "Classify this using a single word if this animal as a bird, reptile, mammal, amphibian or fish: Dog: ", "completion": "mammal"}. Which statement correctly describes this JSONL artifact?

options.text: Each line is a separate, complete JSON object containing a prompt and its completion
