Which guardrail capability helps reduce these hallucinations by scoring how well the response is supported by the reference source?
explanation: The notes describe the Contextual grounding check as scoring (against a threshold) whether a response sticks to the reference source and is factually correct, helping reduce hallucination in RAG — matching the requirement. A blocks custom topics. B masks personal data. D blocks profane language; none of these evaluate how well an answer sticks to its source.

question: A content moderation team is configuring an Amazon Bedrock guardrail. Which statements accurately describe how Amazon Bedrock Guardrails work according to their design? (Choose TWO.)

options.text: Guardrails filter both the input prompt and the output response of the model.
Guardrails retrain the underlying foundation model to remove harmful behaviour.
A guardrail is an external policy layer that can be reused across multiple applications and foundation models.
Guardrails can only be applied to a single foundation model and cannot be reused.
Guardrails only inspect the model's output and ignore the user's prompt.

explanation: The notes emphasize that Guardrails filters BOTH the input prompt and the output response (A correct), and it's an external policy layer that doesn't retrain the model, can apply to multiple FMs, and is reusable across apps (C correct). B is wrong because a guardrail doesn't retrain the model, it's only an external policy layer. D is wrong because it can apply to multiple FMs and is reusable. E is wrong because a guardrail checks both input and output, not just output.

question: A company needs to verify whether a given image was generated by AWS's own image model, as part of an effort to combat deepfakes and authenticate AI-generated content. Which Amazon Bedrock capability should they use?
explanation: The notes state that Amazon Bedrock Watermark Detection checks the invisible watermark to confirm an image was generated by Titan Image Generator, used to counter deepfakes and verify AI origin — matching this scenario. A (Regex) catches a custom string format in text. C (custom words) blocks specific words/phrases. D (Relevance) checks whether a response is relevant to the question; none relate to images.

question: A data science team is defining a feature group in Amazon SageMaker Feature Store. According to the feature group definition, which THREE elements make up a feature group's definition? (The team must configure all of them.) Which single option correctly lists the required components?

options.text: A list of feature definitions, a record identifier name, and online/offline store configurations
A training job ARN, an endpoint name, and a model registry entry
A record identifier name, a batch transform config, and a hyperparameter list
A list of feature definitions, an inference pipeline, and a data wrangler flow

explanation: According to the notes, a feature group's definition includes: a list of feature definitions, a record identifier name, and configuration for the online and offline stores. So A is correct. Options B, C, D mention components (training job ARN, endpoint, model registry, batch transform, hyperparameters, inference pipeline, data wrangler flow) not listed in the feature group definition in the notes, so they are wrong.

question: An engineer is writing FeatureDefinitions for a SageMaker Feature Store feature group. A feature stores a 64-bit signed integer value. Which FeatureType should be assigned to this feature?
explanation: The notes state that FeatureTypes include String (default), Integral (Int64 – signed 64-bit integer), and Fractional (IEEE 64-bit floating point). A signed 64-bit integer value corresponds to Integral, so C is correct. String is the default type for text (A wrong), Fractional is for floating-point numbers (B wrong), and Boolean isn't a FeatureType listed in the notes (D wrong).

question: A team is building the OfflineStoreConfig section of the configuration JSON for a SageMaker Feature Store feature group. They must specify where the offline data is stored in Amazon S3 and the encryption key. Which TWO settings belong under the S3StorageConfig of the OfflineStoreConfig? (Choose TWO.)
explanation: According to the notes, OfflineStoreConfig consists of DataCatalogConfig and S3StorageConfig, where S3StorageConfig has KmsKeyId and S3Uri. So A (S3Uri) and B (KmsKeyId) are correct. EnableOnlineStore belongs to OnlineStoreConfig (C wrong). RecordIdentifierFeatureName and RoleArn are top-level fields of the config JSON, not inside S3StorageConfig (D, E wrong).

question: While reviewing SageMaker Feature Store terminology, a practitioner needs to describe a single set of values for the features in a feature group. Which Feature Store component does this describe?
explanation: The notes define a Record as 'a set of values for features in a feature group', so B is correct. A Feature Group is a logical grouping of features holding metadata (A wrong), FeatureDefinition is used to define a feature and its data type (C wrong), and OnlineStoreConfig is the configuration section for the online store in the config JSON (D wrong).

question: A retailer wants to use SageMaker Autopilot to predict the exact dollar amount a customer will spend on their next purchase. Which ML problem type should be selected?
explanation: Predicting a number (a quantitative value like a spending amount) is a linear regression problem per the notes ('number → regression'). Binary classification only produces 2 outcomes true/false, multiclass produces 3+ categories, and time-series forecasting is used for future values over a time series — not a single number based on customer attributes.

question: A telecom company wants to predict whether each subscriber will churn (leave) or stay. Which ML problem type in SageMaker Autopilot best fits this use case?
explanation: Churn prediction only has 2 outcomes (leave/stay = true/false), matching the definition of binary classification in the notes ('2 outcomes → binary classification', e.g. churn prediction). Linear regression is used to predict a number; multiclass is used for 3+ categories; time-series forecasting is used for future values over time.

question: A hospital wants to predict which of several possible patient outcomes will occur after discharge, where each patient is assigned to one of many outcome categories. Which ML problem type is appropriate?
explanation: Predicting patient outcomes after discharge is a '3+ category prediction' problem, corresponding to multiclass classification per the notes ('3+ labels → multiclass'). Binary only produces 2 outcomes; linear regression predicts a number; time-series forecasting predicts future values over a time series.

question: A warehouse operations team wants to predict inventory levels for the next quarter based on historical values over time. Which ML problem type should they choose?
explanation: Predicting next quarter's inventory based on future values over time is time-series forecasting per the notes ('future values over time → time-series forecasting', e.g. predict next quarter inventory → Timeseries). Linear regression predicts a number but not a series of future values over time; multiclass is for categories; binary is for 2 outcomes.

question: A manufacturer wants to build a custom model that classifies product photos as either defective or not defective. According to the notes' use-case-to-data-type mapping, which data type corresponds to this single-label image prediction use case?
explanation: The notes map 'Single-label image prediction → predict manufacturing defects in images → Image (JPG, PNG)'. So the data type is Image (JPG, PNG). Text/Binary/Categorical corresponds to multi-category text prediction; Timeseries corresponds to time-series forecasting; Numeric data corresponds to numeric prediction (house prices).

question: A machine learning team needs to load several years of historical transaction records directly into the offline store of Amazon SageMaker Feature Store so the data can be used to train a new model. Which approach supports this batch ingestion requirement for backfilling historical records?

options.text: Call the synchronous PutRecord API so the records are written to the online store and eventually replicated to the offline store.
Ingest directly into the offline store using a SageMaker Data Wrangler or a SageMaker Processing job with the Spark container.
Call the PutRecord API to write to both the online and offline store simultaneously.
Enable real-time streaming ingestion into the online store to capture the historical records.

explanation: According to the notes, ingesting directly into the offline store is useful for backfilling historical records for training, and is done using SageMaker Data Wrangler or a SageMaker Processing job with a Spark container -> B correct. A and C use the PutRecord API (targeting the online store / both stores), not directly ingesting into the offline store for backfill. D (real-time streaming) isn't mentioned in the notes and doesn't fit backfilling historical data.

question: During a design review, a developer asks which API is used to perform batch ingestion into the online store of Amazon SageMaker Feature Store. Which API call should be used?

options.text: A SageMaker Processing job using the Spark container.
The synchronous PutRecord API, which is eventually replicated to the offline store.
SageMaker Data Wrangler exporting directly to the online store.

explanation: According to the notes, ingestion into the online store is done by calling the synchronous PutRecord API, after which data is eventually replicated to the offline store -> C correct. A (BatchGetRecord) isn't mentioned for ingestion. B (Spark container) and D (Data Wrangler) are ways to ingest directly into the offline store, not the online store.

question: A team is documenting the batch ingestion models available for Amazon SageMaker Feature Store. According to these ingestion models, which statements are correct? (Choose TWO.)

options.text: Ingesting into both the online and offline store is done by calling the PutRecord API, which is buffered and written to the offline store.
Batch ingestion supports only a single model: writing to the online store.
Ingesting directly into the offline store is useful for backfilling historical records for training.
Ingesting into the online store requires a SageMaker Processing job with the Spark container.
Ingesting into the offline store is done by calling the synchronous PutRecord API.

explanation: The notes describe three batch ingestion models. A is correct: ingest into both stores by calling the PutRecord API, buffered and written down to the offline store. C is correct: ingesting directly into the offline store is useful for backfilling historical data for training. B is wrong because there are three models, not one. D is wrong because the online store uses the PutRecord API, not a Spark Processing job. E is wrong because the offline store is ingested directly via Data Wrangler/Spark, not via the PutRecord API.

question: A data engineering team uses SageMaker Feature Store and needs to write individual feature records in real time as events arrive from a Kafka topic. Which approach should they use to ingest these features?

options.text: Streaming ingestion using the PutRecord API
Batch ingestion using a Spark container
Offline Store Query to load records

explanation: According to the notes, ingestion happens two ways: Streaming via the PutRecord API (Streaming Ingestion API) for writing individual records in real time from Kafka/Kinesis, and Batching via Data Wrangler/Spark Container. Since this is writing individual records (single-record) in real time from Kafka, the correct answer is streaming ingestion with the PutRecord API (A). Data Wrangler (B) and the Spark container (C) are batch, used for bulk/backfill rather than live single-record. Offline Store Query (D) is used to query/serve features, not to ingest.

question: A machine learning team must load several years of historical customer data into SageMaker Feature Store in bulk to backfill features for model training. Which ingestion method is most appropriate?

options.text: Streaming ingestion via the PutRecord API
Batch ingestion via Data Wrangler or a Spark container
The Serving API to push records into the Online Store

explanation: The notes state clearly that Batch ingestion (via Data Wrangler/Spark) is used to backfill historical data, in bulk. Since the requirement is loading bulk historical data for backfill, B is correct. Streaming/PutRecord (A) and single-record real-time from Kinesis (C) are for live streaming and don't fit bulk loading. Serving API (D) is used to serve features to the ML Model, not to ingest data.

question: A company relies on SageMaker Feature Store to guarantee that features are trustworthy before they are stored. What does Feature Store do at ingestion time to help ensure data quality? (Choose TWO.)

options.text: Validates that input data conforms to the defined data types
Validates that the input record contains all features
Automatically retrains the ML model when data drifts
Encrypts each feature value with a customer-managed key
Removes duplicate records from the Offline Store

explanation: The notes state that Feature Store performs data and schema validations at ingestion time: checking the input data matches the defined data types (A) and that the input record contains all the required features (B). These are two independently correct facts stated directly. Options C (auto-retrain on drift), D (encryption with a CMK), and E (deduplicating records in the Offline Store) aren't in the notes.

question: A data scientist observes that after writing a feature record to the SageMaker Feature Store Online Store, the same record becomes available in the Offline Store without any additional pipeline being built. Which Feature Store capability explains this behavior?

options.text: Automatic replication between the Online Store and Offline Store
The PutRecord API copying data on each call
Data Wrangler scheduling a nightly batch job
The Offline Store Query writing back to the Online Store

explanation: The notes state that Feature Store consists of an Online Store and Offline Store with automatic replication between the two stores, so data syncs automatically without needing a separate pipeline (A). PutRecord (B) is the streaming ingestion API, not the mechanism for replicating between the two stores. Data Wrangler batch (C) is a bulk ingestion method, not an automatic sync mechanism. Offline Store Query (D) is used to query/serve features, and doesn't write back into the Online Store.

question: A machine learning team deploys a real-time recommendation API that must look up the most recent value of a customer feature with very low latency for each inference request. Which SageMaker Feature Store store type should they use?

options.text: Offline store, because it stores the full feature history
Online store, because it provides low-latency lookup of the latest record for real-time inference
Offline store, because it is backed by Amazon S3
Online store, because it is optimized for batch inference over historical data

explanation: According to the notes, the Online store provides low-latency lookup of the single most recent record for real-time inference — matching the requirement for low latency and the latest value. A and C are wrong because the Offline store is used for training/batch and stores the full history on S3, not real-time. D is wrong because the Online store serves real-time inference, not batch inference.

question: A data science team needs to store the complete history of feature values in Amazon S3 to support model training and batch inference jobs. Which SageMaker Feature Store store type meets this requirement?
explanation: The notes state clearly that the Offline store stores the full history on S3, used for training and batch inference. A is wrong because the Online store only keeps the single latest record for real-time inference. C and D aren't store types mentioned in the notes (only Online and Offline exist).

question: An organization notices that a model performs worse in production than during testing because the features computed at serving time differ from those used during training. How does SageMaker Feature Store help prevent this problem?

options.text: It automatically retrains the model whenever production data drifts
It authors feature processing logic once and uses the same features for both training and inference, reducing training-serving skew
It increases the online store latency to match the offline store
It stores features only for inference so training uses raw data

explanation: This is exactly the main reason to use a Feature Store: feature-processing logic is written once and reused for both training and inference, thereby avoiding training-serving skew (where a feature differs between training and serving, hurting the model). A is wrong because the notes don't mention auto-retraining on drift. C is nonsensical and not in the notes. D is wrong because features are used for both training and inference, not just inference.

question: Which of the following are stated benefits of using SageMaker Feature Store for ML development? (Choose TWO.)

options.text: It provides a centralized store for features and associated metadata so features can be easily discovered and reused
It reduces repetitive data processing and curation work needed to convert raw data into features
It automatically labels raw training data using human reviewers
It guarantees the lowest inference cost across all AWS services
It replaces the need for any model training

explanation: The notes state that Feature Store is a centralized store for features and related metadata for easy discovery and reuse (A), and it reduces repetitive data-processing and curation work needed to turn raw data into features (B). C is wrong because that's a function of a data-labeling service, not in the notes. D and E are claims not mentioned in the notes at all.

question: A team wants to understand the primary value proposition of SageMaker Feature Store for data scientists and ML engineers. Which statement best describes it?

options.text: It makes it easy to create, share, and manage features for ML development
It is a managed service exclusively for deploying model endpoints
It provides a distributed compute cluster for training deep learning models
It is a data warehouse used only for business intelligence reporting

explanation: According to the notes, Feature Store helps data scientists, ML engineers, and practitioners easily create, share, and manage features for ML development — this is the core value description. B, C, D all describe other types of services (deploying an endpoint, a training compute cluster, a BI data warehouse) that don't reflect Feature Store's function as stated in the notes.

question: A data scientist has finished preparing a dataset in Amazon SageMaker Data Wrangler and now wants to import the transformed features into SageMaker Feature Store. Within the Data Wrangler data flow, what should the data scientist do to accomplish this?

options.text: Add an Export Step under the Export tab of the data flow
Add a Transform Step under the Analysis tab of the data flow
Enable the Feature Store integration from the Import tab of the data flow
Attach an inference endpoint under the Deploy tab of the data flow

explanation: According to the notes, to import data into Feature Store from Data Wrangler, you add an Export Step under the Export tab of the data flow, so A is correct. B is wrong because this is an Export, not a Transform, and it's not under the Analysis tab. C is wrong because sending data to Feature Store is done via the Export tab, not the Import tab. D is wrong because the notes don't mention attaching an inference endpoint or a Deploy tab.

question: A machine learning team selects the Feature Store option when adding an Export Step in SageMaker Data Wrangler. What is the direct result of choosing this export option?

options.text: It provisions a real-time inference endpoint that serves the prepared features
It generates a Jupyter Notebook that creates a Feature Store feature group and adds features to an offline or online store
It automatically retrains the model using the newly prepared features
It exports the flow as reusable Python Code that only runs on a schedule

explanation: According to the notes, the Feature Store export option generates a Jupyter Notebook, and this notebook creates a Feature Store feature group and adds features to the offline or online store, so B is correct. A is wrong because the notes say nothing about creating an inference endpoint. C is wrong because there's no automatic model retraining. D confuses Feature Store with the Python Code option (a separate, different export option).

question: When adding an Export Step in SageMaker Data Wrangler, which of the following are valid export options available to the user? (Choose TWO.)
explanation: According to the notes, the export options include Processing Job, Pipeline, Python Code, and Feature Store; so A (Processing Job) and D (Pipeline) are both valid options. B (Ground Truth labeling job), C (Model Registry), and E (CloudFormation template) aren't listed in the notes, so they are distractor options.

question: A data engineering team wants to push real-time events from Apache Kafka, Amazon Kinesis, and Spark Streaming into Amazon SageMaker Feature Store for low-latency serving. Which API should they use to ingest these streaming records?
explanation: Theo notes, du lieu duoc day vao mot stream va duoc ingest thong qua PutRecord API, va API nay duoc goi boi cac nguon nhu Apache Kafka, Amazon Kinesis, Spark Streaming. Vi vay A dung. Cac phuong an B, C, D khong duoc de cap trong notes nhu la co che ingest streaming; chung khong phai la API dung de day record vao stream nen deu sai.

question: An ML team ingests feature data using the streaming PutRecord API. According to how Feature Store handles PUT requests, what happens to the data immediately after ingestion?

options.text: It is written asynchronously to the offline store first, then copied to the online store
It is synchronously written to the online store, and optionally buffered and written to the offline store (S3) within a few minutes
It is written only to the offline store (S3) and never made available online
It is queued in Kafka until a nightly batch job loads it into both stores

explanation: Notes neu ro: du lieu tu PUT request duoc ghi dong bo (synchronously) vao online store, va (tuy chon) duoc buffer roi ghi vao offline store (S3) trong vong vai phut sau khi ingest. Do do B dung. A sai vi thu tu nguoc lai (online store la noi ghi dong bo truoc). C sai vi du lieu van duoc ghi vao online store. D sai vi khong co co che nightly batch qua Kafka nao duoc mo ta.

question: A company needs a streaming ingestion endpoint for its feature pipeline that can serve features for real-time inference. Which characteristics describe the PutRecord ingestion endpoint? (Choose TWO.)

options.text: It is designed for millisecond-level latency
It guarantees exactly-once delivery across all AWS Regions
It requires disabling the online store to function
It can only be called from a single source at a time

explanation: Notes mo ta endpoint duoc thiet ke cho millisecond-level latency va high-throughput cost-optimized ingestion, nen A va B dung. C sai vi notes khong de cap bao dam exactly-once da Region. D sai vi online store van duoc ghi dong bo, khong bi disable. E sai vi endpoint co the duoc goi boi nhieu nguon khac nhau (Kafka, Kinesis, Spark Streaming).

question: A team is concerned about scaling write throughput for their streaming feature ingestion and about retaining ingested data for future model training and historical record access. Which statements are supported by the Feature Store streaming ingestion design?

options.text: The PutRecord API can be parallelized for higher-throughput writes, and Feature Store automatically replicates ingested data into the offline store for future training and historical access
The PutRecord API cannot be parallelized, so throughput is fixed regardless of load
Data must be manually exported from the online store to enable any future training use
Feature Store replicates data into the offline store only when the online store is deleted

explanation: Notes noi PutRecord API co the duoc parallelized de ghi throughput cao hon, va Feature Store cung cap automatic replication du lieu da ingest vao offline store phuc vu training/historical record access. Do do A dung. B sai vi API co the parallelized. C sai vi viec replication vao offline store la tu dong, khong can export thu cong. D sai vi replication dien ra tu dong sau khi ingest (trong vai phut), khong phu thuoc vao viec xoa online store.

question: An application needs to retrieve the most recent feature values for a specific record identifier with very low latency to serve a real-time inference request. Which SageMaker Feature Store Runtime API call should the team use?

options.text: GetRecord, which reads only the latest record from the OnlineStore
PutRecord, which reads the latest record from the OfflineStore
DeleteRecord, which returns the latest record before removing it
GetRecord, which scans all historic records in the OfflineStore

explanation: According to the notes, GetRecord only reads the latest record stored in the OnlineStore, suited for fast reads for real-time inference. B is wrong because PutRecord is a write API, not a read, and the OfflineStore isn't used for fast reads. C is wrong because DeleteRecord is used to delete a record, not to read. D is wrong because GetRecord only reads the OnlineStore (latest), and doesn't scan historic records in the OfflineStore.

question: A data engineer calls PutRecord to write a feature record whose timestamp corresponds to the latest event for its record identifier. According to how SageMaker Feature Store Runtime handles this, where is the record written?

options.text: To both the OnlineStore and the OfflineStore
To neither store until a GetRecord call confirms it

explanation: The notes state clearly that PutRecord writes to BOTH the OnlineStore and OfflineStore; when it's the latest record for an identifier, it's written to both. A and B are wrong because the latest record isn't written to only one store. D is wrong because PutRecord writes directly and doesn't depend on GetRecord.

question: A team uses PutRecord to ingest a feature record whose event timestamp is older than the current latest record for the same record identifier (a historic record). Where does the SageMaker Feature Store Runtime write this historic record?

options.text: To both the OnlineStore and the OfflineStore
It is rejected because historic records cannot be ingested

explanation: According to the notes, with PutRecord, if it's a historic record, it's only written to the OfflineStore (preserving history). A and C are wrong because a historic record doesn't update the OnlineStore (which only holds the latest). D is wrong because a historic record is still accepted and stored in the OfflineStore, not rejected.

question: An engineer calls DeleteRecord on a record in a FeatureGroup. What is the resulting behavior in the OfflineStore?

options.text: A new record appears in the OfflineStore with is_deleted set to True
The record is permanently purged from both the OnlineStore and OfflineStore with no trace
An empty record is returned because no RecordIdentifierValue is found
The record is moved from the OfflineStore to the OnlineStore

explanation: The notes state that DeleteRecord creates a new record in the OfflineStore with is_deleted = True (a soft delete that preserves the historical trace). B is wrong because the OfflineStore still keeps a record marked is_deleted, it isn't wiped without a trace. C describes the behavior of GetRecord when an identifier isn't found, not DeleteRecord. D is wrong because there's no moving of records between the two stores.

question: A data science team has created a feature group in Amazon SageMaker Feature Store using the offline store. They now want to build a training dataset by running ad hoc SQL queries against these features. Which AWS service enables them to query the offline feature store data directly?

options.text: Amazon Athena querying an AWS Glue Data Catalog

explanation: According to the notes, after creating a feature group in the offline feature store, you can run queries using Amazon Athena on an AWS Glue catalog. So A is correct. B (Kinesis Data Analytics), C (QuickSight), and D (Redshift Spectrum) aren't mentioned in the notes as ways to query the offline feature store.

question: An ML engineer needs to understand how data flows when querying an offline SageMaker Feature Store with SQL. Which sequence correctly describes the data flow?

options.text: Feature Store → Athena → Glue Data Catalog → S3 Bucket
Feature Store → Glue Data Catalog → Athena → S3 Bucket
S3 Bucket → Feature Store → Athena → Glue Data Catalog
Athena → Feature Store → S3 Bucket → Glue Data Catalog

explanation: The notes state clearly the flow: Feature Store → Glue Data Catalog → Athena → S3 Bucket, so B is correct. A swaps the positions of Athena and Glue Data Catalog. C and D order the components incorrectly compared to the flow described in the notes.

question: A team wants to run Amazon Athena queries against their newly created offline feature group. They are concerned they will need to manually configure a data catalog before querying. What is true about the data catalog requirement for this workflow?

options.text: The catalog details are auto-registered in Feature Store, so no manual registration is required.
The team must manually create and register the AWS Glue Data Catalog before running any query.
Athena does not require any data catalog to query the offline feature store.
The catalog must be registered manually in Amazon S3 before Athena can access it.

explanation: The notes state that the workflow requires data to be registered in a data catalog, but the catalog details are auto-registered within Feature Store. So A is correct. B is wrong because manual registration isn't needed. C is wrong because a data catalog is still required. D is wrong because the catalog isn't manually registered in S3.

question: A data scientist wants to retrieve a sample of rows from an offline feature group table using Amazon Athena so they can build a dataset for model training. Which SQL statement matches the query pattern for this workflow?
explanation: The notes give the standard query example: SELECT * FROM <DatabaseName>.<TableName> LIMIT 1000, which is valid SQL syntax used by Athena, so B is correct. A, C, D are invalid syntax and don't appear in the notes.

question: A machine learning team has deployed a trained model to a real-time SageMaker endpoint. Their application now needs to send data and receive predictions from the hosted model. Which SageMaker endpoint should the application call?
explanation: According to the notes, the region-specific endpoint runtime.sagemaker.<region>.amazonaws.com is used to send inference requests (data plane) to a model hosted in SageMaker — 'runtime' = predictions. A (api.sagemaker...) is the control plane used to create/train/manage, not to get predictions. C and D are endpoints that don't exist in the notes.

question: An ML engineer is writing scripts to create training jobs, manage notebook instances, and set up endpoint configurations in Amazon SageMaker. According to the SageMaker endpoint structure, which endpoint category do these control-plane operations use?

options.text: The region-specific endpoint (runtime.sagemaker...), because it handles all model lifecycle operations
The FIPS runtime endpoint (runtime-fips.sagemaker...), because management calls require encryption
The Service Endpoint (api.sagemaker...), which is used for training, deploying, and managing resources
A public S3 endpoint, because SageMaker metadata is stored in S3

explanation: The notes state clearly that the Service Endpoint api.sagemaker.<region>.amazonaws.com is the control plane, used for training/deploying models, creating and managing notebook instances, and endpoint configurations. A and B are wrong because runtime endpoints are the data plane used for inference, not for managing resources. D isn't in the notes.

question: A security reviewer is auditing how client applications communicate with Amazon SageMaker service and region-specific endpoints. Which statement about the transport protocol accepted by these endpoints is correct?

options.text: Both the Service Endpoint and the region-specific endpoint accept HTTPS requests
Only the region-specific endpoint accepts HTTPS; the Service Endpoint uses plain HTTP
Both endpoints require SSH tunneling rather than HTTPS
Both endpoints accept only unencrypted HTTP requests

explanation: The notes state that both Service Endpoints and Region-specific endpoints accept HTTPS requests. So A is correct. B is wrong because both use HTTPS, not just the region-specific one. C (SSH) and D (unencrypted HTTP) are neither mentioned and both contradict the notes.

question: A data scientist is working in an Amazon SageMaker notebook and wants to interact with SageMaker to run training jobs, create endpoints, and manage input datasets without writing raw boto3 API calls. Which approach provides these convenient high-level methods?

options.text: Use the SageMaker Python SDK and instantiate a sagemaker.Session() object
Call the low-level boto3 SageMaker client directly for every operation
Use the AWS CLI exclusively from the notebook terminal
Write custom REST calls to the SageMaker service endpoint

explanation: According to the notes, the SageMaker Python SDK is a high-level, open-source Python library that hides raw boto3 calls, and is the typical way to interact with SageMaker from a notebook. The Session object provides convenience methods for handling training jobs, endpoints, and input datasets. B is wrong because raw boto3 is exactly what the SDK helps hide. C and D aren't mentioned and aren't the approach described in the notes.

question: An ML engineer needs the name of the default Amazon S3 bucket that SageMaker uses for its interactions so that training artifacts can be stored there. Which SageMaker Session method returns this value?
explanation: According to the notes, default_bucket returns the name of the default bucket for SageMaker's interactions (e.g. bucket = session.default_bucket()). A (list_s3_files) only lists files in a bucket/key. B (read_s3_file) reads a single file from S3. D (upload_data) uploads local files/folders to S3 and doesn't return a bucket name.

question: A team is using the SageMaker Python SDK. They want to launch a training job and then deploy the resulting model to an endpoint. Which SDK object wraps a training job (algorithm plus instance configuration) and exposes .fit() to train and .deploy() to create an endpoint?
explanation: According to the notes, an Estimator is an SDK object that wraps a training job (algorithm + instance configuration); .fit() trains and .deploy() creates an endpoint. A (Session) provides convenience methods but isn't the object that wraps a training job with .fit()/.deploy(). C (Processor) and D (Predictor) aren't mentioned in the notes as playing this role.

question: A developer has generated a report as an in-memory Python string and separately has a local directory of files. Using the SageMaker Session, which methods should they use to (1) upload the string directly as a file body to S3 and (2) upload the local directory to S3? (Choose TWO.)
explanation: According to the notes, upload_string_as_file_body uploads a string as a file body, and upload_data uploads local files/folders to S3 — these are two independently correct methods. C (download_data) downloads rather than uploads. D (read_s3_file) reads a single file from S3. E (list_s3_files) only lists files, doesn't upload.

question: A data scientist wants to let SageMaker automatically explore algorithms and hyperparameters to build a candidate model from a dataset, using the SageMaker Session. Which method creates this type of job?
explanation: According to the notes, auto_ml creates an AutoML job — a job for SageMaker to automatically discover and build a model. A (process) creates a processing job. B (compile_model) creates a SageMaker Neo compilation job to compile a model. C (tune / create_tuning_job) creates a hyperparameter tuning job — tuning hyperparameters for an already-chosen algorithm, not automatically choosing the algorithm like AutoML.

question: A machine learning team is setting up a training job with the SageMaker Python SDK. They want the simplest and default source for supplying their training data to the estimator. Which data source should they use?

options.text: An Amazon S3 path pointing to the training data
An Amazon EFS file system mounted to the training instance
An Amazon FSx for Lustre file system mounted to the training instance
A local directory on the developer's workstation

explanation: According to the notes, S3 is the default and simplest data source ("S3 is the default/simplest source"). So A is correct. B (EFS) and C (FSx Lustre) should only be used when you need high-throughput, low-latency access for a large, repeatedly reused dataset, not the default choice. D isn't mentioned in the notes as a training data source.

question: A deep learning team runs many HPC-style training jobs that repeatedly reuse a very large dataset and requires the fastest possible file system throughput mounted to the training instances. Which data source should they choose?

options.text: Amazon S3 with the data referenced by an S3 path
Amazon FSx for Lustre mounted to the EC2 instance running the training job
Amazon EFS mounted with a read-write access mode
A separate S3 bucket per training job to increase parallelism

explanation: The notes state clearly that FSx Lustre is the fastest for HPC/DL ("FSx Lustre = fastest for HPC/DL") and fits when you need high throughput, low latency with a large, repeatedly reused dataset. So B is correct. A (S3) is the default/simplest source but not the fastest for HPC/DL. C (EFS) is a valid mount option but the notes specify FSx Lustre as the fastest, not EFS. D isn't in the notes and is unrelated to the file-system throughput requirement.

question: A data scientist has mounted a file system to the training instance and needs to reference it as the input source for a SageMaker training job. Which class from the SageMaker Python SDK is appropriate for referencing a mounted file system?
explanation: The notes indicate using `FileSystemInput` (or `FileSystemRecordSet`) to reference an already-mounted file system. Among the options, only C matches, so C is correct. A, B, D aren't mentioned in the notes as the class used to reference a mounted file system.

question: An engineer is configuring a TensorFlow estimator to train against data on a mounted EFS file system. Which parameters and steps are consistent with the SageMaker Python SDK workflow described for using a mounted file system? (Choose TWO.)

options.text: Create a FileSystemInput specifying file_system_id, file_system_type='EFS', directory_path, and file_system_access_mode='ro'
Start the training job by calling estimator.fit(file_system_input)
Set the estimator's data_location parameter to the EFS DNS name
Pass the EFS mount as the entry_point argument of the estimator

explanation: According to the example in the notes: create `FileSystemInput(file_system_id, file_system_type='EFS', directory_path, file_system_access_mode='ro')` (A correct) then start with `estimator.fit(file_system_input)` (B correct). C is wrong because the notes don't have a data_location parameter. D is wrong because entry_point is the training script, not the EFS mount. E is wrong because `deploy()` is used to deploy an inference endpoint, while training is started with `fit()`.

question: A data scientist is using the SageMaker Python SDK to train a custom model. They pass three separate input data sources named train, test, and validation to the training job. Which term describes each of these named input data sources?
explanation: According to the notes, each named input data source passed to a training job is called a 'channel' (e.g. train, test, validation), letting the algorithm read each portion of data separately. B is wrong because a hyperparameter is a configuration parameter, not a data source. C is wrong because the model directory is where the output model is stored. D (endpoint variant) doesn't appear in the notes and relates to inference, not training input.

question: A training script running in SageMaker needs to locate the input data for the training split at runtime. According to how SageMaker exposes channels to the script, which environment variable should the script read?
explanation: The notes state clearly that each channel is exposed to the script via an environment variable of the form SM_CHANNEL_<NAME>, so for the channel 'train' it's SM_CHANNEL_TRAIN. A (SM_MODEL_DIR) points to the directory for storing the output model, not training data. B (SM_HPS) contains hyperparameters, not data. D (SM_CHANNEL_TEST) points to test data, not train.

question: A developer wants their SageMaker training script to receive hyperparameters that the client sends when starting the training job. Based on the SDK's behavior, which TWO mechanisms can the script use to access those hyperparameters? (Choose TWO.)

options.text: Read them as command-line arguments parsed with argparse
Load them from the SM_HPS environment variable
Read them from the SM_CHANNEL_TRAIN environment variable
Read them from the SM_MODEL_DIR environment variable
Retrieve them from an S3 validation channel

explanation: The notes describe two independent methods: hyperparameters from the client are passed as command-line arguments (using argparse), or loaded via the SM_HPS environment variable. So A and B are correct. C is wrong because SM_CHANNEL_TRAIN is used for the training data location. D is wrong because SM_MODEL_DIR is the directory for storing the model. E isn't in the notes, and channels are used for data, not hyperparameters.

question: A team splits their dataset into logical channels so that their algorithm can read each split separately during a SageMaker training job. What is the primary benefit of partitioning training data into named channels?

options.text: It lets the algorithm read each data split separately through its own named input source
It automatically tunes the model's hyperparameters for each split
It deploys each split to a separate inference endpoint
It encrypts each split with a different KMS key

explanation: According to the notes, splitting data into named channels lets the algorithm read each split separately through its own dedicated input source. B is wrong because a channel is unrelated to tuning hyperparameters. C is wrong because a channel is used for training input, not for deploying an inference endpoint. D (KMS encryption) isn't mentioned in the notes.

question: An ML engineer is preparing to train a custom model using the SageMaker Python SDK. Which sequence of steps correctly describes the training workflow?

options.text: Create an estimator, prepare a training script, then deploy an endpoint
Prepare a training script, create an estimator, then call the estimator's fit method
Upload data to S3, call fit, then write the training script
Create an estimator, call fit, then prepare a training script

explanation: The notes state clearly that the training process with the SDK has 3 steps in order: (1) prepare a training script, (2) create an estimator, (3) call the fit method. So B is correct. A is wrong because it reorders and adds a deploy-endpoint step (not part of the training process described in the notes). C and D are wrong because they order the steps incorrectly — you can't call fit before you have a script.

question: A training script must save model artifacts so that they can later be used for model hosting. Which environment variable provides the path where the script should write these artifacts, and where do they go afterward?

options.text: SM_CHANNEL_TRAIN, and the artifacts remain on the training host
SM_NUM_GPUS, and the artifacts are uploaded to S3
SM_HPS, and the artifacts are written to a JSON file
SM_MODEL_DIR, and the artifacts are uploaded to S3

explanation: According to the notes, SM_MODEL_DIR is the path where the training job writes model artifacts; after training completes, these artifacts are uploaded to S3 to serve model hosting. So D is correct. A is wrong because SM_CHANNEL_TRAIN is the input data path for the 'train' channel, not where artifacts are written. B is wrong because SM_NUM_GPUS is just the GPU count. C is wrong because SM_HPS is the JSON dump of hyperparameters.

question: A training job defines two input data channels named 'train' and 'test'. How does the training script locate the directories containing the input data for these channels?

options.text: By reading SM_CHANNEL_TRAIN and SM_CHANNEL_TEST, which SageMaker sets to the input data paths
By reading SM_MODEL_DIR for both channels
By parsing SM_HPS, which contains the data paths as JSON
By hardcoding the S3 bucket paths directly in the script

explanation: The notes state that SM_CHANNEL_XXXX is the path to the directory containing input data for a channel; with two channels 'train'/'test' there will be SM_CHANNEL_TRAIN and SM_CHANNEL_TEST. So A is correct. B is wrong because SM_MODEL_DIR is used to write output artifacts. C is wrong because SM_HPS contains hyperparameters, not data paths. D is wrong because the purpose of these env vars is so the script can find data without hardcoding paths.

question: A developer wants the training script to receive hyperparameters (epochs, batch-size, learning-rate) as command-line arguments, and also needs a JSON representation of all hyperparameters that preserves their original types such as boolean and integer. Which two mechanisms described for the training script support these needs? (Choose TWO.)

options.text: Use argparse in the script to read hyperparameters such as epochs, batch-size, and learning-rate
Read SM_HPS, a JSON dump of the hyperparameters that preserves JSON types
Read SM_NUM_GPUS to obtain the hyperparameter values
Read SM_MODEL_DIR to load the hyperparameters as JSON
Read SM_CHANNEL_TRAIN to parse hyperparameters from the input data

explanation: The notes state that the script uses argparse to read hyperparameters (epochs, batch-size, learning-rate) — so A is correct. SM_HPS is a JSON dump of hyperparameters, preserving JSON types (boolean, integer, etc.) — so B is correct. C is wrong because SM_NUM_GPUS is just the GPU count. D is wrong because SM_MODEL_DIR is where model artifacts are written. E is wrong because SM_CHANNEL_TRAIN is the input data path and doesn't contain hyperparameters.

question: A training script needs to know how many GPUs are available on the host so it can configure distributed computation appropriately. Which environment variable provides this information, and what is its value type?

options.text: SM_NUM_GPUS, provided as an integer count of GPUs available to the host
SM_HPS, provided as a JSON dump containing the GPU count
SM_MODEL_DIR, provided as a path string to the GPU devices
SM_CHANNEL_GPU, provided as a directory path to GPU data

explanation: According to the notes, SM_NUM_GPUS is an integer counting the number of GPUs available on the host, matching A's description. B is wrong because SM_HPS contains hyperparameters. C is wrong because SM_MODEL_DIR is the path for writing model artifacts. D is wrong because there's no SM_CHANNEL_GPU variable; SM_CHANNEL_XXXX is used for input data channels.

question: A machine learning engineer wants to test and debug a deep learning training script quickly and cheaply before committing to paid managed SageMaker training instances. Which SageMaker Python SDK capability lets them run training and inference in a Docker container on their own machine?

options.text: Local mode, by setting the instance type to "local"
Managed spot training with a reduced instance count
A serverless inference endpoint with provisioned concurrency

explanation: The notes state clearly that Local Mode runs training/inference in a Docker container on your machine with instance_type="local" for fast, cheap debugging before paying for managed instances. B (managed spot training) still uses managed instances, not running locally. C (Autopilot) and D (serverless endpoint) aren't mentioned and aren't the mechanism for running locally on the user's machine.

question: A team is configuring SageMaker local mode globally by creating a ~/.sagemaker/config.yaml file that enables "local code". Which limitation applies once local code is enabled?

options.text: The estimator can no longer use the dependencies parameter
AWS credentials are no longer required
docker-compose must be uninstalled to avoid conflicts

explanation: The notes say that when "local code" is enabled, you can't use the dependencies parameter in the estimator/model. B is wrong because local mode supports multiple frameworks (TensorFlow, MXNet, Chainer, PyTorch, Scikit-Learn, custom images). C is wrong because credentials are still always needed. D is wrong because docker-compose is required to be installed to use local mode, not removed.

question: A developer sets up local mode using LocalSession instead of a config.yaml file. Which action correctly enables local code with this approach?

options.text: Set sagemaker_session.config = {'local': {'local_code': True}} and pass the session to the estimator
Add local_code: true under a training block in ~/.aws/config
Pass local_code=True as an argument to the fit() method
Export LOCAL_CODE=true as an environment variable before importing sagemaker

explanation: The notes describe how to use a local session: import LocalSession, set sagemaker_session.config = {'local': {'local_code': True}}, then pass the session into the estimator/model. B, C, D aren't mentioned in the notes and don't match the configuration approach described.

question: An engineer is preparing an environment to run SageMaker local mode. According to the requirements and caveats, which two statements are true? (Choose TWO.)

options.text: AWS credentials are still required so the SDK can send to S3
docker-compose must be installed to use local mode
Local mode is fully supported and recommended for production training
Local mode runs natively without Docker on all operating systems
Local mode is stable and fully supported on Windows

explanation: The notes state clearly that AWS credentials are still needed (even if you don't want to use S3), and docker-compose must be installed. C is wrong because local mode is for testing/debugging, not for production training. D is wrong because local mode runs in a Docker container. E is wrong because local mode is only experimental-level on Windows.

question: A data scientist wants to verify a TensorFlow training script locally before running it on managed instances. Which estimator configuration ensures the job runs on a local instance?
explanation: The notes give an example estimator using train_instance_type="local", train_instance_count=1, script_mode=True to ensure training happens on a local instance. A uses the managed instance type ml.p3.2xlarge, so it isn't local. C doesn't match the example (count=4, script_mode=False). D uses the endpoint_type parameter, which isn't in the notes.

question: A machine learning team wants a high-level, ML-purpose-built Python library that provides abstractions such as Estimators and Predictors to streamline training and deploying models on Amazon SageMaker. Which option best fits this requirement?

options.text: The boto3 AWS SDK, because it is the low-level generic API for every AWS service
The SageMaker Python SDK, because it is high-level and purpose-built for ML workflows
The AWS CLI, because it exposes raw API control across all services
The boto3 AWS SDK, because it integrates specifically with SageMaker ML frameworks

explanation: According to the notes, the SageMaker Python SDK is a high-level library, purpose-built for ML (providing Estimators/Predictors) for training and deploying models on SageMaker. A and D are wrong because boto3 is low-level, generic across all services rather than purpose-built for ML; D also mischaracterizes it (that's a feature of the SageMaker SDK, not boto3). C isn't mentioned in the notes.

question: A developer needs to perform real-time inference and transformation using native Python data types against a deployed SageMaker endpoint. Which SageMaker Python SDK high-level abstraction should the developer use?
explanation: The notes define Predictors as an abstraction that performs real-time inference & transformation using Python data types against a SageMaker endpoint. Estimators are used to encapsulate training (choosing the best model), not inference. Transformers encapsulate batch transform jobs (not real-time). Processors encapsulate processing jobs for data processing.

question: A data science team runs large offline scoring jobs where an entire dataset is processed at once rather than served through a live endpoint. Which SageMaker Python SDK abstraction encapsulates these batch transform jobs for inference?
explanation: According to the notes, Transformers encapsulate batch transform jobs for inference — matching the scenario of processing an entire dataset offline. Predictors are for real-time inference via an endpoint. Session is a collection of methods for working with SageMaker resources. Models encapsulate built ML models, not running a batch job.

question: An ML engineer is choosing between the SageMaker Python SDK and boto3 for a project. Which statements accurately describe the distinction as noted? (Choose TWO.)

options.text: The SageMaker Python SDK is a high-level, ML-purpose-built library integrating specifically with SageMaker and specific ML frameworks/tools
boto3 is the high-level ML library that provides Estimators and Predictors
boto3 is a low-level, generic AWS SDK that broadly interacts with every AWS service
The SageMaker Python SDK replaces boto3 for all AWS service interactions
boto3 can only be used with SageMaker and no other AWS service

explanation: The notes state clearly: the SageMaker Python SDK is high-level, ML-purpose-built, integrated specifically with SageMaker and ML frameworks/tools (A correct); while boto3 is low-level, generic, interacting broadly with every AWS service (C correct). B is wrong because Estimators/Predictors belong to the SageMaker SDK, not boto3. D is wrong because the SDK doesn't replace boto3 for every service (boto3 is the generic one for all services). E is wrong because boto3 interacts with every service, not limited to SageMaker.

question: A team wants to bring their own trained model and secure both training and inference within a VPC while using the SageMaker Python SDK. According to the SDK's supported tools, which capabilities directly address these needs?

options.text: BYO Model and Secure Training and Inference with VPC

explanation: Under the Tools section, the notes list exactly two capabilities: BYO Model and Secure Training and Inference with VPC — directly matching the requirement of bringing your own model and securing training/inference within a VPC. A: XGBoost is an ML Algorithm and Model Monitoring is a SageMaker feature, not 'Tools'. C and D are both SageMaker features listed separately, not part of the Tools section, and aren't directly related to BYO model/VPC security.

question: A machine learning team has collected thousands of raw, unlabeled images stored in Amazon S3. Before they can train a supervised image classification model, they need to produce an accurate, labeled training dataset. Which AWS service is designed for this task?
explanation: According to the notes, Amazon SageMaker Ground Truth is a fully managed data-labeling service that helps build an accurate training dataset BEFORE training (offline, pre-training), matching the need to label raw data. A2I is wrong because A2I is used for humans to review a model's PREDICTIONS in production (post-inference), not to label a training set. Model Monitor and Feature Store aren't mentioned and don't serve the purpose of data labeling.

question: A company is building a training dataset from a very large collection of unlabeled records and wants to reduce the cost of human labeling without sacrificing accuracy. How does the automated data labeling feature of SageMaker Ground Truth help achieve this?

options.text: It performs a full statistical analysis of the dataset and removes any records that are difficult to label.
After enough human labels are collected, it trains a model to automatically label the easy items and only sends the ambiguous ones to human labelers.
It sends every record to multiple human labelers and uses majority voting to guarantee accuracy.
It reviews the predictions of the deployed production model and routes low-confidence results to human reviewers.

explanation: The notes state clearly that once enough labels from humans exist, Ground Truth trains a model to automatically label the easy items and only sends ambiguous items to humans, reducing cost on large datasets. A is wrong because Ground Truth doesn't discard difficult records. C is wrong because it doesn't send every record to multiple people and vote (that wouldn't reduce cost). D actually describes A2I (reviewing predictions post-inference), not Ground Truth's automated data labeling.

question: During exam preparation, an ML practitioner needs to distinguish between SageMaker Ground Truth and Amazon Augmented AI (A2I). Which statement correctly describes the difference?

options.text: Ground Truth reviews model predictions in production, while A2I labels the training dataset before training.
Both services are used exclusively to review model predictions after inference.
Ground Truth builds and labels a training dataset offline before training, while A2I provides human review of model predictions in production after inference.
Ground Truth and A2I both build training datasets, but Ground Truth uses only automated labeling with no human workforce.

explanation: According to the Exam note in the notes: Ground Truth = building/labeling a TRAINING dataset (offline, pre-training), while A2I (Augmented AI) = human review of a model's PREDICTIONS in production (post-inference). So C is correct. A reverses the roles of the two services. B is wrong because Ground Truth isn't used to review predictions. D is wrong because Ground Truth combines human labelers (workforce) with optional automated labeling, not automated labeling alone.

question: A data engineer is configuring a labeling job in SageMaker Ground Truth. Which sequence correctly describes the data flow of the labeling workflow?

options.text: Input manifest file and unlabeled data are stored in an S3 bucket, labeled by a human workforce through the Ground Truth UI, and the labeled output is written back to an S3 bucket.
A deployed model generates predictions, which are then reviewed by a human workforce and stored in an S3 bucket.
Unlabeled data is streamed directly into the Ground Truth UI without any S3 storage, and results are returned to the client application.
An input manifest file triggers automatic model training, which produces both the labels and the final trained model.

explanation: The notes describe the flow: Input Manifest File + Unlabeled Data → S3 Bucket → Ground Truth UI (labeled by a human Workforce) → labeled output written back to the S3 Bucket. So A is correct. B describes the prediction-review process (like A2I), not Ground Truth's flow. C is wrong because data is stored in S3, not streamed directly bypassing S3. D is wrong because Ground Truth produces a labeled dataset and doesn't automatically train a final model in this flow.

question: A data science team is setting up a labeling job in Amazon SageMaker Ground Truth. They want to choose a built-in task template that matches the type of data they need to annotate. Which of the following are valid data-category task templates offered by Ground Truth? (Choose TWO.)
explanation: According to the notes, Ground Truth provides task templates grouped by data type: Image, Text, Video, Point cloud, or Custom. So 'Point cloud' (A) and 'Custom' (C) are both correct. Options 'Tabular' (B), 'Time series' (D), and 'Audio' (E) aren't listed in the notes, so they are distractors.

question: A team selects the Image data category when creating a SageMaker Ground Truth labeling job. They want to describe the job requirements to human annotators without writing custom HTML. Which capability does Ground Truth provide in this scenario?

options.text: A WYSIWYG editor to describe job requirements, including good/bad example guidance
A requirement to hand-code the annotation UI using a Custom template
An automatically generated model that labels the images without human review
A managed workforce that is provisioned only for Point cloud jobs

explanation: According to the notes, when choosing Image/Text/Video/Point cloud, Ground Truth provides an easy-to-use WYSIWYG editor for describing the task requirements, along with good/bad example guidance — matching option A. B is wrong because writing your own UI code only applies to the Custom template, not Image. C is wrong because the notes don't mention automatically generating a labeling model. D is wrong because the WYSIWYG editor applies to all of Image/Text/Video/Point cloud, not just Point cloud.

question: While configuring the WYSIWYG editor for a Text labeling job in SageMaker Ground Truth, a project lead wants to know the maximum number of labels they can define for annotators to choose from. What is the limit?
explanation: According to the notes, the WYSIWYG editor (for Image/Text/Video/Point cloud) supports up to 30 labels, so C is correct. The numbers 10 (A), 20 (B), and 'unlimited' (D) don't match the facts in the notes.

question: A data science team is preparing an image labeling job with Amazon SageMaker Ground Truth. During testing, they notice that some images appear in the wrong orientation for the human labelers. Which action must be taken on the S3 bucket that contains the Ground Truth input data to resolve this?

options.text: Enable a CORS configuration on the S3 bucket so that images can be rotated to the correct orientation
Enable S3 Transfer Acceleration on the bucket to speed up image rendering
Enable S3 Versioning so that a corrected copy of each image is stored
Enable default server-side encryption (SSE-S3) on the bucket

explanation: According to the notes, some browsers display images in the wrong orientation, and CORS must be enabled to rotate images to the correct orientation. So answer A is correct. B (Transfer Acceleration) only speeds up data transfer, unrelated to image orientation. C (Versioning) only stores versions and doesn't fix display orientation. D (encryption) relates to data security, not image orientation. Nothing in the notes supports B, C, or D.

question: An ML engineer creates a labeling job entirely through the Amazon SageMaker Ground Truth console. What is true about the CORS configuration for the input data S3 bucket in this scenario?

options.text: CORS must always be added manually after the job is created, regardless of how the job was created
CORS is enabled by default when the job is created through the Ground Truth console
CORS is not supported for jobs created through the console and must use the API instead
CORS is only required when the bucket is in a different AWS Region than the labeling job

explanation: The notes state clearly: if you create a job through the Ground Truth console, CORS is enabled by default. So B is correct. A is wrong because the console enables it automatically, so no manual step is needed. C is wrong because nothing says the console doesn't support CORS. D is wrong because the notes don't mention any Region-based condition; the CORS requirement stems from browser image-orientation issues, not Region.

question: A company's SageMaker Ground Truth input manifest is stored in one S3 bucket, but the actual image files it references are spread across several other S3 buckets. What must the team do to ensure the labeling job displays images correctly?

options.text: Move the input manifest into each bucket that contains input data
Add a CORS configuration only to the bucket that contains the input manifest
Add a CORS configuration to all S3 buckets that contain the input data
Consolidate all data into a single bucket because Ground Truth cannot reference multiple buckets

explanation: The notes state clearly: if the input data is NOT in the same bucket as the input manifest, you must add CORS configuration to ALL the S3 buckets containing input data. So C is correct. B is wrong because configuring only the bucket containing the manifest isn't enough; CORS is needed on the buckets containing the actual images. A and D are solutions not mentioned in the notes and aren't necessary — Ground Truth can still reference multiple buckets as long as each bucket has CORS.

question: An administrator needs to apply the required CORS configuration to an S3 bucket used by a SageMaker Ground Truth job. Under which section of the S3 bucket settings is this CORS configuration applied?
explanation: According to the notes, CORS configuration is done under the Permissions tab of the S3 bucket, so D is correct. The other tabs (Management, Properties, Objects) are other S3 sections not mentioned in the notes as the place to configure CORS, so A, B, C are all wrong.

question: An autonomous-driving team needs to train a computer vision model that requires precise, pixel-level outlines of every pedestrian, vehicle, and lane in each image so the exact shape of each object is captured. Which SageMaker Ground Truth image labeling task type should they choose?
explanation: Semantic segmentation draws labels at the per-pixel level (a per-pixel mask), accurately capturing an object's shape — matching cases needing high precision like medical imaging or self-driving cars. Bounding box (A) only draws a rough rectangle around an object, not precise to each pixel. Image Classification Single Label (C) only assigns one class to the whole image rather than localizing/outlining an object. Label verification (D) is used to check whether an existing label is correct or not, not to create a shape mask.

question: A retail company wants to build a low-cost dataset that only needs a rough rectangle drawn around each product in an image to indicate approximately where the object is located. Which Ground Truth task type best fits this requirement?
explanation: Bounding box draws a rough rectangle around an object ("where roughly") and is a cheap option, fitting when you only need an approximate location. Semantic segmentation (A) gives a precise per-pixel mask but is more expensive and more than needed. Multi-label (B) and Single Label (D) classify the whole image and don't localize an object's position.

question: A team is labeling photos where each image may contain several different subjects at once (for example, an image tagged as both "beach" and "sunset" and "people"), and they want every applicable category assigned to the image. Which SageMaker Ground Truth task type should they use?
explanation: Image Classification (Multi-label) classifies an image into one or more classes at once — matching cases where an image needs multiple labels (beach, sunset, people). Single Label (A) only assigns exactly one class per image, so it doesn't fit. Bounding box (C) and Semantic segmentation (D) are used to outline/localize objects within an image, not to assign multiple classification labels to the whole image.

question: A data science team already has a labeled image dataset produced by an external vendor, and before training they want human workers to review each existing label and mark whether it is correct or incorrect. Which SageMaker Ground Truth image task type is designed for this purpose?
explanation: Label verification is designed to verify whether existing labels in a dataset are correct or not — matching the need to review labels from an external vendor. Semantic segmentation (B), Single Label (C), and Bounding box (D) are all tasks that create new labels (pixel masks, classification, or rectangles), not for checking the correctness of existing labels.

question: A data science team uses Amazon SageMaker Ground Truth to prepare a labeled dataset from customer support tickets. For each ticket, human labelers must highlight and tag specific spans such as person names, organizations, and locations that appear within the ticket text. Which built-in labeling task type should the team choose?
explanation: Named entity recognition (NER) applies labels to words or phrases (spans) WITHIN a piece of text, e.g. person/place/org — matching the requirement of highlighting and labeling specific spans in a ticket. B and C are Text Classification, labeling the ENTIRE piece of text rather than individual spans within it, so they are wrong. D (semantic segmentation) is for images, not covered in the notes for labeling text.

question: A company wants to use Amazon SageMaker Ground Truth to categorize each incoming product review by assigning exactly one topic (for example, "shipping", "pricing", or "quality") to the entire review. Which labeling task type best fits this requirement?

options.text: Text Classification with named entity spans

explanation: Text Classification (Single Label) classifies an entire piece of text into a SINGLE class — matching the requirement of assigning exactly one topic per review. B (Multi-label) allows assigning one or more classes, which doesn't fit when only one label is needed. A (NER) labels spans within a sentence rather than classifying the whole passage. D is a fabricated option that doesn't exist in the notes.

question: A media company is building a SageMaker Ground Truth workflow where each news article can belong to several categories at once, such as "politics", "economy", and "technology". The labelers must be able to assign one or more categories to the whole article. Which text labeling task type meets this need?
explanation: Text Classification (Multi-label) classifies a piece of text into ONE OR MORE classes — matching the requirement that each article can belong to multiple categories at once. A (Single Label) only allows exactly one class, so it isn't sufficient. C (NER) labels words/phrases within the text, not classifying the whole article. D is a fabricated option, not in the notes.

question: A data scientist is manually creating an input manifest file for a SageMaker Ground Truth labeling job that will annotate a set of product photographs stored as .png files in Amazon S3. Which key must each JSON object in the manifest use to reference the images?

options.text: "source", set to the raw inline content of each image
"source-ref", set to the S3 URI of each image file
"source", set to the S3 URI of each image file
"source-ref", set to the raw inline content of each image

explanation: According to the notes, for binary data (image/video/point cloud) you must use the `source-ref` key pointing to the S3 URI of the file. `source` is only used for inline text data (raw text). So B is correct. A and C are wrong because `source` isn't used for images and isn't inline image content. D is wrong because `source-ref` is an S3 URI, not inline content. Using the wrong key causes a connection/format error.

question: An ML team is labeling a corpus of short customer reviews (plain text) using SageMaker Ground Truth and is building the input manifest by hand. Which JSON object is correctly formatted for this text data?

options.text: {"source": "The product arrived quickly and works great"}
{"source-ref": "The product arrived quickly and works great"}

explanation: According to the notes, for text data, `source` contains the raw text itself (raw inline text), e.g. {"source": "Lorem ipsum dolor sit amet"}. So C is correct. A uses `source-ref` (meant for binary data) for text, so it's wrong. B uses `source` but with an S3 URI instead of raw text, so it's wrong. D uses the wrong key `source-ref` for inline text content.

question: A labeling job in SageMaker Ground Truth fails to load the dataset, and the console does not show "Input data connection successful." Which conditions could cause this connection error? (Choose TWO.)

options.text: CORS is not enabled on the S3 bucket
The S3 bucket is in a different AWS Region than the labeling job
The manifest file uses UTF-8 encoding
Each line of the manifest is delimited by a standard line break
Each JSON object is under 100,000 characters

explanation: According to the notes, a connection error occurs when: CORS isn't enabled on the bucket; the data doesn't exist; the data isn't in the right format; the wrong data type is selected; or the bucket isn't in the same region as the labeling job. So A and B are correct. C (UTF-8), D (standard line breaks), and E (JSON < 100,000 characters) are all VALID REQUIREMENTS the manifest must follow, so they help make the manifest correct rather than cause errors.

question: When SageMaker Ground Truth performs automatic data setup, what does it produce and where is it placed?

options.text: It generates an input manifest file and places it in your S3 bucket
It generates a labeled output dataset and stores it in a managed Ground Truth database
It converts your images to text and stores them locally on the labeling instance
It enables CORS on your bucket and returns the manifest to the console only

explanation: According to the notes, with automatic setup, Ground Truth generates an input manifest file and places it in your bucket (e.g. s3://my-bucket/dataset-...manifest). So A is correct. B is wrong because this is the input manifest, not a labeled dataset, and there's no 'managed database' mentioned. C is entirely wrong and not in the notes. D is wrong because the manifest is stored in an S3 bucket, not just returned to the console.

question: A team is authoring a Ground Truth input manifest programmatically. Which requirement must each entry in the manifest satisfy?

options.text: Each line may span multiple lines using unescaped line breaks for readability
Each line is a JSON object, UTF-8 encoded and delimited by a standard line break
Each JSON object may be up to 200,000 characters with no per-attribute limit
Attribute names must begin with a $ prefix to be recognized

explanation: According to the notes, each line of the manifest is a JSON object, UTF-8 encoded, and each line is separated by a standard line break (\n or \r\n), with no unescaped line breaks. So B is correct. A is wrong because the notes prohibit unescaped line breaks. C is wrong because the limit is ≤ 100,000 characters per JSON object and no attribute may exceed 20,000 characters. D is wrong because attribute names must NOT begin with `$`.

question: A user selects "Complete data setup" in the SageMaker Ground Truth console for a labeling job. What is the purpose of this action?

options.text: To process and complete the input data setup, confirming with "Input data connection successful"
To grant the labeling workforce IAM permissions to the S3 bucket
To convert point cloud files from ASCII to binary pack format automatically
To split the manifest into multiple Regions for redundancy

explanation: According to the notes, the 'Complete data setup' button is used to process/finalize the input data setup, and on success it shows 'Input data connection successful'. So A is correct. B, C, D aren't mentioned in the notes at all: this button doesn't grant IAM permissions to the workforce, doesn't automatically convert point cloud format, and doesn't split the manifest across multiple regions (in fact, the manifest must be in the same region as the data and the labeling job).

question: An autonomous vehicle team is using SageMaker Ground Truth to label LIDAR data. For each individual LIDAR frame, they need to identify vehicles and pedestrians by drawing 3D bounding boxes around each object. Which point cloud labeling task type meets this requirement?
explanation: According to the notes, point cloud object detection identifies objects in LIDAR frames by drawing cuboids (3D boxes) around them — matching the requirement of drawing a 3D bounding box. Object tracking (B) is used to track an object's movement over time, not the goal here. Semantic segmentation (C) colors all pixels associated with a label rather than drawing a box. Image classification (D) isn't a point cloud task mentioned in the notes.

question: A robotics company wants to label sequences of LIDAR frames so they can follow how each detected object moves across the frames over time. Which SageMaker Ground Truth point cloud task type should they choose?
explanation: According to the notes, point cloud object tracking is used to track an object's movement across LIDAR frames over time — matching the need to follow an object across a sequence of frames. Semantic segmentation (A) colors pixels by label and doesn't track movement. Object detection (C) only draws a cuboid in each individual frame and doesn't handle movement over time. Text labeling (D) is unrelated to point clouds.

question: A mapping team labeling LIDAR point clouds needs every data point that belongs to a given class (for example, road surface) to be colored with that class label, rather than enclosing objects in boxes. Which point cloud task type provides this?

options.text: Point cloud object detection with tracking

explanation: According to the notes, point cloud semantic segmentation identifies objects by painting all the points/pixels associated with a label — matching the need to assign a color to each point by class rather than draw a box. Object detection (A) draws cuboids around objects. Object tracking (B) tracks movement over time. Option (D) isn't a task type listed in the notes.

question: A machine learning team is preparing a labeled dataset with SageMaker Ground Truth. They need workers to follow a specific instance of a vehicle as it moves across many consecutive frames of a driving video, capturing its position over time. Which Ground Truth video labeling task type is designed for this requirement?

options.text: Video frame point cloud semantic segmentation

explanation: According to the notes, Video Object Tracking is used to track a specific instance of an object across MULTIPLE frames over time (movement) — matching the requirement of following the same car across consecutive frames. Video Classification (A) only classifies the entire clip into classes and doesn't track an object. Video Object Detection (B) only labels an object in a SINGLE frame, not over time. (D) is a point cloud task and isn't described as a mechanism for tracking a vehicle across video frames here.

question: A team wants to assign one of several predefined categories to each short video clip in their dataset (for example, labeling entire clips as "sports", "news", or "advertisement"). Which SageMaker Ground Truth video task type fits this goal?
