options.text: Video Object Tracking with key points

explanation: The notes define Video Classification as classifying a video clip into specific classes — matching assigning one category to the entire clip. Video Object Detection (A) draws a shape around an object in one frame. Video Object Tracking (C) and its keypoint variant (D) both aim to follow an object instance across frames, not label the whole clip.

question: An exam scenario asks you to distinguish Video Object Detection from Video Object Tracking in SageMaker Ground Truth. Which statement correctly captures the difference described in the material?

options.text: Object Detection labels objects in a single frame, while Object Tracking follows the same object instance across frames over time.
Object Detection follows the same object across many frames, while Object Tracking labels objects in a single frame.
Object Detection categorizes the whole clip, while Object Tracking labels objects in a single frame.
Both tasks label objects only in a single frame, differing only in the annotation shape used.

explanation: This matches the Exam tip in the notes exactly: Object Detection = labeling an object in a SINGLE frame; Object Tracking = following the same object instance across MULTIPLE frames over time (movement). (B) reverses the definition. (C) mischaracterizes Object Detection (that's actually Video Classification). (D) is wrong because Object Tracking operates across multiple frames, not just one.

question: A labeling specialist configures a Video Object Detection job in SageMaker Ground Truth to annotate objects within individual frames. Which annotation tools can be used to draw around the specified objects? (Choose TWO.)
explanation: The notes list the drawing tools for Video Object Detection as Bounding box, Polygon, Polyline, and Key point. So Bounding box (A) and Key point (C) are both valid. Named entity recognition span (B) and Sentiment label (D) are for text/NLP tasks, not video. Confusion matrix (E) is a model-evaluation tool, not a labeling tool.

question: A machine learning team is using Amazon SageMaker Ground Truth to build a fully custom labeling workflow. They need to design their own labeling interface that combines custom HTML with pre-built web components tailored to their annotation task. Which technology does SageMaker Ground Truth custom labeling use to define these custom worker task templates?
explanation: The notes state clearly that Custom Labeling uses the Liquid templating language, allowing custom HTML and pre-built web components. B (CloudFormation) is an Infrastructure-as-Code service, unrelated to the labeling UI. C (Jinja2) is a Python/Flask templating engine, not the tool mentioned. D doesn't exist in the notes; Lambda is used for pre/post-labeling tasks, not for defining the UI template.

question: A data science team building a custom SageMaker Ground Truth labeling job wants to run their own label consolidation algorithm to merge annotations from multiple workers into a single result. Which mechanism should they configure to implement this custom consolidation logic?
explanation: According to the notes, the post-labeling task Lambda is used for custom label consolidation algorithms — matching the requirement of merging annotations from multiple workers. A (pre-labeling Lambda) only runs for each dataset object BEFORE it's sent to a worker, and doesn't handle consolidation. C (Liquid template) is only used to build the UI and doesn't handle consolidation logic. D isn't mentioned in the notes.

question: When configuring a custom labeling job in SageMaker Ground Truth, an engineer sets up a pre-labeling task Lambda function. What is the role of this pre-labeling task Lambda?

options.text: It is triggered for each dataset object before it is sent to the workers
It consolidates the labels returned by multiple workers
It renders the custom HTML interface shown to each worker
It automatically labels the dataset without human workers

explanation: The notes state that the pre-labeling task Lambda is triggered for each dataset object BEFORE it's sent to a worker. B is a function of the post-labeling task Lambda (consolidation). C is the role of the Liquid template, not Lambda. D isn't mentioned; Ground Truth custom labeling still relies on workers to apply labels.

question: A team is exploring the built-in template categories available in SageMaker Ground Truth to label an image dataset. Which of the following are image labeling template types provided by Ground Truth? (Choose TWO.)
explanation: According to the notes, templates in the Image group include Bounding Box, Image Classification, Image Contains, Image Similarity, Instance Segmentation, Keypoint, Polygon, and Semantic Segmentation — so A (Semantic Segmentation) and B (Bounding Box) are correct. C (Named Entity Recognition) belongs to the Text group. D (Collect Utterance) belongs to the Audio group. E (Video Clip Classification) belongs to the Video group.

question: A machine learning team is building a custom data labeling workflow in Amazon SageMaker Ground Truth. They want to design a custom worker task template using a set of prebuilt UI components provided by AWS rather than writing all the interface elements from scratch. What are these prebuilt components collectively called?
explanation: According to the notes, the prebuilt components used for custom labeling in SageMaker Ground Truth are called Crowd HTML (around 30 prebuilt components). B (JumpStart) is a repository of prebuilt models/solutions, not a labeling UI component. C (A2I) is a human-review service, but the notes don't call these components 'A2I widgets'. D (Data Wrangler transforms) are data-transformation steps, unrelated to labeling UI components. So A is correct.

question: A developer is authoring a custom labeling template with Crowd HTML components and needs a slider control that lets workers pick an integer rating from 1 to 10, forces a value to be provided, and shows the current value on the handle. Which snippet correctly configures this control?

options.text: <crowd-slider name="howMuch" min="1" max="10" step="1" pin="true" required>

explanation: The notes give this exact example: <crowd-slider name="howMuch" min="1" max="10" step="1" pin="true" required> — step=1 for integer values, pin=true displays the value on the handle, required forces input. A uses crowd-input (a text box), not a slider. C uses crowd-toggle-button (an on/off button), unsuitable for a range of values. D uses crowd-classifier with a range attribute not found in the notes' example. So B is correct.

question: A team is designing a custom worker UI in SageMaker Ground Truth. From the Crowd HTML component library, they need one element that lets workers draw rectangular boxes around objects in an image and another that lets workers assign a category to an entire image. Which two Crowd HTML components should they use? (Choose TWO.)
explanation: According to the component list in the notes: crowd-bounding-box is used to draw a rectangle around an object, and crowd-image-classifier is used to apply a classification label to an image. crowd-slider is a slider for selecting a numeric value. crowd-text-area is a box for long text input. crowd-toast is a toast notification. Only A and B match the two stated requirements.

question: A data science team needs human annotators from their own organization to label a sensitive image dataset using Amazon SageMaker Ground Truth. They do not want to use a public marketplace and want to control exactly who can access the labeling tasks. Which Ground Truth workforce option should they configure?

options.text: Create a private workforce with private teams
Use the Amazon Mechanical Turk public workforce
Use a third-party vendor workforce from AWS Marketplace
Create an anonymous labeling job with a public URL

explanation: The notes state clearly: with a private workforce, you create 'private teams' so people within your own organization perform labeling, fitting sensitive data and control over who has access. B (Mechanical Turk) and C (vendor Marketplace) are public/third-party workforce types, not mentioned in the notes as a solution for a privacy requirement. D doesn't exist in the notes; workers must log in to the Ground Truth UI.

question: When creating a private team in SageMaker Ground Truth, an administrator must choose an identity provider to authenticate the workers to their AWS account. Which identity provider options are available? (Choose TWO.)

options.text: AWS IAM access keys for each worker

explanation: According to the notes, to authenticate a private team into an AWS account, you choose an identity provider of either OpenID Connect (OIDC) or AWS Cognito. So A and B are correct. C (IAM access keys), D (Mechanical Turk credentials), and E (AD Connector) aren't mentioned in the notes as authentication options for a private team.

question: An administrator is creating a private team in Ground Truth and wants to reuse an existing set of users that are already managed in a Cognito User Pool. Which approach matches this requirement?

options.text: Create a private team with OpenID Connect (OIDC) using an external IdP
Create a private team with AWS Cognito and invite or import from existing Cognito user groups
Manually create each worker as a new IAM user in the account
Create a private team with Mechanical Turk and import the qualified workers

explanation: The notes state 'Create a private team with AWS Cognito' allows inviting/importing from existing Cognito user groups, matching the need to reuse users already in a Cognito User Pool. A (OIDC) is for your own IdP (which must support OIDC user groups), not Cognito. C and D aren't in the notes.

question: A team lead wants to add workers to a newly created private team in SageMaker Ground Truth. According to the available options, how can workers be added? (Choose TWO.)

options.text: Invite them by email, up to 50 workers, comma-separated
Import them from a Cognito User Pool
Upload a CSV of unlimited workers directly to Amazon S3
Add them by their AWS account root user email
Automatically enroll all IAM users in the account

explanation: The notes state you can invite a team via email (up to 50 workers, comma-separated) or import them from a Cognito User Pool. So A and B are correct. C (unlimited CSV via S3), D (root user email), and E (automatically adding every IAM user) aren't mentioned in the notes.

question: A data scientist is training an XGBoost model for a binary classification problem on a marketing dataset. They want to optimize the model so it maximizes the area under the curve (AUC), but they are unsure which hyperparameter values to assign. Which AWS service capability should they use to automatically find the training job that produces the highest AUC?
explanation: The notes state clearly that you use SageMaker Automatic Model Tuning: specify a range of values for a parameter, SageMaker runs training jobs across parameter-value variations and returns the job with the highest AUC. B (Ground Truth) is used to label data, C (Feature Store) stores features, D (Model Monitor) monitors a model in production — none search for the optimal hyperparameter.

question: When configuring SageMaker Automatic Model Tuning for the XGBoost use case, what does the engineer need to provide for each hyperparameter, and what does the service return?

options.text: The exact optimal value for each parameter; the service returns the trained model artifact
A range of values for the parameter; the service returns the training job with the highest AUC
A labeled validation dataset; the service returns cleaned feature columns
A fixed learning rate only; the service returns a hyperparameter importance report

explanation: According to the notes, the user specifies a RANGE of values for a parameter, SageMaker runs training jobs across variations, and returns the training job with the highest AUC. A is wrong because the whole point of tuning is that you DON'T know the optimal value. C and D describe functions not in the notes (feature cleaning, importance reporting).

question: In the described XGBoost tuning scenario, which evaluation metric is the objective that SageMaker Automatic Model Tuning aims to maximize?
explanation: The notes state clearly that the objective is to maximize AUC (area under the curve) for a binary classification problem. A (MSE) is for regression, B (perplexity) is for language models, D (BLEU) is for machine translation — none relate to this use case.

question: For the XGBoost binary classification model in the marketing use case, which hyperparameters are listed as tunable through SageMaker Automatic Model Tuning? (Choose TWO.)
explanation: The notes list the tunable parameters: eta, alpha, min_child_weight, max_depth. So max_depth (A) and eta (C) are both correct. B (num_layers), D (dropout_rate), E (batch_size) are hyperparameters of neural networks/deep learning, not mentioned for XGBoost in the notes.

question: A data science team is training a complex deep learning neural network and finds that the number of possible hyperparameter combinations is far too large to explore by hand. Which Amazon SageMaker capability is designed to automatically find the best version of the model for this scenario?
explanation: According to the notes, SageMaker Automatic Model Tuning (also called hyperparameter tuning) finds the best version of a model by running many training jobs, and is especially useful for complex Deep Learning networks when there are too many combinations to try manually. The other options (Data Wrangler, Feature Store, Model Monitor) aren't mentioned in the notes as a hyperparameter-tuning tool.

question: How does SageMaker Automatic Model Tuning determine the best version of a model?

options.text: It automatically selects a different algorithm that performs better on the dataset
It runs many training jobs using the algorithm and hyperparameter ranges you specify, then chooses the hyperparameter values that produce the best model measured by a metric you choose
It removes low-quality records from the training dataset until accuracy improves
It continuously retrains the deployed model on live production traffic

explanation: The notes state clearly that Automatic Model Tuning runs many training jobs using an algorithm and the hyperparameter ranges you specify, then chooses the hyperparameter values that produce the best model according to a metric you choose. Option A is wrong because the tool doesn't change the algorithm, it tunes the hyperparameters of a given algorithm. C is wrong because it doesn't clean data. D is wrong because it doesn't automatically retrain on production traffic.

question: A manager asks the ML team to reduce the cost of running the large number of training jobs launched by an Automatic Model Tuning job. According to AWS guidance, which option helps save money?

options.text: Running all training jobs sequentially on a single instance
Reducing the model to a single hyperparameter

explanation: The notes state clearly that you can save cost using EC2 Spot Instance Pricing. The other options (turning off metrics, running sequentially on a single instance, reducing to one hyperparameter) aren't mentioned in the notes as cost-saving measures, and some even contradict the purpose of tuning.

question: Before starting a SageMaker Automatic Model Tuning job, which prerequisites and considerations are accurate according to AWS? (Choose TWO.)

options.text: You need a dataset, an understanding of the algorithm type to train, and an understanding of how to measure success
Hyperparameter tuning is guaranteed to improve the accuracy of any model
Hyperparameter tuning might not improve your model and is an advanced tool that is part of the scientific development process
Automatic Model Tuning can only be applied to SageMaker built-in algorithms
A trained and deployed endpoint is required before tuning can begin

explanation: The notes list the prerequisites before tuning: having a dataset, understanding the type of algorithm to train, and understanding how to measure success (A correct). The notes also warn that tuning MIGHT NOT improve the model and is an advanced tool that's part of the scientific development process (C correct). B is wrong because it isn't guaranteed to improve. D is wrong because tuning applies to built-in, custom algorithms, and pre-built containers. E is wrong because the notes don't require an already-deployed endpoint as a prerequisite.

question: A healthcare company needs to label a dataset that contains patient records with protected health information (PHI). The data must never leave the organization's control. Which SageMaker Ground Truth workforce option should the company assign to the labeling job?

options.text: Amazon Mechanical Turk, because it provides an on-demand 24/7 crowd
A private workforce made up of the company's own employees or contractors
A vendor-managed workforce sourced from the AWS Marketplace
Amazon Mechanical Turk with a signed NDA applied to each worker

explanation: According to the notes, a Private workforce is a team you source yourself (your employees/contractors), for data that must stay within your organization — best suited for sensitive/PII data. So B is correct. A and D are wrong because Mechanical Turk is a public crowd, NOT used for confidential data (the notes state clearly 'NOT for confidential data'), and the notes don't mention an NDA option for Mechanical Turk. C is wrong because although a vendor is bound by an NDA, it's still a third party outside your organization and doesn't guarantee data stays internal.

question: When creating a SageMaker Ground Truth labeling job, an ML engineer must assign a workforce. How many workforce options are available to choose from?
explanation: The notes state clearly that when creating a labeling job you must assign a workforce, and there are '3 workforce options': Amazon Mechanical Turk, Private, and Vendor managed. So C (Three) is correct. The other numbers don't match the notes.

question: A startup has no internal labeling team but needs specialized labeling expertise at scale for a large image dataset. The data is sensitive, so any external labelers must be bound by a nondisclosure agreement. Which workforce option best fits these requirements?

options.text: Private workforce, using the startup's own staff
Amazon Mechanical Turk, for its low cost and public crowd
A vendor-managed workforce available through the AWS Marketplace
Amazon Mechanical Turk combined with a private review team

explanation: According to the notes, Vendor managed is the choice when you need expertise/scale but don't have an internal team, and it's still bound by an NDA via AWS Marketplace — exactly matching this scenario. So C is correct. A is wrong because the startup has no internal team. B and D are wrong because Mechanical Turk is a public crowd, unsuitable for sensitive data, and the notes don't describe this combined scenario.

question: A team needs to label a large volume of non-confidential, publicly shareable data at the lowest possible cost, and wants access to a large on-demand global workforce. Which SageMaker Ground Truth workforce option should they choose?

options.text: A vendor-managed workforce from the AWS Marketplace
A private workforce of internal employees
A private workforce supplemented by contractors under NDA

explanation: The notes describe Amazon Mechanical Turk as an on-demand 24/7 workforce of over 500,000 people globally, the cheapest option, a public crowd — fitting non-confidential data, large volume, low cost. So B is correct. A is wrong because a vendor targets expertise/scale rather than being the cheapest. C and D are wrong because a private workforce is for sensitive/internal data and isn't cost-optimal for public data.

question: A machine learning team is using SageMaker Automatic Model Tuning and wants the strategy that reaches good hyperparameter values in the fewest training jobs by learning from the results of previous iterations. Which tuning strategy should they choose?
explanation: According to the notes, Bayesian Search uses previous iterations to guide the next iteration (building a Gaussian Process), so it converges to a good value in the FEWEST training jobs and is the most efficient. Grid Search is brute-force, the most expensive. Random Search is fast and highly parallel but 'naive', not learning from past results. Hyperband stops poorly performing jobs early, fitting iterative DL, but the notes assign 'fewest training jobs / most efficient' to Bayesian.

question: A data scientist has only a small, limited set of candidate hyperparameter values and wants a strategy that is guaranteed to evaluate the best combination within that grid, accepting that the jobs will run independently in parallel. Which strategy best fits this requirement?
explanation: The notes state that Grid Search evaluates ALL possible combinations (exhaustive/brute-force), is guaranteed to find the best combination within the grid, is only feasible when the number of candidates is limited, and its jobs are independent so they can run in parallel. Bayesian doesn't guarantee scanning the whole grid and is less parallel. Random Search only samples randomly, so it doesn't guarantee finding the best combination. Hyperband is for iterative DL with early stopping, not for scanning an entire small grid.

question: A team is running a deep learning training workload and wants Automatic Model Tuning to save cost by early-stopping training jobs that are performing poorly. Which SageMaker tuning option is designed for this iterative deep learning scenario?
explanation: The notes state clearly that SageMaker also provides Hyperband, which stops poorly performing jobs early and is best suited for iterative deep learning training. Grid Search runs every combination, without early stopping. Random Search only samples randomly. Bayesian optimizes by learning from history, but the notes don't tie it to DL early-stopping.

question: An engineer needs the fastest possible tuning approach with many jobs running fully in parallel within a fixed budget, and does not require the algorithm to learn from prior results. Which strategy matches this need?

options.text: Bayesian Search, because it converges in fewer jobs
Random Search, because it is fully parallel and does not learn from past results
Grid Search, because it guarantees the best combination
Hyperband, because it early-stops poor jobs

explanation: The notes describe Random Search: cheaper than grid, samples combinations randomly, has good coverage for a given budget, is fully parallel, but does NOT learn from past results; used when you need speed and many parallel jobs. Bayesian is less parallel because each choice depends on prior results. Grid Search is expensive with exploding cost. Hyperband targets early stopping for DL, not the goal of 'no need to learn from history'.

question: An organization is evaluating why Bayesian Search is described as smart but less parallelizable than the other strategies in SageMaker Automatic Model Tuning. Which statements from the tuning approach explain its behavior? (Choose TWO.)

options.text: It builds a distribution of functions (a Gaussian Process) and updates it each iteration to decide which regions of the hyperparameter space to explore
It evaluates all possible combinations of hyperparameters exhaustively
Each pick depends on prior results, so the jobs are sequential and less parallelizable
It samples hyperparameter tuples completely at random without using history
It early-stops poorly performing jobs during iterative deep learning training

explanation: According to the notes, Bayesian Search builds a distribution of functions (Gaussian Process), updating the Gaussian each iteration to identify which region of the space to explore next (A), and because each choice depends on the previous result, it is sequential and less parallel (C). B describes Grid Search (exhaustive). D describes Random Search (fully random, doesn't use history). E describes Hyperband (early stopping for iterative DL).

question: A retail company runs a product-recommendation model on Amazon SageMaker. The application must return a recommendation to users the moment they view a product page, requiring an always-on, low-latency HTTPS API that returns one prediction per request. Which deployment option should the team use?

options.text: A SageMaker real-time endpoint using SageMaker Hosting Services
A SageMaker model package export to Amazon S3

explanation: The notes state clearly that Realtime Prediction (Endpoints) hosts a model on a server with an accessible API endpoint, sending an HTTPS request and receiving a prediction; uses SageMaker Hosting Services, suited for interactive/online predictions, low-latency, always-on, per-request. So B is correct. A (Batch Transform) is for large offline datasets, with no persistent endpoint, so it doesn't provide immediate low latency. C (compilation job) is only a model optimization/compilation step, not a way to serve online predictions. D (model package export) isn't a way to get real-time predictions.

question: A data engineering team needs to score a very large historical dataset stored in Amazon S3 once per week. Latency of individual predictions does not matter, and they want to avoid paying for a server that runs continuously. Which SageMaker option best fits this requirement?
explanation: According to the notes, Batch Predictions (Batch Transform Jobs) apply predictions to a large dataset, only needing a server for the duration of the job, with no persistent endpoint, cheaper when latency doesn't matter, used for large offline datasets. This exactly matches the weekly scoring scenario, so C is correct. A (real-time always-on) costs money to run continuously and isn't necessary when latency doesn't matter. B (Serverless) is for intermittent traffic, not processing one large offline dataset. D (Async) is for large payloads/long inference times via a queue, not the main goal here.

question: According to the notes, what is the definition of inference in the context of a deployed machine learning model?

options.text: The process of training a model on labeled data
The act of requesting and getting a prediction
The process of compiling a model for a target device
The act of packaging model artifacts into a model package

explanation: The notes define clearly: Inference is the act of requesting and getting a prediction; after a model is trained, it must be deployed to infer predictions. So B is correct. A describes training, not inference. C describes a compilation job. D describes a model package — all different SageMaker concepts, not the definition of inference.

question: An application sends inference requests with unusually large payloads, and each prediction can take a long time to compute. The team wants SageMaker to queue these requests rather than block on a synchronous response. Which inference option is designed for this scenario?
explanation: The notes state that Async = large payloads / long inference with a queue, matching the scenario of large payloads, long inference times, and needing a queue, so C is correct. A (Batch Transform) is for large offline datasets as a job, and doesn't describe a queuing mechanism for individual large requests. B (Serverless) is for intermittent traffic with auto-scale-to-zero, and doesn't emphasize large payloads/queuing. D (Real-time endpoint) is synchronous, low-latency per-request, and doesn't queue large payloads/long inference.

question: A team is comparing a SageMaker real-time endpoint with SageMaker Batch Transform. Which TWO statements correctly describe a SageMaker real-time endpoint according to the notes?

options.text: It provides an always-on, low-latency API that you pay for while it is running
It spins up a server only for the duration of a job and has no persistent endpoint
It returns a prediction per request over an HTTPS request
It is the cheapest choice for large offline datasets when latency does not matter
It automatically scales to zero for intermittent traffic

explanation: According to the notes, a real-time endpoint is low-latency, always-on, per-request, and you pay while it's running (A correct), and it's served via an HTTPS request returning one prediction per request (C correct). B describes Batch Transform (only using a server for the job duration, no persistent endpoint). D also describes Batch Transform (cheaper for large offline datasets when latency doesn't matter). E describes Serverless (auto-scale-to-zero for intermittent traffic), not a real-time endpoint.

question: A data science team wants to package the same feature-engineering code that they used during training so it also runs automatically at inference time, in order to avoid train/serve skew. They plan to chain preprocessing, a trained model, and postprocessing behind a single Amazon SageMaker endpoint. Which SageMaker capability should they use?

options.text: An inference pipeline composed of a linear sequence of containers
A multi-model endpoint that loads models on demand
A SageMaker batch transform job with a single container
Automatic model tuning with hyperparameter optimization

explanation: According to the notes, an inference pipeline is a SageMaker model consisting of a linear sequence of containers handling preprocessing → model → postprocessing behind a single endpoint, letting the same feature-engineering code run in both training and inference, avoiding train/serve skew. B (multi-model endpoint) and C (single-container batch transform) aren't concepts in the notes for this chaining purpose; D (hyperparameter tuning) is unrelated to chaining containers.

question: An ML engineer is designing an inference pipeline in Amazon SageMaker. What is the valid number of containers that can be chained together in the linear sequence?
explanation: The notes state clearly that an inference pipeline is a SageMaker model consisting of a linear sequence of 2 to 15 containers. A is wrong because a pipeline needs at least 2 containers; C and D are fabricated numbers not in the notes.

question: A team has deployed an inference pipeline to a SageMaker endpoint. They now need to change the preprocessing logic in the first container. According to the characteristics of inference pipelines, how must they apply this change?

options.text: Edit the running container in place on each EC2 instance
Hot-swap only the preprocessing container while keeping the endpoint running
Deploy a new version of the pipeline because inference pipelines are immutable
Scale the endpoint horizontally to force a configuration refresh

explanation: The notes state clearly that inference pipelines are immutable — to update an endpoint you must deploy a new version. So A (editing in place), B (hot-swapping individual containers), and D (scaling to refresh the configuration) all contradict the stated immutability.

question: In a SageMaker inference pipeline, how are the containers deployed and executed relative to the EC2 instances in the endpoint, and what benefit does this provide?

options.text: Each container runs on a separate EC2 instance, which reduces cost
SageMaker installs and runs all containers on each EC2 instance, co-locating them for low-latency inference
Containers are distributed across multiple AWS Regions for high availability
Only the first container runs on EC2 while the rest run in AWS Lambda

explanation: According to the notes, when deployed, SageMaker installs and runs all the containers on each EC2 instance of the endpoint (or transform job); the containers are co-located on the same EC2 instance, giving low inference latency. A, C, D are all wrong because the notes state clearly that the containers sit together on each instance rather than on separate instances, aren't distributed across multiple regions, and there's no running the rest on Lambda.

question: An ML engineer is building an inference pipeline and wants to reuse existing SageMaker-provided containers for the data transformation steps (preprocessing and postprocessing) rather than building them from scratch. Which containers are specifically mentioned as reusable for these data transforms? (Choose TWO.)

options.text: The SageMaker Spark ML Serving container

explanation: The notes state you can reuse the containers used for data transform from training: the SageMaker Spark ML Serving container and the scikit-learn container (e.g. a sample flow using the SparkML Serving Image for pre/post-processing). C, D, E are services/solutions not mentioned in the notes as reusable containers for data transform.

question: A machine learning engineer has finished training a model and now wants to deploy it for inference on Amazon SageMaker. According to the recommended workflow, what must the engineer do before the model can be deployed?

options.text: Create an inference pipeline endpoint first
Register the training job in a model registry first
Enable automatic scaling on the endpoint first

explanation: The notes state clearly: 'Before you can deploy a model you must first create a model.' So A is correct. B, C, D are steps or concepts not mentioned in the notes as a prerequisite for deployment; according to the notes, the mandatory step before deploying is exactly creating the model.

question: An engineer needs to programmatically create a model as the first step before deployment. Which API command should be used to accomplish this?
explanation: The notes state exactly: 'Use the CreateModel API command to do so' to create a model. So C is correct. A (DeployModel), B (CreateEndpoint), D (CreateInferencePipeline) aren't the command mentioned in the notes for creating a model.

question: A data science team has trained a preprocessing model using Spark ML to transform and engineer features for their dataset. They now want to serialize this Spark ML job and add it as a component to their Amazon SageMaker inference pipeline. Which tool should they use to package and serialize the Spark ML job into a container for the inference pipeline?
explanation: According to the notes, Spark ML jobs are packaged/serialized using MLeap into MLeap containers, then the MLeap container is added to the inference pipeline. AWS Glue (B) and Amazon EMR (D) are just environments for running the Spark ML job, not the serialization tool used to add it to the pipeline. Scikit-Learn (C) is a different type of preprocessor, unrelated to serializing Spark ML.

question: A machine learning engineer needs to run Spark ML jobs for feature processing before training a model in Amazon SageMaker. According to the recommended approaches, which options allow the engineer to run Spark ML jobs? (Choose TWO.)

options.text: Run Spark ML with AWS Glue from SageMaker Notebooks
Connect to existing Amazon EMR clusters to run Spark ML jobs
Package Spark ML jobs directly into SageMaker without any serialization
Run Spark ML jobs using Scikit-Learn containers
Convert Spark ML jobs to MLeap before running the preprocessing step

explanation: The notes state two ways to run Spark ML: (A) running Spark ML with AWS Glue from SageMaker Notebooks, and (B) connecting to existing Amazon EMR clusters to run the Spark ML job. (C) is wrong because packaging directly into SageMaker is for scikit-learn, not Spark ML. (D) is wrong because Scikit-Learn is a separate preprocessor, not used to run Spark ML. (E) is wrong because MLeap is used to serialize/package after the job exists, not a prerequisite for running the job.

question: A team wants to perform feature engineering with scikit-learn as a preprocessing step and deploy it within Amazon SageMaker. What is the recommended way to prepare scikit-learn jobs for use in SageMaker?

options.text: Serialize the scikit-learn jobs with MLeap before adding them to the pipeline
Run and package scikit-learn jobs into containers directly in Amazon SageMaker
Run the scikit-learn jobs on existing Amazon EMR clusters
Execute the scikit-learn jobs with AWS Glue from SageMaker Notebooks

explanation: The notes state clearly: with Scikit-Learn, you run and package the scikit-learn job into a container directly within Amazon SageMaker. MLeap (A) is only used to serialize Spark ML jobs. Amazon EMR (C) and AWS Glue from SageMaker Notebooks (D) are ways to run Spark ML, not applicable to scikit-learn per the notes.

question: During the workflow of building an inference pipeline, at what stage are preprocessors such as Spark ML jobs or Scikit-Learn jobs used?

options.text: After training, to serialize the final model output
Only during real-time inference, to post-process predictions
Before training, to transform data and engineer features
After deployment, to monitor model drift in production

explanation: According to the notes, before training, you use preprocessors to transform data and engineer features, using Spark ML jobs or Scikit-Learn jobs. Options A, B, D describe other phases (after training, only at inference, or after deployment), none of which match the role of the preprocessor described in the notes.

question: A data science team has deployed a fraud-detection model to a SageMaker real-time inference endpoint. The model receives very few requests overnight, yet the team is surprised to see charges accruing during those idle hours. Which characteristic of a SageMaker real-time (hosting services) endpoint explains this behavior?

options.text: The endpoint runs on a persistent ML EC2 instance that stays always on, so you pay continuously regardless of request volume.
The endpoint automatically scales to zero instances when idle but still bills a fixed storage fee.
The endpoint processes requests only in scheduled batches, and each batch incurs a separate charge.
The endpoint charges per prediction, so overnight charges must come from retries.

explanation: The notes state clearly that a real-time endpoint = a persistent endpoint that is always on, continuously billed for low latency, running on an ML EC2 instance. So even with few requests at night, it's still billed (A correct). B is wrong because the notes don't say the endpoint scales to zero. C describes Batch Transform, not a real-time endpoint. D is wrong because the cost comes from an always-running instance, not billed per prediction/retry.

question: A company only needs to score a large stored dataset once per day and does not require synchronous, low-latency responses. According to SageMaker guidance, which inference option is the more cost-effective choice compared with running a persistent real-time endpoint?

options.text: Deploy a real-time endpoint and delete it after each daily run.
Use Batch Transform, since no always-on endpoint is required for batch inference.
Use invoke_endpoint against an existing endpoint for each record.
Call the deploy function on the Estimator with a larger instance type.
Increase initial_instance_count to process records in parallel.

explanation: The notes state: if you only need batch inference and don't need a persistent endpoint, use Batch Transform, which is cheaper (B correct). A, C, D, E all revolve around setting up/calling a persistent real-time endpoint — more expensive and unnecessary for a batch workload, exactly as the notes warn.

question: A developer has finished training a model with a SageMaker Estimator and now wants to stand up a real-time inference endpoint. Which method should the developer call on the Estimator, and what does it return?

options.text: Call invoke_endpoint, which returns the endpoint name.
Call endpoint_name, which returns a Batch Transform job.
Call deploy, which returns a Predictor that launches EC2 server(s) and configures the hosting services endpoint.
Call fit, which returns a persistent endpoint ready for synchronous requests.

explanation: According to the notes, when ready to deploy for real-time inference, call the deploy function on the Estimator; deploy returns a Predictor — a deployable model, spinning up an EC2 server and configuring the SageMaker hosting services endpoint (C correct). A is wrong: invoke_endpoint is used to make predictions, not to deploy. B is wrong: endpoint_name only returns the endpoint's name. D is wrong: fit is used to train, not to deploy.

question: After deploying a model, an application needs to send a single record to the real-time endpoint and get a prediction back. Which API call is used, and what must be supplied with it?

options.text: Call deploy, supplying instance_type and initial_instance_count.
Call invoke_endpoint, supplying the endpoint name and the data via Body, along with ContentType and Accept.
Call endpoint_name, supplying the Predictor object and the training data.
Call deploy, supplying the Body, ContentType, and Accept parameters.

explanation: The notes state: use invoke_endpoint to make a prediction, providing the endpoint name and data via Body (along with ContentType, Accept) (B correct). A and D are wrong: deploy is used to deploy an endpoint (specifying instance_type, initial_instance_count), not to make predictions. C is wrong: endpoint_name only returns the endpoint's name and doesn't accept data for prediction.

question: A machine learning team deploys a single trained model to a SageMaker real-time endpoint. They want to embed data transformation logic that runs before the model receives a request and after it produces a result, without adding any additional containers to the deployment. Which approach meets this requirement?

options.text: Include the pre-processing and post-processing logic directly within the model's inference script
Build an Inference Pipeline that chains multiple containers in sequential order
Add pre-processing and post-processing steps to a SageMaker Pipeline
Run a SageMaker Processing job before and after each inference request

explanation: According to the notes, the Single Model approach embeds pre/post-processing logic directly in the inference script, without needing any additional container. B (Inference Pipeline) requires multiple containers running sequentially, violating the 'no additional container' requirement. C uses a SageMaker Pipeline with separate steps, not embedded within a single model's script. D is wrong because SageMaker Processing is only used with a Pipeline, and doesn't run around each individual inference request.

question: A team wants to combine several containers into one model so that each container's output feeds the next in a fixed order during inference. According to the described approaches, how is this pattern built?

options.text: By adding SageMaker Processing steps to a SageMaker Pipeline
By creating an Inference Pipeline model from multiple containers that run in sequential order using an Estimator
By writing all pre-processing and post-processing inside a single TensorFlow Serving inference script
By running each container as an independent SageMaker Processing job

explanation: The notes describe an Inference Pipeline as creating a model from multiple containers running in sequential order, and it's created using an Estimator (NOT using SageMaker Processing). So B is correct. A and D are wrong because an Inference Pipeline doesn't use SageMaker Processing. C is the Single Model approach (a single script), not chaining multiple containers.

question: An engineer is authoring pre-processing and post-processing as distinct steps within a SageMaker Pipeline. Which capability is intended to be used for these steps?

options.text: TensorFlow Serving pre and post function handlers
An Estimator that packages multiple containers
SageMaker Processing, which is intended to work with Pipeline
A single inference script deployed to a real-time endpoint

explanation: According to the notes, the SageMaker Pipelines approach creates pre/post steps within a Pipeline and uses SageMaker Processing, which is only used to work with a Pipeline. So C is correct. A is a detail of the Single Model approach using TensorFlow Serving. B belongs to the Inference Pipeline (using an Estimator). D is also the Single Model approach, not steps within a Pipeline.

question: A developer serves a model using TensorFlow Serving and needs to run custom transformations immediately before and after model inference within one deployed model. Which detail from the described approaches applies?

options.text: SageMaker Processing must be invoked around the inference call
Multiple containers must be chained with an Estimator
TensorFlow Serving provides designated function handlers for pre and post processing
A SageMaker Pipeline step is required to host the transformations

explanation: The notes state clearly that in the Single Model approach, when using TensorFlow Serving, the model has dedicated function handlers for pre and post processing. So C is correct. A is wrong because SageMaker Processing is only used with a Pipeline. B describes an Inference Pipeline. D is the SageMaker Pipelines approach, not necessary for a single TensorFlow Serving model.

question: A machine learning team has a trained SageMaker model and needs to score a large historical dataset stored in Amazon S3. The scoring is a one-time offline job, and there is no requirement for real-time predictions or a continuously running endpoint. Which SageMaker capability BEST meets this requirement while minimizing cost?

options.text: Deploy the model to a real-time inference endpoint and send the dataset row by row.
Use SageMaker Batch Transform to score the entire dataset at once and write results back to S3.
Enable auto scaling on a persistent endpoint to handle the large dataset.
Create a serverless inference endpoint that stays warm for the duration of the dataset.

explanation: The notes state clearly that when you don't need real-time and don't need an always-running endpoint, and want to process a large dataset once, use Batch Transform: it spins up instances, scores the entire dataset from S3, writes results back to S3, then shuts down, making it cheaper. A and C both use a persistent/real-time endpoint — exactly the trap the notes warn against (no need for an always-on endpoint). D still creates a background-running endpoint, unsuitable for a one-time offline problem.

question: When configuring a SageMaker Batch Transform job, a data scientist creates a transformer on the Estimator. Which parameters are specified at the point of CREATING the transformer object, as opposed to when starting the job? (Choose TWO.)

options.text: The S3 input data path for the dataset to be scored.
The number of ML EC2 instances to use (instance_count).
The S3 output path where results are written (output_path).
The content_type of the input file, such as text/csv.
The split_type used to break the input into records.

explanation: According to the notes, when creating a transformer on an Estimator, you specify the type/number of ML EC2 instances (instance_count, instance_type) and the S3 output path (output_path). So B and C are correct. A, D, E (input path, content_type, split_type) are parameters passed when CALLING transform to start the job, not when creating the transformer.

question: A data scientist has created a SageMaker transformer and now calls transform() to start a batch job on a CSV dataset stored in S3, where each record is on its own line. Which set of arguments correctly reflects how the input is specified according to the batch transform workflow?
explanation: The notes state clearly that when calling transform, you specify the S3 input path with data_type='S3Prefix', content_type='text/csv', split_type='Line'. So A is correct. B, C, D use a different data_type, content_type, or split_type that don't match the facts in the notes (CSV split by line), so they are wrong.

question: A SageMaker Batch Transform job has completed successfully and written its output to the configured S3 output path. What is the correct next step to obtain the scored results on a local machine?

options.text: Invoke the transformer's real-time endpoint to stream the predictions locally.
Query the results directly from the running batch transform instances before they shut down.
Download the output file recursively from S3, for example: aws s3 cp {batch_output} ./ --recursive
Call transformer.wait() again to have the results returned to the local session.

explanation: According to the notes, when the job completes it creates files at the output path, and you download the results using the recursive S3 command: aws s3 cp {batch_output} ./ --recursive - so C is correct. A is wrong because batch transform doesn't create a real-time endpoint. B is wrong because the instances have already shut down after the job finishes. D is wrong because transformer.wait() only waits for the job to complete and doesn't return the result files locally.

question: A machine learning team needs to host a TensorFlow object-detection model and a separate PyTorch text-classification model. To reduce infrastructure sprawl, they want both models served from a single SageMaker endpoint, even though each model requires a different framework container. Which SageMaker capability best fits this requirement?
explanation: The notes state clearly: Multi-Container endpoints let you deploy multiple containers (possibly different frameworks) on a single endpoint, up to 15 different containers. This exactly matches the scenario of two models using different frameworks (TensorFlow and PyTorch). A (multi-model) is wrong because multi-model uses multiple models BUT within a SINGLE container, which doesn't fit when different frameworks are needed. C and D aren't mentioned in the notes as a solution for combining multiple frameworks on one endpoint.

question: A developer has deployed a SageMaker multi-container endpoint and wants to route an inference request directly to one specific container named 'secondContainer', rather than running the containers as a serial pipeline. Which parameter should be used with invoke_endpoint to target that container?
explanation: According to the notes, to directly invoke a specific container in Direct mode, use invoke_endpoint with TargetContainerHostname (e.g. 'secondContainer'). A (TargetModel) is used for multi-model endpoints to select a model, not a container. B (ContainerName) isn't a parameter mentioned. D (InferenceExecutionConfig) is passed into create_model to configure the execution mode (e.g. Direct), not an invoke_endpoint parameter for selecting a container.

question: A team is designing a SageMaker multi-container endpoint and wants each container to be callable on its own using Direct invocation mode. According to the guidance, what are the benefits of accessing containers individually via direct invocation? (Choose TWO.)

options.text: It automatically encrypts all inference traffic
It allows more than 15 containers per endpoint
It removes the need to call create_model

explanation: The notes state clearly: accessing individual containers via direct invocation ('Mode': 'Direct') improves endpoint utilization AND optimizes cost. These are two independent benefits recorded in the notes. C (encrypting traffic) isn't mentioned. D is wrong because the maximum limit is still 15 containers, direct invocation doesn't change this number. E is wrong because creating the endpoint still requires sagemaker.create_model(...).

question: When programmatically creating a SageMaker multi-container endpoint that hosts two containers, which approach matches the documented setup?

options.text: Call sagemaker.create_model(...) passing InferenceExecutionConfig and a Containers=[container1, container2] list
Call sagemaker.create_model(...) once per container and merge the endpoints afterward
Deploy each container as a separate endpoint and place them behind an Application Load Balancer
Package both containers into a single image and register it as one multi-model container

explanation: The notes state exactly: create it via sagemaker.create_model(...) passing InferenceExecutionConfig and a list of Containers=[container1, container2]. B is wrong because a multi-container endpoint is defined in a SINGLE create_model call with a list of containers, no need to merge multiple endpoints. C describes multiple separate endpoints behind an ALB — the opposite of the goal of combining them into one endpoint, and not in the notes. D describes multi-model (multiple models within the same container), not multi-container with different frameworks.

question: A machine learning team needs to deploy thousands of small, similar models on Amazon SageMaker, with one model trained per customer. They want to minimize hosting costs. Which SageMaker deployment approach BEST fits this requirement?

options.text: Multi-model endpoints that host many models on a shared serving container
Multi-container endpoints running each model in its own framework container
A dedicated single-model endpoint for each customer model
Amazon Elastic Inference attached to each model container

explanation: The notes state clearly that multi-model endpoints save cost by hosting multiple models within a single serving container, ideal for a large number of small, similar models (e.g. 1 model per customer). A is correct. B (Multi-Container) is for models with different frameworks/containers, not this scenario. C creating a separate endpoint per model is expensive, the opposite of the cost-saving goal. D Elastic Inference can't be used with multi-model-enabled containers per the notes.

question: An architect is evaluating SageMaker multi-model endpoints. Which characteristic determines when multiple models can be hosted together on a single shared container?

options.text: The models must each use a different machine learning framework
The models are of the same type/framework and are loaded on demand into memory
The models must all be attached to Amazon Elastic Inference
The models must run in separate containers behind separate endpoints

explanation: According to the notes, multi-model means multiple models in the same container (same framework), loaded/unloaded (load on demand) from memory as needed (time-sharing). B is correct. A describes Multi-Container (different frameworks), and the notes warn not to confuse the two. C is wrong because Elastic Inference can't be used with multi-model-enabled containers. D describes separate single-model endpoints, contradicting the concept of a shared container.

question: A team plans to consolidate many models onto a single SageMaker multi-model endpoint. According to best practices, under what condition do multi-model endpoints work BEST?

options.text: When the models vary widely in size and invocation latency
When each model uses a distinct serving framework
When the models are fairly similar in size and invocation latency
When each model is deployed with Amazon Elastic Inference

explanation: The notes state that multi-model endpoints work best when models are fairly similar in size and invocation latency. C is correct. A is the opposite (large differences), which isn't ideal. B describes the different-framework case, which belongs to Multi-Container. D is wrong because Elastic Inference isn't compatible with multi-model-enabled containers.

question: Which of the following are features supported by SageMaker multi-model endpoints? (Choose TWO.)

options.text: Automatic conversion of models to a single shared framework
Guaranteed dedicated memory per model with no time-sharing

explanation: The notes list multi-model endpoints as supporting A/B testing (A) and working with Auto Scaling along with AWS PrivateLink (C). B is wrong because the notes state clearly it CANNOT be used with Amazon Elastic Inference. D isn't in the notes; models must already share the same framework, there's no automatic conversion. E is wrong because multi-model relies on time-sharing of memory resources, not dedicated memory.

question: When creating a model and its endpoint for a multi-model deployment in SageMaker, which configuration steps are required?

options.text: Choose "Use a single model" and then define a serial inference pipeline only
Choose "Use multiple models" when creating the model, and add production variants when creating the endpoint
Attach Elastic Inference accelerators and skip production variants
Create one production variant per framework and disable Auto Scaling

explanation: According to the notes: when creating a model, choose 'Use multiple models' (providing model artifacts and an inference image); when creating an endpoint, you just add production variants (model name, training job, variant name, instance type, initial instance count, initial weight). B is correct. A uses a single model, the wrong step. C is wrong because Elastic Inference can't be used with a multi-model container, and production variants are still required. D fabricates a constraint not in the notes; multi-model uses the same framework, without splitting by framework.

question: An ML engineer wants to run a SageMaker Processing job that uses a specific version of the SciKit-Learn framework for data preprocessing. They need to define the EC2 instance type and the SciKit-Learn framework version for the job. Which class should they use to configure this?
explanation: According to the notes, to run a processing job with SciKit-Learn, you use SKLearnProcessor to define the EC2 instance type and SciKit-Learn framework version. So A is correct. Options B (ScriptProcessor), C (SKLearnEstimator), D (FrameworkProcessor) aren't mentioned in the notes and aren't the class specified for this scenario.

question: A data scientist has configured an SKLearnProcessor and now needs to execute the SageMaker Processing job that runs a 'preprocessing.py' script and produces train, validation, and test outputs. Which method must they call to start the job?
explanation: According to the notes, you call run to run the job, providing code='preprocessing.py', inputs=[ProcessingInput(...)], and outputs=[ProcessingOutput(...)] for train/validation/test. So B is correct. A (fit) is used to train a model, not run a processing job; C (deploy) is used to deploy an endpoint; D (transform) relates to batch transform — none apply here per the notes.

question: When calling the run method on an SKLearnProcessor to preprocess data, which parameters are used to specify the processing script and to define the train, validation, and test data locations?

options.text: entry_point for the script and channels for the datasets
source_dir for the script and hyperparameters for the datasets
code for the script, with inputs and outputs for the datasets
image_uri for the script and data_config for the datasets

explanation: According to the notes, when calling run you provide code='preprocessing.py' (the script), inputs=[ProcessingInput(...)], and outputs=[ProcessingOutput(...)] for train/validation/test. So C is correct. Options A (entry_point/channels), B (source_dir/hyperparameters), D (image_uri/data_config) aren't the parameters described in the notes for SKLearnProcessor.run.

question: A machine learning team notices that a fraud-detection model deployed to production is gradually producing less accurate predictions as customer transaction patterns shift over time. Which AWS service is designed to detect and alert on this degradation?
explanation: According to the notes, when model accuracy gradually degrades over time due to changing data distribution (model drift leading to 'model decay'), Amazon SageMaker Model Monitor is the service that monitors model quality in production, detecting and alerting. Autopilot is used to automatically train models, Ground Truth is used to label data, Feature Store is used to store features — none of these three are tied by the notes to detecting drift.

question: A data science team deploys a model behind a real-time endpoint and wants continuous monitoring of prediction quality in production using SageMaker Model Monitor. Which monitoring mode fits this deployment?

options.text: On-schedule monitoring for asynchronous batch transform jobs
Continuous monitoring with a real-time endpoint
Continuous monitoring with a batch transform job that runs regularly
Event-driven monitoring triggered by S3 object uploads

explanation: The notes list 3 monitoring modes: continuous monitoring with a real-time endpoint, continuous monitoring with a scheduled batch transform job, and on-schedule monitoring for asynchronous batch transform jobs. Since the model is deployed behind a real-time endpoint, the correct answer is 'Continuous monitoring with a real-time endpoint' (B). A and C are real modes but are for batch transform, which doesn't match the scenario. D (event-driven from S3 upload) isn't among the modes stated in the notes.

question: A team configuring SageMaker Model Monitor wants to detect when the relative importance of input features changes over time (for example, via changes in SHAP values). Which type of monitoring addresses this specifically?
explanation: The notes list 4 monitoring types: data quality (drift versus a baseline), model quality (accuracy versus ground-truth labels), bias drift (using Clarify), and feature attribution drift (when feature importance per SHAP changes). Tracking changes in feature importance using SHAP is exactly feature attribution drift (C). Data quality tracks input data drift, model quality tracks accuracy against real labels, bias drift uses Clarify to track bias shift — none focus on changes in SHAP importance.

question: An organization implements SageMaker Model Monitor and expects it to keep the production model accurate over time. Which statements accurately describe how Model Monitor behaves? (Choose TWO.)

options.text: It emits CloudWatch metrics and can alert when values deviate from the baseline.
It automatically retrains the model when drift is detected.
It detects and alerts on drift but does not retrain the model on its own.
It replaces the deployed endpoint with a newly trained version automatically.
It labels new production data to create updated ground-truth datasets.

explanation: According to the notes, Model Monitor emits CloudWatch metrics and alerts when there's deviation from the baseline (A correct), and it only detects and alerts rather than automatically retraining the model (C correct). So B and D are wrong because the notes state clearly 'It detects & alerts, it doesn't automatically retrain' — it doesn't automatically retrain or replace the endpoint. E is wrong because data labeling isn't a function of Model Monitor described in the notes.

question: A data science team needs to run a distributed Apache Spark preprocessing job on Amazon SageMaker Processing. They want to define the EC2 instance type and specify the PySpark framework version for the job. Which class should they use to configure this processing job?
explanation: According to the notes, to run a processing job with Apache Spark, you use PySparkProcessor to define the EC2 instance type and PySpark framework version (e.g. framework_version='2.4'). So A is correct. Options B (ScriptProcessor), C (SKLearnProcessor), and D (FrameworkProcessor) aren't classes specific to PySpark/Spark and aren't mentioned in the notes for this scenario, so all are wrong.

question: An ML engineer is submitting a PySpark preprocessing script to a SageMaker Processing job using a PySparkProcessor. They need to pass the preprocessing code file along with input and output bucket key prefixes to the job. Which combination of parameters on the run call should they use?
explanation: According to the notes, you call run with submit_app='preprocess.py' to specify the PySpark script, and arguments=[...] to pass input/output buckets and key prefixes. So B is correct. Options A (entry_point/hyperparameters), C (source_dir/inputs), and D (code/job_arguments) aren't the parameters stated in the notes for PySparkProcessor.run, so they are wrong.

question: A team is configuring a PySparkProcessor for a SageMaker Processing job. Which settings can they specify directly when defining the PySparkProcessor to control the compute environment and the Spark framework? (Choose TWO.)

options.text: The PySpark framework version (for example, framework_version='2.4')
The EC2 instance type (for example, instance_type='ml.m5.xlarge')
The S3 output encryption key alias
The Auto Scaling target tracking policy

explanation: According to the notes, PySparkProcessor is used to define the EC2 instance type and PySpark framework version (e.g. framework_version='2.4', instance_type='ml.m5.xlarge'). So A and B are correct. Options C (output encryption key alias), D (VPC subnet routing table), and E (Auto Scaling policy) aren't mentioned in the notes, so they are wrong.

question: A machine learning team needs a service that lets them catalog trained models for production, manage multiple model versions, associate training metrics as metadata, and control an approval status (approve or reject) before a model is deployed through a CI/CD pipeline. Which SageMaker capability best fits these requirements?
explanation: According to the notes, SageMaker Model Registry lets you govern, catalog, version, and deploy models, attach metadata (training metrics), manage approval status, and auto-deploy with CI/CD — matching every requirement. Model Monitor (A) is used to monitor drift at runtime, not manage versions/approval. Model Cards (B) is only documentation/governance, not a version + approval + CI/CD mechanism. Feature Store (D) isn't mentioned for this purpose in the notes.

question: In SageMaker Model Registry, which statement correctly describes a Model Group?

options.text: It is a specific version of a model that includes the trained weights and inference code.
It is a logical grouping of ML models that contains many model versions.
It is a document used to record governance details about a model.
It is a runtime service that detects data drift for a deployed model.

explanation: The notes define a Model Group as a logical grouping of ML models, containing multiple model versions — matching B. Option A describes a Model Version (containing trained weights + inference code), not a Model Group. C describes Model Cards (governance documentation). D describes Model Monitor (monitoring drift at runtime).

question: A data scientist registers a trained model as a specific Model Version in the SageMaker Model Registry. According to the registry's definition, what does a Model Version include?

options.text: Only the metadata such as training metrics, with no executable artifacts
A collection of many model groups organized by business unit
The trained weights and the inference code for the model
Live monitoring rules that alert on model drift in production

explanation: The notes state clearly that a Model Version is a specific version of a model, including trained weights and inference code — matching C. A is wrong because a version isn't just metadata but includes the actual executable artifact. B reverses the relationship (a Model Group contains multiple versions, not the other way around). D describes a function of Model Monitor, not the content of a Model Version.

question: A team wants to distinguish between SageMaker capabilities so they choose the right tool for each need. Based on the notes, which TWO statements are correct?

options.text: Model Registry is used to manage model versions and approval status before deployment and integrate with CI/CD.
Model Cards are used to manage model versions and automate deployment through CI/CD pipelines.
Model Monitor is used to monitor model drift at runtime.
Model Registry is used to detect data drift for models already running in production.
Model Cards automatically approve or reject models before deployment.

explanation: A is correct: Model Registry manages version + approval status before deployment and integrates with CI/CD (per the Exam tip in the notes). C is correct: Model Monitor is used to monitor drift at runtime. B is wrong because Model Cards is only documentation/governance, not managing versions or CI/CD (that's Registry's role). D is wrong because detecting drift at runtime is Model Monitor's job, not Registry's. E is wrong because Model Cards doesn't auto-approve/reject — approval status is a Model Registry function.

question: A data science team needs to run feature engineering, data validation, and model evaluation workloads on fully managed infrastructure, without provisioning or managing the underlying cluster themselves. Which SageMaker capability is purpose-built for these ML processing tasks?
explanation: Theo notes, SageMaker Processing cho phep chay cac workload preprocessing, postprocessing va model evaluation tren ha tang fully managed, va ho tro feature engineering, data validation, model evaluation, model interpretation. Vi vay A dung. B (Ground Truth) la dich vu ghi nhan du lieu, C (Feature Store) luu tru feature, D (Model Monitor) giam sat mo hinh dang chay - deu khong phai khai niem duoc mo ta trong notes cho cac workload xu ly nay.

question: During a SageMaker Processing job, where does the processing container read its input data from and write its results to, before the output is stored back in Amazon S3?

options.text: It reads from /opt/ml/model and writes to /opt/ml/checkpoints
It reads from /opt/ml/processing/input and writes to /opt/ml/processing/output
It reads from and writes directly to Amazon S3 without any local container paths
It reads from /opt/ml/input/data and writes to /opt/ml/output/inference

explanation: Notes mo ta kien truc: S3 input to /opt/ml/processing/input to Processing Container to /opt/ml/processing/output to S3. Do do B dung. A, C, D dung cac duong dan khong co trong notes; dac biet C sai vi container van dung cac local path chuan de doc/ghi truoc khi du lieu duoc dua lai S3.

question: A company wants to reuse SageMaker Processing not only while data scientists are experimenting, but also to evaluate model performance after the code has already been deployed in production. Which statement about the SageMaker Processing APIs is correct?

options.text: The Processing APIs can only be used during the experimentation phase and cannot run once code is in production
The Processing APIs can only run after a model has been deployed to a real-time endpoint
The Processing APIs can be used both during the experimentation phase and after code is deployed in production to evaluate performance
The Processing APIs are restricted to real-time inference traffic and cannot perform batch evaluation

explanation: Notes ghi ro Processing APIs co the duoc dung trong experimentation phase va sau khi code da duoc deploy trong production (de danh gia hieu nang). Vi vay C dung. A va B dao nguoc/gioi han sai pham vi su dung; D bia ra rang buoc ve real-time inference khong co trong notes.

question: An ML team is configuring a SageMaker Processing job and needs to decide on the container image for the processing workload. According to how SageMaker Processing works, which options are valid choices for the processing container image? (Choose TWO.)

options.text: An image that must be pulled exclusively from AWS Marketplace
An on-premises Docker daemon image that bypasses the managed cluster

explanation: Notes neu rang processing container image co the la mot SageMaker built-in image hoac image cua rieng ban (your own). Do do A va B dung. C (bat buoc tu Marketplace), D (on-premises Docker bo qua managed cluster) va E (Lambda layer) deu la du kien khong co trong notes; hon nua notes noi job chay tren fully managed cluster instance.

question: A machine learning engineer is building an automated workflow in Amazon SageMaker that consists of a series of interconnected steps. In what format is a SageMaker Pipelines pipeline definition encoded?
explanation: According to the notes, SageMaker Pipelines is a chain of linked steps defined by a JSON pipeline definition. So A is correct. B (YAML CloudFormation), C (Python setup script), and D (XML) aren't mentioned in the notes as the pipeline definition's format.

question: A data scientist wants to understand how a SageMaker Pipelines pipeline definition represents the structure of the workflow internally. Which structure does the pipeline definition use to encode the pipeline?

options.text: A flat list of unordered tasks

explanation: The notes state clearly that the Pipeline Definition encodes the pipeline as a Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG), so A is correct. C is wrong because a DAG is 'acyclic' (has no cycles) - no node can reference back to itself. B (relational database schema) and D (flat list of unordered tasks) aren't mentioned and contradict the directed-graph nature of a pipeline.

question: An ML engineer notices that in a SageMaker Pipelines DAG, they never explicitly declare which step must run after another step. How are the relationships between steps in the pipeline determined?

options.text: By data dependencies between the steps, which are pre-determined
By manually defining the execution order in the JSON definition
By the alphabetical order of the step names
By the timestamp at which each step is created

explanation: According to the notes, the relationship between steps is determined by data dependencies and is pre-determined - you don't define relationships yourself. So A is correct. B is wrong because the notes state clearly you don't define relationships manually. C (alphabetical order) and D (timestamp) aren't in the notes.

question: A team is reviewing their SageMaker training workflow in SageMaker Studio. How is a SageMaker Pipelines pipeline visualized in SageMaker Studio?

options.text: As a Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) of the interconnected steps
As a Gantt chart of step durations
As a pie chart of resource utilization
As a raw table of CloudWatch log entries

explanation: The notes state that the pipeline is visualized in SageMaker Studio as a DAG (e.g. AbaloneProcess → AbaloneTrain → AbaloneEval → ...). So A is correct. B (Gantt chart), C (pie chart), and D (CloudWatch log table) aren't mentioned in the notes.

question: A machine learning team wants to build and orchestrate an end-to-end ML workflow that is fully managed and runs directly within Amazon SageMaker, without having to integrate and manage separate AWS orchestration services. Which capability should they use?
explanation: Theo notes, SageMaker Model Building Pipelines la cong cu de xay dung ML pipelines voi tich hop truc tiep vao SageMaker (fully managed, directly within SageMaker), khong can tuong tac voi cac dich vu AWS khac. B (Data Wrangler), C (Feature Store) va D (Ground Truth) khong duoc mo ta trong notes la cong cu orchestrate pipeline, nen deu sai.

question: A data science team wants to reduce the run time of repeated pipeline executions in Amazon SageMaker. They notice that some steps produce identical outputs each run and do not need to be recomputed. Which SageMaker Pipelines feature lets them reuse the output of a previous execution of the same step without re-running it?
explanation: Notes ghi ro Step Reuse cho phep chi dinh cac step de caching, tai su dung output tu lan chay truoc cua cung mot step trong cung pipeline ma khong phai chay lai. A (Data Lineage Tracking) chi theo doi lich su du lieu, khong tai su dung output. C (Studio Integration) de track/view/execute qua UI. D (Python SDK) de tao pipeline bang code. Cac phuong an nay khong lien quan den caching.

question: During an audit, a governance team needs to trace, for a given pipeline execution, where the data originally came from, where it was used as input, and what outputs were generated. Which SageMaker Pipelines capability provides this?
explanation: Data Lineage Tracking theo notes theo doi lich su cua du lieu trong qua trinh thuc thi pipeline: du lieu den tu dau, duoc dung lam input o dau, va output nao duoc tao ra - dung nhu yeu cau audit. A (Step Reuse) chi lien quan caching de tai su dung output. B (Python SDK) de tao pipeline bang code. D (Studio Integration) de track/view/execute qua UI, khong phai truy vet nguon goc du lieu chi tiet. Do do C la dap an dung.

question: A developer prefers to define and create SageMaker ML pipelines programmatically in code rather than through a graphical interface, and also wants to be able to track, view, and execute those pipelines in a visual UI. Which two integrations of SageMaker Model Building Pipelines support these needs? (Choose TWO.)
explanation: Notes liet ke SageMaker Python SDK Integration (tao pipeline bang Python theo cach programmatic) va SageMaker Studio Integration (track, view va execute pipeline trong UI cua Studio) - hai dieu nay dung khop voi hai nhu cau. C (Step Reuse) va D (Data Lineage Tracking) la loi ich khac, khong lien quan viec tao bang code hay UI. E (Ground Truth Integration) khong duoc de cap trong notes. Vi vay A va B la dung.

question: A machine learning governance team needs a single, standardized way to document their production models, including model details, training metrics, performance evaluations, and deployment history, so they can manage and govern models across the organization. Which SageMaker capability is designed for this purpose?
explanation: According to the notes, SageMaker Model Cards is a 'documentation framework' for managing and governing ML models, recording model details, training metrics, performance evaluations, and deployment history. This exactly matches the requirement in the question. B (Feature Store) is used to store and manage features, C (Data Wrangler) is used for data preparation, D (Ground Truth) is used for data labeling — none is a documentation framework for model governance, and none is mentioned in the notes for this purpose.

question: A team trains a model directly on Amazon SageMaker and wants to minimize the manual effort required to populate the documentation for that model. According to the way Model Cards work, what benefit can the team rely on?

options.text: Model Cards must be written entirely by hand in a text editor before deployment
Models trained on SageMaker can auto-populate the Model Card
Model Cards are generated only after a human governance review is completed
Model Cards can only be created by importing data from an external documentation tool

explanation: The notes state clearly: 'Models trained on SageMaker can auto-populate Model Cards' — meaning a model trained on SageMaker can automatically fill in the Model Card, reducing manual effort. A is wrong because it isn't mandatory to write it entirely by hand. C is wrong because auto-populate doesn't depend on waiting for human review. D is wrong because the notes don't mention it only being creatable by importing from an external tool; in fact it can also be created via the SageMaker Python SDK.

question: An ML engineer is establishing a workflow for creating and maintaining SageMaker Model Cards as part of the model lifecycle. Based on how Model Cards behave, which TWO statements are correct? (Choose TWO.)

options.text: Model Cards can be versioned and exist in various states, such as Draft
Model Cards can be created programmatically using the SageMaker Python SDK
Model Cards can only ever exist in a single, immutable final state
Model Cards can only be created through the AWS Billing console
Model Cards replace the need to train or evaluate the model at all

explanation: The notes state that Model Cards 'can be versioned, in various states (e.g. Draft), created via the SageMaker Python SDK' — so A and B are both correct and independent.
