A is wrong because Transaction Fraud Insights is for cases where the entity ALREADY has an interaction history for the model to analyze. C is wrong because Account Takeover Insights is used when an account has been compromised by phishing or another attack. D is wrong because "External Events Insights" isn't a predefined model; EXTERNAL_EVENTS is just a trainingDataSource.

question: A payment platform evaluating returning users wants to detect whether their existing accounts were compromised through phishing. Which two predefined Amazon Fraud Detector models are most relevant to describe, respectively, (1) evaluating entities that have a history of interactions and (2) detecting a compromised account? (Choose TWO.)
explanation: The correct answers are B and C. According to the notes, Transaction Fraud Insights is for cases where the entity being evaluated already HAS an interaction history the model can analyze to improve accuracy (item 1). Account Takeover Insights is used when an account has been compromised by phishing or another attack (item 2). A is wrong because Online Fraud Insights is for cases with LITTLE historical data. D and E are wrong because "Fake Account Insights" and "Payment Risk Insights" aren't among the predefined models listed in the notes.

question: A developer is using boto3 to create an Amazon Fraud Detector model with create_model_version. After reviewing the model's performance, what action deploys the model so it can be used for real-time fraud detection?

options.text: Call create_model_version again with trainingDataSource set to EXTERNAL_EVENTS
Set unlabeledEventsTreatment to AUTO in the training data schema
Set the model version status to ACTIVE via update_model_version_status
Upload the training dataset to the referenced S3 bucket

explanation: The correct answer is C. The notes state under the Deploy section: after reviewing performance, set the model version to ACTIVE via update_model_version_status to detect fraud in real time. A is wrong because create_model_version is the step for creating/training a model, not deploying. B is wrong because unlabeledEventsTreatment=AUTO is a parameter within trainingDataSchema when creating a model, unrelated to deployment. D is wrong because uploading a dataset to S3 is a data-preparation step that happens before training, not deployment.

question: When defining an Event type in Amazon Fraud Detector, which element represents WHO is performing the event, such as a Customer?
explanation: The correct answer is A. The notes define Entities as representing WHO is performing the event, e.g. Customer. B is wrong because Labels classify an event as fraudulent or legitimate. C is wrong because Variables are the data points used in the model, e.g. Location, Transaction Amount. D is wrong because Outcomes define the predicted fraud result (e.g. high_risk, actions approve/review), not part of the Event type definition per the Labels/Entities/Variables requirement.

question: In the Amazon Fraud Detector component flow, a data scientist needs to interpret variable values during a fraud prediction using an expression language (operators, regex) and return a specific result. Which two statements correctly describe the roles of Rules and Outcomes in this flow? (Choose TWO.)

options.text: Rules interpret variable values during a fraud prediction using an Expression and reference a Variable or List
Rules are numerical values representing the estimated risk level of an event being fraudulent
Outcomes define the fraud prediction result, such as risk levels and actions like approve or review
Outcomes are the model-generated scores that feed directly into Thresholds
Rules are generated automatically by the model and cannot be user defined

explanation: The correct answers are A and C. The notes state that Rules interpret the value of a variable during fraud prediction, consisting of a Variable or List (data to operate on), an Expression (a rule language with operators, regex), and an Outcome. Outcomes define the predicted result, e.g. risk levels (high_risk, medium_risk, low_risk) and actions (approve, review). B is wrong because that description belongs to Scores (a numeric value indicating risk level), not Rules. D is wrong because Outcomes aren't scores; Scores are produced by the model, while Thresholds are fed into Rules. E is wrong because Rules are user-defined, not automatically generated by the model.

question: A media company wants to add a feature that reads website articles aloud to users, turning text into lifelike speech. Which AWS service should the team use?
explanation: Answer A. According to the notes, Amazon Polly is a text-to-speech (TTS) service that converts text into natural-sounding speech, fitting use cases like reading articles, e-learning, accessibility. B is wrong because Amazon Transcribe is speech-to-text (STT), the opposite. C is wrong because Amazon Lex is used for real-time two-way voice chatbots, not just reading text. D (Comprehend) isn't a TTS service.

question: A support team has recordings of customer phone calls and needs to produce written transcripts of what was said. Which AWS service is designed for this speech-to-text task?
explanation: Answer B. The notes emphasize the distinction: Transcribe = STT (speech→text), correct for converting call audio into text. A and D are wrong because Polly is TTS (text→speech), the opposite direction. C is wrong because Lex is for real-time two-way voice chatbots, not for producing a transcript.

question: A brand wants Amazon Polly to produce the most natural-sounding audio and to use features such as the Newscaster speaking style and brand voices. Which Polly voice type should they choose?
explanation: Answer D. According to the notes, Polly supports Standard and Neural (NTTS); Neural gives more natural voices and unlocks features like the Newscaster style and brand voices. A is wrong because Standard doesn't have these features. B and C aren't voice types in the notes — 'whispered' is just an SSML effect, not a voice type.

question: A developer is writing SSML for Amazon Polly and wants to insert a one-second pause at a specific point, for example <break time="1s"/>. Which SSML tag inserts a pause?
explanation: Answer B. The notes list <break> as the tag for creating a pause, with the example <break time="1s"/>. A (<prosody>) adjusts volume/rate/pitch rather than creating a pause. C (<say-as>) controls how special word types are read. D (<sub>) is used to read abbreviations/acronyms via a substituted form.

question: An audiobook team needs Amazon Polly to adjust the volume, speaking rate, and pitch of a passage. Which SSML tag provides this control?
explanation: Answer C. The notes state clearly that <prosody> controls volume, speaking rate, and pitch. A (<emphasis>) only emphasizes a word (Strong/Moderate/Reduced). B (<phoneme>) specifies phonetic pronunciation for a text segment. D (<lang>) specifies a different language and doesn't adjust volume/rate/pitch.

question: A team is enhancing Amazon Polly output with SSML but notices certain features only work when a Neural voice is selected. Which TWO SSML capabilities require Neural voices? (Choose TWO.)

options.text: The Newscaster speaking style via <amazon:domain name="news">

explanation: Answers B and D. The notes state clearly that some tags only work with Neural voices, specifically the Newscaster domain (<amazon:domain name="news">) and DRC (<amazon:effect name="drc">). A (<break>), C (<sub>), and E (whispered) are regular SSML tags, not restricted to Neural only per the notes.

question: A social media company needs to automatically detect and flag inappropriate or unsafe imagery in user-uploaded photos and videos before they are published. The team has no machine learning expertise and wants a managed service with prebuilt capabilities. Which AWS service and feature should they use?

options.text: Amazon Textract to extract text from the uploaded media
Amazon Rekognition Custom Labels trained on the company's own photos

explanation: The notes state clearly: 'Content moderation for images/video → Rekognition' and Moderating Content is a prebuilt model. So use Amazon Rekognition content moderation (C). A is wrong because Textract only extracts text/document/form/table, not moderating image content. B is wrong because Custom Labels is used to recognize business-specific objects (trained on your own images), not general unsafe-content moderation, and here they want a prebuilt solution without needing ML expertise. D (People Pathing) only tracks a person's path and doesn't moderate content.

question: A banking application requires face-based authentication and must prevent spoofing attempts where an attacker presents a photo or video of a legitimate user instead of being physically present. Which Amazon Rekognition capability directly addresses this requirement?
explanation: The notes state: 'Face Liveness prevents spoofing during facial authentication', matching the requirement to prevent spoofing with photos/videos during authentication. So D is correct. A (Recognizing Celebrities) only identifies famous people. B (Detecting Text) only reads text in images. C (Searching Faces in a Collection) finds/matches faces in a collection, but isn't the anti-spoofing mechanism described in the notes.

question: A manufacturing company wants to detect its own proprietary product logos and company-specific scenes that generic vision models do not recognize. They are willing to provide their own labeled images for training. Which Amazon Rekognition capability fits this need?

options.text: The prebuilt Object Detection model with detect_labels
The prebuilt Face Detection model with detect_faces

explanation: The notes say Custom Labels 'can identify objects, logos, and scenes in images that are specific to your business needs' and is 'trained on your own images'. Since a business-specific logo/scene needs training on custom images, A is correct. B is a prebuilt model for general object detection, not identifying business-specific logos. C is used for face detection, unrelated to logos. D detects video segments, not business logos/scenes.

question: A developer needs to extract structured data from scanned invoices, including form fields and tables. A colleague suggests using Amazon Rekognition's Detecting Text feature. Which statement correctly describes the appropriate service choice based on the differences between these services?

options.text: Rekognition Detecting Text is the correct choice because it extracts structured forms and tables from documents
Amazon Textract should be used because it is designed to extract structured document, form, and table data
Rekognition Custom Labels should be used to read the invoice fields
Amazon Rekognition content moderation should be used to validate the invoice

explanation: The notes clearly distinguish: 'Text-in-image ≠ Textract (Textract = structured document/form/table extraction)'. Rekognition Detecting Text only reads text appearing in an image and doesn't extract structured form/table data, so B is correct and A is wrong. C (Custom Labels) is used to recognize business-specific objects/logos/scenes, not extract form data. D (content moderation) is used for content moderation, unrelated to invoice extraction.

question: A team is preparing images to send to the Amazon Rekognition API. Which TWO statements about image input requirements are correct according to Amazon Rekognition? (Choose TWO.)

options.text: Images must be in JPG or PNG format
Rekognition can access images stored in an Amazon S3 bucket
Images must always be manually converted to CSV before submission
Only the TIFF and BMP formats are supported by the API
The AWS SDKs require you to disable base64 encoding manually

explanation: The notes state: 'Image requirements: JPG or PNG' (A correct) and 'can access images from an S3 bucket' (B correct). C is wrong because there's no requirement to convert to CSV; CSV isn't an image format. D is wrong because the notes only state JPG/PNG, not TIFF/BMP. E is wrong because the AWS SDKs (Java, JavaScript, Python, PHP) automatically base64-encode the image, with no need to manually disable it; base64 only applies when passing data via the HTTP API.

question: A data analyst is organizing Amazon Athena resources and needs to identify the object that represents a set of saved queries whose access can be granted to other users. Which Athena component fits this description?
explanation: According to the notes, a Workgroup is a collection of saved queries that you grant other users access to, matching the description. A data source is a group of databases (a catalog). A database is a group of tables (a schema). A dataset is a table's raw data. So only Workgroup fits.

question: When a new query runs in Amazon Athena and no catalog is explicitly specified, which data source is used by default?
explanation: The notes state clearly that the data source (sometimes called the catalog) has a default value of AwsDataCatalog. The other options (AwsGlueCatalog, DefaultSchema, AthenaCatalog) aren't mentioned in the notes and aren't the default value.

question: A developer needs to change the schema of an existing Athena table by adding a new column, and later remove an obsolete table entirely. Which SQL subset contains the statements used for these schema-defining operations?
explanation: According to the notes, DDL (Data Definition Language) defines the schema and includes CREATE, ALTER, DROP — exactly the commands used to change table structure (ALTER to add a column) and delete a table (DROP). DML operates on the dataset (INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE). DQL is used to select the dataset (SELECT). TCL isn't mentioned in the notes.

question: An Athena user wants to modify the raw data within a table by inserting new rows and updating existing values. Which two SQL statements belong to the DML subset described for manipulating datasets? (Choose TWO.)
explanation: The notes define DML (Data Manipulation Language) as operating on the dataset with examples INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE — so INSERT and UPDATE are the two correct DML commands. SELECT belongs to DQL (selecting the dataset). CREATE and DROP belong to DDL (defining the schema), not DML.

question: A company wants to adopt an international standard specifically to establish and continually improve a management system for the responsible development and use of its AI-based products. Which standard is designed for this purpose?

options.text: ISO/IEC 42001:2023 for an AI Management System (AIMS)
NIST AI Risk Management Framework (AI RMF)

explanation: ISO/IEC 42001:2023 is an international standard specifying requirements for establishing, implementing, maintaining, and continually improving an AI Management System (AIMS) for responsible AI development and use. A (ISO 27001) is for information security management, not AI. C (NIST AI RMF) is a voluntary framework, not a certifiable management standard. D (EU AI Act) is a risk-classification law, not a management system.

question: An organization notes that ISO/IEC 42001:2023 is often compared to ISO/IEC 27001. What is the key distinction between the two standards?

options.text: ISO/IEC 42001 targets an AI management system, whereas ISO/IEC 27001 targets information security management
ISO/IEC 42001 is a voluntary framework, whereas ISO/IEC 27001 is legally binding
ISO/IEC 42001 classifies AI systems by risk level, whereas ISO/IEC 27001 does not
ISO/IEC 42001 cannot be certified, whereas ISO/IEC 27001 can be certified
ISO/IEC 42001 applies only to AI providers, whereas ISO/IEC 27001 applies only to AI users

explanation: According to the notes, ISO/IEC 42001 is similar to ISO 27001 but for an AI management system, while 27001 is for information security. B is wrong because 'voluntary vs. legally mandated' isn't the distinguishing point stated. C is wrong because classifying by risk level is a feature of the EU AI Act, not 42001. D is wrong because 42001 is certifiable. E is wrong because 42001 is for both entities that provide and use AI.

question: A compliance team wants to obtain a third-party certification demonstrating that their AI governance program meets an established standard. Which option supports formal certification?
explanation: The notes state clearly that ISO/IEC 42001 is certifiable, similar to ISO 27001's certification model. A (NIST AI RMF) is described as voluntary and not issuing certification. B (EU AI Act) is a risk-classification law, not a certification mechanism. D isn't mentioned in the notes as an AI-governance certification standard.

question: During an exam prep review, a candidate must avoid confusing ISO/IEC 42001 with other AI governance references mentioned alongside it. Which two statements correctly describe those other references? (Choose TWO.)

options.text: NIST AI RMF is a voluntary risk-management framework that does not provide certification
The EU AI Act is a law that classifies AI systems by risk
NIST AI RMF is an international standard for an AI Management System that can be certified
The EU AI Act is a voluntary framework identical to ISO/IEC 27001
NIST AI RMF is a mandatory certification scheme for information security

explanation: According to the notes, you need to distinguish: NIST AI RMF is an AI risk-management framework, voluntary and non-certifying (A correct); the EU AI Act is a risk-classification law (B correct). C is wrong because a certifiable AIMS is a feature of ISO/IEC 42001, not the NIST AI RMF. D is wrong because the EU AI Act is a law, not a voluntary framework, and isn't the same as ISO 27001. E is wrong because the NIST AI RMF is voluntary and isn't an information-security certification scheme.

question: A development team is building an LLM-powered customer support assistant on AWS and wants a reference framework that specifically catalogs the most critical security vulnerabilities for large language model applications. Which resource best meets this need?

options.text: The OWASP Top 10 for Large Language Model Applications
The traditional OWASP Top 10 for web applications
The AWS Well-Architected Framework operational excellence pillar
The NIST Cybersecurity Framework general controls list

explanation: The notes state clearly that the OWASP Top 10 for Large Language Model Applications is a list of the 10 most critical security vulnerabilities SPECIFIC to LLM applications. B is wrong because that's the traditional OWASP Top 10 for web apps, and the notes warn not to confuse the two. C and D aren't LLM-specific vulnerability lists and aren't mentioned in the notes.

question: According to the OWASP Top 10 for LLM Applications, which vulnerability is identified as the number one risk for LLM applications?
explanation: The notes state clearly that risk #1 is Prompt Injection. Options A (training data poisoning), B (sensitive info disclosure), and D (excessive agency) are all other items in the OWASP Top 10 for LLM list, but not the top risk.

question: A CISO is planning the rollout of generative AI technology across the organization and wants a structured guide to help govern the deployment securely. According to the OWASP Top 10 for LLM Applications resource, what does it include to support this need?

options.text: A Security & Governance Checklist for a CISO managing GenAI rollout
A pre-built Amazon Bedrock Guardrails configuration template
A certified compliance audit report for GenAI workloads
A managed threat-detection service for LLM endpoints

explanation: The notes state clearly that the document includes a Security & Governance Checklist — guidance for a CISO managing the rollout of GenAI technology. B is wrong: Bedrock Guardrails is a separate mitigation measure, not part of the OWASP checklist. C and D are services/reports not mentioned in the notes.

question: A security architect is documenting example vulnerabilities that the OWASP Top 10 for LLM Applications highlights. Which of the following are cited as example vulnerabilities in this list? (Choose TWO.)

options.text: Cross-site request forgery on a web form
Weak Wi-Fi encryption on office networks

explanation: The notes list example vulnerabilities including prompt injection, data leakage, inadequate sandboxing, unauthorized code execution — so A and C are correct. B (CSRF web form), D (physical data center intrusion), and E (weak Wi-Fi encryption) are general/traditional web risks, not among the OWASP Top 10 for LLM examples per the notes.

question: A team has identified prompt injection as a key risk from the OWASP Top 10 for LLM Applications and wants an AWS service capability to help mitigate such threats when deploying models. Which option is described as a mitigation measure?
explanation: The notes state clearly that Guardrails (Bedrock) is a mitigation measure for LLM security threats. B, C, D are other AWS services but aren't mentioned in the notes as a mitigation measure for the OWASP Top 10 for LLM.

question: A startup wants to deploy a generative AI feature quickly with the least security responsibility for the underlying model and infrastructure. According to the AWS Generative AI Security Scoping Matrix, which two scopes represent the LOWEST ownership and security burden? (Choose TWO.)
explanation: In the matrix, the lower the scope, the less you own/control the model and data, so the fewer security responsibilities you have. Scope 1 (Consumer App, e.g. ChatGPT, Midjourney) and Scope 2 (Enterprise App/SaaS, e.g. Salesforce Einstein GPT, Amazon CodeWhisperer) are the two lowest scopes, fitting when you want speed and less responsibility. Scopes 3, 4, 5 progressively increase control, so they carry more security burden (C, D, E wrong).

question: A company builds an application on top of Amazon Bedrock base models without modifying the model itself. Which scope of the Generative AI Security Scoping Matrix does this represent?
explanation: Scope 3 - Pre-trained Models means building your application on top of a versioned model, a typical example being Amazon Bedrock base models. Scope 2 is using an existing app/SaaS with a built-in GenAI feature (wrong). Scope 4 is fine-tuning a model on your data (wrong, here the model isn't modified). Scope 5 is training a model from scratch (wrong).

question: A team fine-tunes a foundation model on their proprietary dataset using Amazon Bedrock customized models and Amazon SageMaker JumpStart. Which scope in the Generative AI Security Scoping Matrix best describes this work?
explanation: Scope 4 - Fine-tuned Models means fine-tuning a model on your data; the example in the notes is exactly Amazon Bedrock customized models and Amazon SageMaker JumpStart. Scope 3 only uses an unmodified base model (wrong). Scope 5 is training from scratch, not fine-tuning (wrong). Scope 2 is using existing SaaS (wrong).

question: An organization needs maximum control over its model and intellectual property and is willing to accept the full security responsibility that comes with it. It decides to train a model from scratch on its own data using Amazon SageMaker. Which scope of the Generative AI Security Scoping Matrix applies?
explanation: Scope 5 - Self-trained Models means training a model from scratch on your data, e.g. Amazon SageMaker; this is the highest scope, with maximum control/ownership but carrying the full security burden. Scope 4 only fine-tunes an existing model (wrong). Scope 3 uses a base model (wrong). Scope 1 uses a public service, with the lowest control (wrong).

question: A security architect is explaining the Generative AI Security Scoping Matrix to stakeholders. Which statement correctly describes the relationship between scope level and security responsibility, and the foundational areas every scope must address?

options.text: As the scope number increases, ownership and control of the model and data increase, so security responsibility increases, and every scope must secure Governance & Compliance, Legal & Privacy, Risk Management, Controls, and Resilience.
As the scope number increases, AWS assumes more of the security responsibility, so higher scopes require the least customer effort.
The scope number only reflects cost and has no relationship to how much security responsibility the customer owns.
Only Scope 5 must address Governance & Compliance and Resilience; lower scopes are exempt from these areas.

explanation: According to the notes, higher scope = more control and security ownership: the higher the scope, the more you own/control the model and data, so the more security responsibility you have. Additionally, ALL scopes must secure across these pillars: Governance & Compliance, Legal & Privacy, Risk Management, Controls, Resilience. B is wrong because customer responsibility increases (not AWS taking on more). C is wrong because scope directly relates to the level of ownership/security responsibility. D is wrong because every scope must address these foundational pillars.

question: A data analytics team stores large volumes of raw log files (CSV and JSON) in Amazon S3. They need to run occasional, ad-hoc SQL queries directly against these files without provisioning any servers, managing clusters, or loading the data into a database first. Which AWS service best meets this requirement?
explanation: According to the notes, Amazon Athena is an interactive query service that lets you run SQL directly on S3 data, serverless (no cluster/infrastructure), fitting ad-hoc SQL over S3 files (CSV/JSON/Parquet) without needing to build a warehouse or load data into a DB. B (Redshift) is wrong because Redshift is for a long-term warehouse/large complex joins, not ad-hoc serverless. C (Glue) is wrong because Glue is an ETL service (preparing/moving data), not for querying. D (EMR) isn't the choice described in the notes for serverless SQL over S3.

question: A company needs to define and store the table schemas that Amazon Athena will use when running SQL queries over data in Amazon S3. Which service provides the schema definitions that Athena reads?
explanation: The notes state clearly: Athena uses the schema from the Glue Data Catalog. This is where the schema definitions are stored that Athena reads when querying. A (Redshift Spectrum) isn't mentioned as a schema source for Athena. C (S3 Inventory) is just a service Athena integrates with, but not a schema source. D (CloudFormation) is an integration but for infrastructure-as-code, not a schema source.

question: During a solution review, an architect must distinguish the primary purpose of Amazon Athena from AWS Glue. According to best practice, how should each service be positioned?

options.text: Athena is used for ETL to prepare and move data, while Glue is used to run SQL queries
Both Athena and Glue are primarily used to run interactive SQL queries on S3
Athena is used to run serverless SQL queries on S3, while Glue is used for ETL to prepare and move data
Athena is a long-term data warehouse, while Glue is used for ad-hoc queries

explanation: The notes emphasize (Exam tip): Athena = query (serverless SQL over S3) vs Glue = ETL (preparing/moving data). So C is correct. A reverses the roles of the two services. B is wrong because Glue is ETL rather than running SQL queries. D is wrong because a long-term warehouse is Redshift, not Athena.

question: A team wants to interactively run data analytics on their S3 data using Apache Spark from within Jupyter-compatible notebooks, all managed inside Amazon Athena. Which Athena capability supports this?
explanation: The notes state that Athena does two things, including 'Apache Spark on Amazon Athena', which lets you interactively run data analytics using Apache Spark, accessed via Jupyter-compatible notebooks. So B is correct. A (Athena SQL / Trino SQL) is for running SQL queries, not a Spark notebook. C (QuickSight) is an integration for BI/visualization, not running Spark. D (JDBC/ODBC) is only an access method for Athena SQL, not Spark.

question: An engineer is reviewing the technical foundations of Amazon Athena SQL. Which TWO statements accurately describe Athena based on its design?

options.text: It is based on the open-source distributed query engine Apache Presto
Athena SQL uses Trino SQL, which is a fork of Apache Presto
It requires loading all S3 data into a managed database before querying
It is billed at a fixed monthly cluster rate regardless of usage
It only supports access through the AWS Management Console

explanation: The notes state: Athena is based on Apache Presto (A correct), and Athena SQL uses Trino SQL, a fork of Apache Presto (B correct). C is wrong because Athena is schema-on-read — it doesn't load data into a DB, it only queries in place. D is wrong because Athena is serverless, paying only for data scanned (pay only for what you use), not a fixed cluster rate. E is wrong because Athena SQL is accessible via the Console, JDBC/ODBC drivers, or CLI/SDKs.

question: A US-based company deploys an automated system that makes critical lending decisions for consumers. Their compliance team is evaluating which regulatory framework specifically requires them to conduct impact assessments and report select documentation to a federal agency. Which framework does this describe?
explanation: The correct answer is B. The notes state clearly that the Algorithmic Accountability Act is a US bill requiring companies to conduct impact assessments of automated decision-making systems and report a selected impact-assessment document to the FTC. A is wrong because the EU AI Act is an EU law, not US. C is wrong because GDPR focuses on privacy/data protection, not impact assessment for automated decisions. D is wrong because FCRA regulates consumer credit reporting rather than an impact-assessment reporting framework to a federal agency as described, and isn't mentioned in the notes.

question: Under the Algorithmic Accountability Act, which US federal agency is responsible for creating the structured assessment and reporting regulations that companies must follow?
explanation: The correct answer is C. The notes state clearly that the bill requires the FTC to create rules for structured assessment/reporting, and companies must report a selected impact-assessment document to the FTC. A, B, D are other federal agencies, but aren't mentioned in the notes as the responsible agency for this framework.

question: A team is comparing the Algorithmic Accountability Act with the EU AI Act and GDPR to understand its distinguishing focus. Which statement best captures the primary emphasis of the Algorithmic Accountability Act as described?

options.text: It is an enacted EU law governing high-risk AI systems across member states
It is a proposed US bill focused on fairness/bias and transparency of automated decision-making
It is a privacy and data-protection regulation restricting how personal data is processed
It is a voluntary industry standard for documenting model training data

explanation: The correct answer is B. The notes emphasize this is a US bill (proposed), focusing on bias/fairness and transparency, distinct from the EU AI Act and GDPR. A describes the EU AI Act (already an EU law), not this Act. C describes GDPR (privacy/data protection). D is incorrect because this is a legislative bill, not a voluntary industry standard.

question: During a review, a stakeholder claims the Algorithmic Accountability Act only applies to companies that build AI models, not those that merely use them. Based on the described scope of the bill, why is this claim incorrect?

options.text: The bill applies only to companies that sell AI models to the government
The bill makes both decision-makers who use systems and technology builders responsible for assessing impact
The bill exempts model builders and targets only consumer-facing deployers
The bill applies exclusively to companies headquartered within the EU

explanation: The correct answer is B. The notes state the bill assigns responsibility to both decision-makers (using the model) and technology builders for conducting impact assessments, applying to AI systems that a company uses and sells. A is wrong because it's not limited to selling to government. C is wrong because builders aren't exempt. D is wrong because this is a US bill, not limited to the EU.

question: According to the described goals of the Algorithmic Accountability Act of 2023, which outcomes does the bill aim to achieve? (Choose TWO.)

options.text: Require companies to assess the impacts of the AI systems they use and sell
Create new transparency about when and how automated systems are used, empowering consumers to make informed choices
Mandate that all AI models be open-sourced for public inspection
Establish criminal penalties and prison sentences for algorithmic bias
Replace GDPR as the primary global data-protection standard

explanation: The correct answers are A and B. The notes state clearly that the Act of 2023 requires companies to assess the impact of AI systems they use and sell (A), and creates new transparency about when/how such systems are used, empowering consumers to make informed choices (B). C is wrong because the notes don't mention an open-source requirement. D is wrong because the notes don't mention criminal penalties/imprisonment. E is wrong because this Act is a US bill on fairness/transparency and doesn't replace GDPR (the EU's privacy framework).

question: A data analyst needs to create a table in Amazon Athena but wants the schema to be discovered automatically by scanning the underlying data instead of writing it by hand. Which approach should the analyst use?

options.text: Run an AWS Glue crawler that crawls the data to produce a table schema
Write a SQL CREATE TABLE statement that defines every column manually
Use the AWS Glue Wizard to type in the column definitions
Query the data directly from Amazon S3 without creating any table

explanation: According to the notes, a table can be created automatically by an AWS Glue crawler, a tool that crawls the data to generate the table's schema, so A is correct. B (SQL CREATE TABLE) and C (Glue Wizard) are two manual table-creation methods where the user must define the schema themselves rather than auto-discovering it. D isn't a table-creation method and isn't mentioned in the notes.

question: When a user creates a table in Amazon Athena, in which other AWS service does that table also exist?
explanation: The notes state clearly that an Athena table is exactly a table in the AWS Glue Data Catalog, existing in both services when you create an Athena table, so B is correct. A (S3) is where the source data (LOCATION) is stored, not where the table definition is stored. C (Redshift) and D (DynamoDB) aren't mentioned in the notes.

question: A user is writing an Athena query and needs to reference a table using its fully qualified name. According to the standard convention, which value should be used as the catalog part of the FROM clause?
explanation: According to the notes, when querying FROM you use AWSDataCatalog, e.g. FROM "AWSDataCatalog"."your_database_name"."your_table_name", so C is correct. B (default) is the default database name where a table is typically created, not the catalog part. A and D are fabricated names that don't appear in the notes.

question: A team is creating an Athena table with a SQL CREATE EXTERNAL TABLE statement for log data that must be parsed with a regular expression. Which two elements must the statement specify? (Choose TWO.)

options.text: How to parse each row, for example using a regex SerDe
The S3 location of the data with a LOCATION clause
The number of Athena query engine nodes to provision
The IAM role that the Glue crawler will assume
A backup retention period for the table data

explanation: The notes state clearly: when using SQL you specify how to parse each row (possibly via regex, e.g. ROW FORMAT SERDE 'org.apache.hadoop.hive.serde2.RegexSerDe') and the data's location (LOCATION 's3://...'), so A and B are correct. C (number of engine nodes) doesn't need to be declared and isn't in the notes; Athena is serverless. D refers to a Glue crawler, not the CREATE TABLE statement. E (backup retention time) isn't mentioned.

question: A data engineer is defining an Athena table over VPC flow logs and wants to store a column that holds IPv4 addresses so it can be used in query filters. When they attempt to specify the ipaddress type in the CREATE TABLE (DDL) statement, the statement fails. What is the correct explanation of how the ipaddress type behaves in Athena?

options.text: The ipaddress type can be used in DML queries but is not supported in DDL statements such as CREATE TABLE.
The ipaddress type is only supported in DDL statements and cannot be referenced in DML queries.
The ipaddress type must first be cast from a binary column stored in Parquet before it can be used.
The ipaddress type is a Hive string literal that requires single or double quotes in DDL.

explanation: According to the notes, ipaddress is used for an IP address in DML queries and is 'not supported for DDL'. So A is correct: usable in DML but not supported in DDL like CREATE TABLE, matching the error encountered. B is entirely reversed, so it's wrong. C confuses it with binary (used in parquet) and is unrelated to ipaddress. D describes the string type (a Hive data type), not ipaddress.

question: A developer notices that in Athena the integer type is written differently depending on the statement being used. According to the type reference, which keyword form should be used in a DDL CREATE TABLE statement versus a DQL SELECT statement for a 32-bit signed integer?

options.text: Use Integer in CREATE TABLE (DDL) and Int in SELECT (DQL).
Use Int in CREATE TABLE (DDL) and Integer in SELECT (DQL).
Use bigint in both CREATE TABLE and SELECT for a 32-bit value.
Use smallint in CREATE TABLE and integer in SELECT.
Use Int in both CREATE TABLE and SELECT interchangeably.

explanation: The notes state clearly: 'Int used in DDL Create Table, Integer used in DQL SELECT'. So B is correct. A reverses the two, so it's wrong. C is wrong because bigint is a 64-bit type, not another way to write a 32-bit integer. D is wrong because smallint is a completely different 16-bit type. E is wrong because the two statements use different forms (Int vs Integer), not interchangeable at will.

question: An analyst is choosing exact-length and variable-length string types plus a fixed-precision numeric type for a financial table in Athena. Based on the Athena data type limits, which TWO statements are correct?

options.text: varchar(n) is variable length and supports a length from 1 to 65535.
decimal(precision, scale) allows a maximum of 38 for both precision and scale.
char(n) is a variable-length character type with no upper length bound.
varchar(n) is fixed length and always padded to 65535 characters.
decimal(precision, scale) allows a maximum precision of 65535.

explanation: The notes: varchar(n) is 'variable length, 1 to 65535' so A is correct; decimal(precision, scale) has 'precision and scale both max 38' so B is correct. C is wrong because char(n) is fixed-length, not variable-length. D is wrong because varchar is variable length, not fixed and doesn't pad up to 65535. E is wrong because the 65535 limit is for varchar, while decimal precision's max is 38.

question: A data engineer needs to store whole numbers that will never exceed a few hundred and wants to minimize storage by picking the smallest sufficient Athena integer type. Which type stores an 8-bit signed integer with a range of -128 to 127?
explanation: The notes: tinyint is an '8-bit signed integer (-128 to 127)', matching the description exactly, so C is correct. A smallint is 16-bit (-32,768 to 32,767), so it's wrong. B bigint is 64-bit, so it's wrong. D integer is 32-bit, so it's wrong.

question: A data engineering team needs to run a distributed big data ETL pipeline on very large datasets using AWS Glue Jobs. Which Glue Job engine is the most appropriate choice for this workload?
explanation: According to the notes, Spark is chosen for distributed big data, fitting an ETL pipeline over very large datasets. Python Shell is used for small/lightweight tasks, so it doesn't fit big data. Ray is distributed Python/ML, not the primary choice for general big-data ETL. Jupyter Notebook isn't an engine, just a way to create a Glue Job.

question: A developer wants to run a small, lightweight data processing task on AWS Glue Jobs without the overhead of a distributed cluster. According to the engine selection guidance, which engine best fits this requirement?
explanation: The notes state clearly that Python Shell is used for small/lightweight tasks, matching the requirement of a lightweight task not needing a distributed cluster. Spark is for distributed big data, Ray is for distributed Python/ML, and Spark Streaming is also part of the distributed Spark family — all overkill for a small task.

question: A team runs a standard (non-streaming) Spark job on AWS Glue. Based on the default resource allocation, how many Data Processing Units (DPUs) are allocated to this job?
explanation: According to the notes, each Spark job is allocated 10 DPUs. The number 2 DPUs is for a Spark Streaming job, and 6 M-DPUs is for a Ray job. 1 DPU doesn't appear in the notes.

question: A cloud engineer is exploring the available authoring options for creating AWS Glue Jobs before writing an ETL pipeline. Which of the following are valid ways to create Glue Jobs? (Choose TWO.)

options.text: Visual ETL using AWS Glue Studio

explanation: The notes list three ways to create Glue Jobs: Visual ETL (AWS Glue Studio), Jupyter Notebooks, and Script Editor (within AWS). So Visual ETL (Glue Studio) and Script Editor are the two correct answers. AWS Lambda, SageMaker Autopilot, and QuickSight aren't mentioned as ways to create Glue Jobs in the notes.

question: A data engineering team needs to discover, prepare, move, and integrate data from more than 70 diverse sources into a data lake before it can be used for machine learning. They want a serverless service that requires no infrastructure to manage. Which AWS service best fits this requirement?
explanation: According to the notes, AWS Glue is a serverless data-integration service that helps discover, prepare, move, and integrate data from over 70 sources into a data lake/warehouse, without managing infrastructure. Athena and Redshift Spectrum are tools for querying already-cataloged data rather than the primary integration/ETL service. EMR is a big-data processing platform, but the notes position Glue as the choice for serverless ETL/ELT to integrate data.

question: An analytics team has loaded transformed data into a data lake using AWS Glue. They now want to immediately search and query the cataloged data using SQL, and also access the same metadata from EMR and Redshift Spectrum without redefining schemas. Which capability of AWS Glue makes this shared metadata access possible?

options.text: Glue Crawlers, which automatically move data between services
The Glue Data Catalog, a centralized metadata store shared across Athena, EMR, and Redshift Spectrum
Glue ETL jobs, which run Spark and Python transformations
AWS Glue DataBrew, which visually cleanses data
Glue Data Quality, which validates records

explanation: The notes emphasize that the Data Catalog is a shared metadata repository used by Amazon Athena, Amazon EMR, and Amazon Redshift Spectrum, letting you query cataloged data without redefining the schema. The Crawler only auto-discovers the schema (doesn't move data). ETL jobs perform transforms (Spark/Python/Ray). DataBrew is used for visual cleansing, Data Quality is used for quality checks — none is a shared metadata repository.

question: Within AWS Glue, which component is specifically responsible for automatically detecting the schema of source data and populating the Data Catalog?
explanation: According to the notes, Crawler = auto-discovers the schema, a component that automatically detects the schema of source data and populates it into the Data Catalog. ETL jobs (Spark/Python/Ray) perform transforms. DataBrew is used for visual data cleansing. Data Quality is used to assess data quality. Only the Crawler handles schema discovery.

question: A team is deciding between AWS Glue and other AWS analytics services. According to the guidance, in which situation should they specifically choose AWS Glue?

options.text: When they only need to run ad hoc SQL queries directly against data with no transformation
When they need to transform and integrate data from multiple sources, not just query it
When they need to build interactive business intelligence dashboards
When they need a fully managed relational database for transactional workloads

explanation: The notes state clearly that Glue should be chosen when you need to transform/integrate data, not just query it. If you only need to run SQL queries without transformation, a tool like Athena fits better. Building a BI dashboard or managing a transactional relational database is outside Glue's scope per the notes (Glue = ETL + Catalog).

question: According to the notes, which of the following are stated use cases for AWS Glue? (Choose TWO.)
explanation: The notes list Glue's use cases as including: analytics, machine learning, and application development. So Analytics and Machine learning are the two correct answers. Real-time video streaming, managed message queuing, and blockchain ledger management aren't mentioned in the notes as Glue use cases.

question: A data engineering team wants to build ETL pipelines quickly using a drag-and-drop interface, with little to no coding, while still generating a Spark script they can customize. Which AWS service best fits this requirement?
explanation: The notes state clearly that AWS Glue Studio (Visual ETL) lets you build an ETL pipeline visually with a drag-and-drop interface with little/no code, and it automatically generates a PySpark script for data engineers. DataBrew (B) is a no-code data-prep tool for analysts and does NOT generate a script. Athena (C) and Lambda (D) aren't mentioned as visual ETL-building tools in the notes.

question: In AWS Glue Studio, a pipeline is composed of nodes. Which node type represents the transformations you want to apply to the data?
explanation: According to the notes, a pipeline consists of 3 node types: Sources (the data used), Transforms (what you want to do with the data), and Targets (where the data is sent). So Transforms (C) is the node describing data transformations. Sources (A) is the input data, Targets (B) is the destination. Triggers (D) isn't a node type mentioned in the notes.

question: A team wants to apply version control to the ETL pipelines they build in AWS Glue Studio. According to AWS Glue Studio's supported integrations, which services can they use? (Choose TWO)
explanation: The notes list the version-control services for a pipeline as including: AWS CodeCommit, GitHub, GitLab, and BitBucket. So CodeCommit (A) and GitHub (B) are both correct. Amazon S3 versioning (C), CloudFormation (D), and DynamoDB (E) aren't among the version-control options listed.

question: An analyst who cannot write code needs a no-code tool offering 250+ prebuilt transformations for data preparation, and specifically does NOT want a Spark script to be generated. Which service should they choose instead of AWS Glue Studio?
explanation: The notes clearly distinguish: Glue Studio is Visual ETL for developers and GENERATES a Spark script, while DataBrew is no-code data prep for analysts with 250+ transforms and does NOT generate a script. Since the requirement is no-code for an analyst, 250+ transforms, and not wanting a generated script, the answer is DataBrew (B). Glue Studio (A) generates a script, so it doesn't fit. SageMaker (C) and CodeCommit (D) aren't mentioned in this context.

question: A developer downloads the Python script produced by the AWS Glue Studio Visual ETL to run it locally. To execute it successfully, what must they do, and what will the script contain?

options.text: Install the required AWS Glue Libraries; the script uses awsglue imports, GlueContext, SparkContext, DynamicFrame, and job.commit()
Install the Amazon SageMaker SDK; the script uses boto3 clients and pandas DataFrames only
Enable Amazon S3 versioning; the script uses SQL statements executed through Athena
Install AWS DataBrew locally; the script uses 250+ prebuilt transforms with no imports

explanation: The notes say the Python script generated by Visual ETL can be downloaded and run, but you must install the AWS Glue Libraries (github.com/awslabs/aws-glue-libs); the script uses awsglue imports, GlueContext, SparkContext, DynamicFrame, and job.commit(). So A is correct. B, C, D mention libraries/concepts (SageMaker SDK, S3 versioning/Athena SQL, DataBrew) that don't match the description in the notes.

question: A data team runs a data lake on Amazon S3 and needs a single, centralized place to store and share schema, table, and database metadata that can be queried by Amazon Athena, Amazon EMR, Redshift Spectrum, and AWS Glue ETL jobs. Which AWS service should they use?
explanation: The AWS Glue Data Catalog is a shared metadata store (schema/table/database), compatible with the Apache Hive Metastore, and one catalog serves multiple engines (Athena, EMR, Redshift Spectrum, Glue ETL) that all read from it — matching the need for a 'centralized schema directory' for a data lake. A (Glue ETL) is a data-transformation service, not a metadata store. C (Crawler) only auto-discovers the schema to populate the Catalog, not the central storage itself. D (Athena) is a query engine, which reads metadata from the Catalog rather than being the catalog itself.

question: A new engineer claims that storing table definitions in the AWS Glue Data Catalog means the actual records are moved out of Amazon S3 and into the catalog. How should this be corrected?

options.text: Correct — the Data Catalog physically stores the data records for faster queries
The Data Catalog stores only metadata; the actual data remains in Amazon S3
The data is duplicated into both the Data Catalog and Amazon S3
The data is moved into AWS Glue ETL storage while metadata stays in S3
The Data Catalog stores data only for Apache Iceberg tables

explanation: According to the notes: the Data Catalog = metadata, does NOT contain the actual data, which stays in S3. So B is correct. A is wrong because the catalog doesn't store data records. C is wrong because there's no duplicating data into the catalog. D is wrong because Glue is ETL, not a place that stores data instead of S3. E is wrong because the catalog only stores metadata for every table type, including Iceberg, not the actual data.

question: A team stores raw files in Amazon S3 but does not yet have any table definitions in their AWS Glue Data Catalog. They want the schema to be discovered automatically and used to populate the Glue tables. Which AWS Glue component performs this?
explanation: AWS Glue Crawler auto-discovers schema formats to define Glue tables, i.e. automatically populating metadata into the Catalog — matching the requirement. A (database) is just a container holding multiple Glue tables, and doesn't discover schemas. B (table) is the metadata definition representing the data, the result rather than the discovery tool. D (ETL job) is used to transform data, not to auto-discover a schema.

question: When creating a standard AWS Glue table (not an Apache Iceberg table), which of the following are true according to the table's requirements and supported sources? (Choose TWO.)

options.text: You must specify a data format such as Avro, CSV, JSON, XML, Parquet, or ORC
Data can be sourced from Amazon S3, Amazon Kinesis, or Apache Kafka
It uses its own expressive SQL data format instead of specifying a format
It can only source data from Amazon RDS
It automatically stores the underlying data records inside the table definition

explanation: For a standard AWS Glue table, the notes state clearly: you must specify a data format (Avro, CSV, JSON, XML, Parquet, ORC) — so A is correct; and data comes from Amazon S3, Kinesis, Kafka — so B is correct. C describes an Apache Iceberg table (using its own SQL-based format), not a standard table. D is wrong because the sources are S3/Kinesis/Kafka, not limited to RDS. E is wrong because a table is just a metadata definition and doesn't store the actual data.

question: A data engineer needs to organize related metadata by placing multiple AWS Glue tables together in a logical container. Which AWS Glue construct provides this container?
explanation: An AWS Glue database is a container holding multiple AWS Glue tables — matching its role of grouping/organizing tables. A (Crawler) only discovers schemas to define tables, not a container. C (Iceberg table) is a specific table type, not a container holding multiple tables. D (ETL job) is used to transform data, not to organize metadata.

question: A data engineering team stores raw datasets in Amazon S3, and new files with evolving structures are added frequently. The team wants the AWS Glue Data Catalog to stay current automatically without anyone writing schema definitions by hand. Which AWS Glue capability should they use?

options.text: An AWS Glue crawler that scans the data source, infers the schema, and populates tables in the Data Catalog
An AWS Glue crawler that runs SQL queries directly against the S3 data
An AWS Glue crawler that transforms the raw data into a cleaned, analytics-ready format
A manually authored Data Catalog table definition that is edited each time files change

explanation: According to the notes, an AWS Glue Data Crawler automatically scans data sources, infers the schema, and creates/updates tables in the Data Catalog, eliminating manual schema definition. So A is correct. B is wrong because a crawler isn't a place to query data. C is wrong because a crawler doesn't transform data. D is wrong because avoiding manual schema definition is exactly what a crawler replaces.

question: During an architecture review, an engineer claims that an AWS Glue crawler is the component that runs analytical queries and reshapes datasets in a data pipeline. According to the primary purpose of a Glue crawler, how should this claim be corrected?

options.text: The crawler executes queries against the data but does not transform it
The crawler transforms the data but does not execute queries against it
The crawler discovers the schema to populate the Data Catalog; it neither queries nor transforms the data
The crawler both queries and transforms the data as part of catalog population

explanation: The notes emphasize: Crawler = discovering the schema (populating the Catalog), not a place to query or transform data. So C is correct. A, B, D all assign the crawler a query or transform function that the notes state clearly it doesn't perform.

question: A team needs their AWS Glue crawler to update the Data Catalog both automatically at regular intervals and manually right after a large one-time data load. Which statement about how a Glue crawler can be triggered is correct?

options.text: It can run only on a fixed schedule
It can run only on demand when manually started
It can run either on a schedule or on demand
It runs continuously and cannot be scheduled or started manually

explanation: The notes state clearly that a Data Crawler can run on a schedule or on-demand. So C is correct, satisfying both the need for automatic periodic runs and manual triggering. A and B are each only half correct, so they are wrong. D is wrong because a crawler doesn't run continuously and can be scheduled or manually triggered.

question: A solutions architect is confirming which data stores an AWS Glue crawler can connect to in order to catalog their schemas. Based on the supported data sources, which TWO of the following can a Glue crawler crawl? (Choose TWO.)
explanation: The notes list the sources a crawler can connect to as including Amazon S3 and DynamoDB (along with JDBC sources like Redshift/Snowflake/RDS, MongoDB/DocumentDB, Delta Lake, Iceberg, Hudi). So A and B are correct. C (SQS), D (SNS), and E (CloudWatch Logs) aren't among the listed data sources, so they are wrong.

question: A data engineering team wants to enforce data quality checks on their AWS Glue Data Catalog and ETL pipelines before the data is used to train machine learning models. They need a way to define specific data quality rules declaratively. Which AWS service and capability should they use?

options.text: AWS Glue DataBrew to visually transform and clean the data
AWS Glue Data Quality using DQDL to define data quality rules
Amazon Athena to query the data for null values
AWS Glue Crawlers to catalog the data schema

explanation: According to the notes, AWS Glue Data Quality measures and monitors data quality, using DQDL (Data Quality Definition Language) to define rules and enforce checks on the Data Catalog and Glue ETL pipelines, fitting the need to ensure clean data before training ML. A is wrong because DataBrew is used to transform/clean data, not define quality rules. C (Athena) and D (Crawlers) aren't described in the notes as data-quality rule-definition tools.

question: An exam question asks about the underlying technology of AWS Glue Data Quality. On which open-source framework is AWS Glue Data Quality built?

options.text: The AWS open-source DeeQu unit test framework, built on Apache Spark unit tests

explanation: The notes state clearly that Glue Data Quality is built on the open-source DeeQu framework (based on Apache Spark unit tests). B, C, D aren't mentioned as the foundation of Glue Data Quality in the notes.

question: After running an AWS Glue Data Quality evaluation on a dataset, what output does the team receive to understand the overall health of their data, and what additional benefit does it provide?

options.text: A transformed dataset with all invalid rows automatically removed
A trained ML model ready for deployment to an endpoint
A Data Quality score that overviews data health and helps identify the exact records that lowered scores
A cost and usage report showing pipeline spend

explanation: According to the notes, after evaluation you get an overall Data Quality score reflecting data health and helping pinpoint exactly which records are lowering the score. A describes DataBrew-like behavior (cleaning/transforming), not the output of Data Quality. B and D aren't mentioned in the notes as results of a data-quality evaluation.

question: A team needs to detect hard-to-detect data quality issues and anomalies that are not easily captured by simple static rules. Which built-in capability of AWS Glue Data Quality addresses this need?

options.text: It uses machine learning (ML) to detect anomalies and hard-to-detect data quality issues
It requires manually writing SQL queries for every anomaly
It relies solely on the 25+ out-of-the-box rules with no anomaly detection
It exports data to Amazon QuickSight for visual inspection

explanation: The notes state that Glue Data Quality uses ML to detect anomalies and hard-to-find quality issues. C is wrong because besides rules, it also uses ML for anomaly detection. B and D aren't mentioned in the notes.

question: A company is evaluating AWS Glue Data Quality for their pipelines. Which TWO statements accurately describe characteristics of AWS Glue Data Quality according to the service description? (Choose TWO.)

options.text: It is serverless, so you pay for what you use
It provides 25+ out-of-the-box data quality rules and lets you create rules for specific needs
It is primarily used to visually transform and clean data like AWS Glue DataBrew
It requires provisioning and managing dedicated Spark clusters continuously
It replaces the need for an AWS Glue Data Catalog

explanation: The notes confirm that Glue Data Quality is serverless (pay for what you use) and provides 25+ built-in rules while allowing custom rule creation as needed. C is wrong because transforming/cleaning data is DataBrew's role, distinct from Data Quality (quality/reliability). D is wrong because a serverless service doesn't require self-managing a cluster. E is wrong because Data Quality enforces checks ON the Data Catalog rather than replacing it.

question: A data engineering team needs to store vast amounts of raw log files, JSON documents, and images from many different sources without defining a fixed schema up front. They want to apply structure only when the data is queried. Which characteristic best describes the storage approach they are looking for?

options.text: Schema-on-write, where structure is enforced before data is stored
Schema-on-read, where raw data is stored first and schema is applied at query time
Fixed relational schema enforced at ingestion for structured data only
Columnar compression applied automatically during data loading

explanation: According to the notes, the key characteristic of a data lake is schema-on-read: storing raw data first, applying the schema when queried. This is exactly what the team needs. A (schema-on-write) is a characteristic of a data warehouse, not a data lake. C is wrong because a data lake holds every type of data (structured/semi/unstructured), not only structured, and doesn't force a relational schema on ingest. D isn't mentioned in the notes.

question: A company wants to build a centralized repository to store structured, semi-structured, and unstructured data in raw form at large scale on AWS, primarily using objects (blobs) or files as the storage medium. Which AWS service is typically used as the foundation for this data lake?
explanation: The notes state clearly: on AWS a data lake is typically S3, using objects (blobs) or files as the storage medium. A (Redshift) is a data warehouse (processed, schema-on-write, structured) rather than a data lake. B (RDS) and D (DynamoDB) aren't mentioned as a data-lake foundation in the notes.

question: An organization is comparing a data lake with a data warehouse to decide where to store their processed, structured reporting data that requires schema enforcement before loading. Based on these characteristics, which option correctly matches the workload to the right solution?

options.text: A data lake, because it uses schema-on-write for structured data
A data warehouse such as Amazon Redshift, because it stores processed, structured data with schema-on-write
A data lake on Amazon S3, because it enforces schema before writing structured data
A data warehouse such as Amazon S3, because it stores raw data with schema-on-read
A data lake, because it only stores structured data after processing

explanation: The notes distinguish: a data warehouse = processed, schema-on-write, structured, Redshift; while a data lake = raw, schema-on-read, any format, S3. The requirement here is processed, structured data, enforcing a schema before loading -> exactly a data warehouse (Redshift), so B is correct. A, C, E incorrectly assign schema-on-write/structured attributes to a data lake. D incorrectly labels S3 as a data warehouse.

question: A team is designing their data lake pipeline and reaches the stage where they need to change or blend collected data into new semi-structured data using ELT/ETL engines. According to the standard data lake stages, which stage does this describe?
explanation: According to the notes, the Transform stage is 'change or blend data into new semi-structured data using ELT/ETL engines' — matching the description exactly. A (Collect) is pulling data from sources. C (Publish) is putting the dataset into meta catalogs so analysts can find it. D (Distribution) is allowing programs/APIs to access the data.

question: In a data lake architecture, an organization wants analysts to be able to quickly find useful datasets, and also wants various programs and APIs to be able to access the data. Which two data lake stages address these two needs? (Choose TWO.)

options.text: Collect, which pulls data from various data sources
Transform, which blends data into new semi-structured data using ELT/ETL engines
Publish, which publishes datasets to meta catalogs so analysts can quickly find useful data
Distribution, which allows access to data by various programs or APIs
Ingest, which validates schema before storing structured records

explanation: The notes list 4 stages: Publish = putting the dataset into meta catalogs so analysts can quickly find useful data (satisfying requirement 1), and Distribution = allowing programs/APIs to access the data (satisfying requirement 2) -> C and D are correct. A (Collect) and B (Transform) are other stages that don't solve these two needs. E (Ingest with schema validation) doesn't exist in the notes, and describes schema-on-write, which isn't part of a data lake.

question: A data analyst on a marketing team needs to clean and normalize a large set of raw customer records for an upcoming analytics project. The analyst is not a developer and wants to avoid writing any Spark or Python code. Which AWS service best fits this requirement?

options.text: AWS Glue Studio, to build the pipeline with a visual editor that generates Spark scripts
AWS Glue DataBrew, a no-code visual tool for cleaning and normalizing data
AWS Glue Data Quality, to validate the quality of the dataset
Amazon SageMaker, to write a custom preprocessing script in a notebook

explanation: According to the notes, AWS Glue DataBrew is a no-code data-prep tool that lets you clean and normalize data through a visual interface without writing code, for analysts/data scientists who don't code -> B is correct. A is wrong because Glue Studio is visual ETL but generates a Spark script (for engineers). C is wrong because Data Quality is used to validate data quality, not clean/normalize. D is wrong because writing a script in a notebook is still code, contradicting the no-code requirement.

question: A team is comparing AWS Glue DataBrew, AWS Glue Studio, and AWS Glue Data Quality to choose the right tool. Which statement correctly distinguishes AWS Glue Studio from DataBrew?

options.text: Glue Studio is a no-code tool for analysts, while DataBrew generates Spark code for engineers
Glue Studio validates the quality of raw data, while DataBrew cleans and normalizes it
Glue Studio is a visual ETL tool that generates code for engineers, while DataBrew is no-code data prep for analysts
Both Glue Studio and DataBrew require analysts to write Spark scripts manually

explanation: The notes clearly distinguish: DataBrew = no-code prep (analyst); Glue Studio = visual ETL generating code (engineer); Data Quality = quality validation -> C is correct. A reverses the roles of the two tools, so it's wrong. B describes Glue Studio as if it were Data Quality, so it's wrong. D is wrong because DataBrew is entirely no-code, without needing to write Spark manually.

question: An organization wants to reduce the time its analysts and data scientists spend preparing data for analytics and machine learning. According to the capabilities of AWS Glue DataBrew, by how much can it reduce data preparation time and how does it help automate tasks?

options.text: By up to 80 percent, using over 250 ready-made transformations
By up to 50 percent, using over 100 ready-made transformations
By up to 80 percent, but only after engineers write custom Spark scripts
By up to 25 percent, using a fixed set of 10 built-in transformations

explanation: The notes state that DataBrew helps reduce data-prep time for analytics and ML by up to 80 percent, and it has over 250 built-in transforms for automation -> A is correct. B, D have the wrong numbers (50%/100, 25%/10 aren't in the notes). C is wrong because DataBrew is entirely no-code, without requiring an engineer to write a Spark script.

question: A startup with an unpredictable data preparation workload wants a tool that requires no infrastructure management and charges only for actual usage. Which characteristic of AWS Glue DataBrew addresses this?

options.text: It runs on dedicated EC2 instances that must be provisioned in advance
It requires a fixed monthly subscription regardless of usage
It is a serverless offering where you pay for what you use
It requires reserving Spark clusters to guarantee capacity

explanation: The notes state clearly that DataBrew is a serverless offering, pay for what you use -> C is correct, fitting unpredictable workloads and not requiring infrastructure management. A is wrong because serverless doesn't require pre-provisioning EC2. B is wrong because it charges by usage rather than a fixed monthly fee. D is wrong because there's no need to reserve a Spark cluster.

question: A software company wants to use a single foundation model to summarize support tickets, translate documentation, and generate code snippets, without retraining the model for each task. Which advantage of generative AI does this scenario best illustrate?
explanation: According to the notes (A.1), adaptability is a single foundation model's ability to handle many tasks (summarize, translate, code, chat) without retraining, just by changing the prompt — matching this scenario. Responsiveness is near-real-time response (the wrong focus). Interpretability and nondeterminism are limitations (A.2), not the advantage described.

question: A financial services team requires that submitting the exact same prompt must return the exact same output every single time so results can be reproduced and tested reliably. Which characteristic of generative AI makes it a poor fit for this hard requirement?
explanation: The notes (A.2 and the exam tip) state clearly that nondeterminism means the same prompt can produce different answers (due to sampling/temperature), making it hard to reproduce and test; if 'same input → same output' is a strict requirement, GenAI doesn't fit. Hallucinations are fabricating information, inaccuracy means factually wrong/out of date — neither is about reproducibility. Simplicity is an advantage.

question: A chatbot built on a large language model confidently produces an answer that sounds plausible and well-formed but contains completely fabricated facts. Which limitation of generative AI does this describe?
explanation: According to the notes (A.2), hallucination is producing information that sounds plausible but is wrong/fabricated — exactly matching the 'plausible but fabricated' description. Nondeterminism is about the same prompt giving different outputs. Cross-domain performance and adaptability are advantages/metrics, not reliability risks.

question: A product manager wants to measure the BUSINESS impact of a newly launched generative AI feature on the company's e-commerce site. Which metrics are most appropriate for this goal? (Choose TWO.)
explanation: The notes (A.4 and the exam tip) distinguish business metrics (ARPU, CLV, conversion rate, ROI) from model metrics (accuracy, AUC, F1). To measure business impact, choose conversion rate and ARPU. F1 and AUC are model metrics (item C), not measures of commercial impact. Interpretability is a technical limitation, not a business metric.

question: A business analyst with no machine learning background needs to build a text-generation prototype quickly using natural language prompts instead of writing code or building an ML pipeline. Which advantage of generative AI does this reflect?
explanation: According to the notes (A.1 and the exam tip), simplicity lowers the technical barrier — using natural language instead of writing code/an ML pipeline, shortening time-to-market; matching 'easy to use, no ML expert needed'. Interpretability and nondeterminism are limitations. Cross-domain performance is a metric about performing well across many domains, not about ease of use.

question: A team is selecting a generative AI model for a healthcare application that must satisfy data residency rules and industry regulations. Which selection factor most directly addresses these requirements?
explanation: The notes (A.3) list compliance as including data residency, industry regulations, licensing, and privacy — matching the requirement for data residency and healthcare industry regulations. Performance requirements are latency/throughput/quality. Model type is choosing the modality (text/image/embedding). Capabilities are multilingual support, context length, reasoning, code — not legal constraints.

question: A company stores analytics data in a data lake on Amazon S3. The security team needs to grant and revoke access to specific tables in a way similar to a relational database, controlling which users can query which data. Which AWS service is designed to provide this fine-grained governance layer for the data lake?
explanation: According to the notes, AWS Lake Formation is a data-lake governance/security service, providing an RDBMS-style grant/revoke permission model for fine-grained access control over a data lake on S3. Glue Data Catalog (A) only manages metadata, not the permissions layer. Athena (C) and Redshift Spectrum (D) are query engines where Lake Formation's permissions are enforced, not the governance layer itself.

question: A data engineer states that AWS Lake Formation will replace the need for IAM policies in their environment. According to how Lake Formation works, why is this statement incorrect?

options.text: Lake Formation only manages metadata and has no permissions model of its own.
Lake Formation provides a permissions model that augments the IAM permissions model, not replaces it.
Lake Formation permissions apply only to Amazon EC2 instances, so IAM is still required for S3.
Lake Formation can only be used after all IAM policies are deleted from the account.
Lake Formation delegates all access decisions entirely to IAM without adding any controls.

explanation: The notes state clearly that Lake Formation provides its own permission model to AUGMENT the IAM model rather than replace it, via a simple RDBMS-style grant/revoke mechanism. A is wrong because managing metadata belongs to the Glue Data Catalog, while Lake Formation has its own permission model. C and D are fabricated and not in the notes. E is wrong because Lake Formation adds a fine-grained control layer rather than handing everything over to IAM.

question: A governance team wants to restrict analysts so they can only see certain columns and certain rows within a table in the data lake. At what levels can AWS Lake Formation enforce granular access controls?

options.text: At the column, row, and cell levels
Only at the S3 bucket level

explanation: The notes state clearly that Lake Formation enforces permissions with fine-grained controls at the column, row, and cell level. A, B, D all describe coarser levels of control (database, whole table, or bucket) and don't reflect the fine-grained capability described in the notes.

question: A company defines fine-grained permissions in AWS Lake Formation. Across which of the following services are these permissions enforced when users query the data lake? (Choose TWO.)
explanation: The notes list Lake Formation as enforcing permissions on Athena, QuickSight, Redshift Spectrum, EMR, and Glue — so Athena (A) and Redshift Spectrum (C) are both correct. SageMaker Ground Truth (B), Rekognition (D), and Comprehend (E) aren't among the engines Lake Formation applies permissions to per the notes.

question: An architect notes that AWS Lake Formation and AWS Glue can operate together on the same set of table definitions. What is the correct relationship between them regarding metadata?

options.text: Lake Formation and AWS Glue share the same Data Catalog, with Lake Formation adding fine-grained governance.
Lake Formation maintains a separate catalog that must be manually synchronized with Glue.
AWS Glue handles fine-grained permissions while Lake Formation stores the metadata.
Neither service uses a Data Catalog; both rely solely on S3 object tags.
Lake Formation replaces the Glue Data Catalog entirely once enabled.

explanation: The notes emphasize that AWS Lake Formation and AWS Glue share the same Data Catalog; the Glue Data Catalog handles metadata while Lake Formation adds the fine-grained governance/permissions layer. B is wrong because it's not a separate catalog requiring manual syncing. C reverses the roles (Glue = metadata, Lake Formation = permissions). D is fabricated. E is wrong because Lake Formation augments rather than replaces the catalog.

question: A machine learning team has finished tuning a fraud-detection model and reports strong technical results. Leadership now wants a single measure that captures the overall value the project delivers to the business relative to what was spent. Which metric BEST answers this need?
explanation: According to the notes, ROI (Return on Investment) is the overall measure of a project's value: the return gained relative to the cost spent, matching the requirement for 'overall value relative to what was spent'. AUC, F1, and Accuracy are all model (technical) metrics reflecting only how good the model is technically, not the business value.

question: A team builds a classifier on a highly imbalanced dataset where the positive class is rare. A manager points out that the model reports a very high accuracy figure. Why should the team be cautious about relying on accuracy here, and which metric is better suited?

options.text: Accuracy can be misleading on imbalanced data; F1 score is better suited for a rare class
Accuracy is misleading only on balanced data; ROI is better suited for a rare class
Accuracy is always the most reliable metric; no alternative is needed
Accuracy cannot be computed on imbalanced data; development cost should be used instead

explanation: The notes state clearly that Accuracy is easily misleading with imbalanced data, and the F1 score (the harmonic mean of precision & recall) is good for a rare/imbalanced class. Option B is wrong because it reverses this (accuracy is misleading on imbalanced data, not balanced) and ROI is a business metric, not a suitable replacement. C is wrong because accuracy isn't always reliable. D is wrong because accuracy can still be computed, and development cost is a business metric.

question: During a project review, a stakeholder asks for the metric that specifically measures how well a binary classification model can DISTINGUISH between the positive and negative classes, where 1.0 means perfect separation and 0.5 means random guessing. Which metric is being described?
explanation: The notes define AUC as the model's ability to distinguish the positive/negative class, with 1.0 = perfect and 0.5 = random guessing, matching the description exactly. F1 is the harmonic mean of precision & recall (not described with 1.0/0.5). Accuracy is the proportion of correct predictions. Cost per user is a business metric and doesn't measure class-separation ability.

question: A company is evaluating a generative AI feature and wants to track BUSINESS metrics rather than technical model metrics. Which of the following are business metrics according to the framework? (Choose TWO.)
explanation: According to the notes, business metrics include Cost per user (the cost to serve each user, including inference/tokens) and Customer feedback (customer response/satisfaction). AUC, F1 score, and Accuracy are all model (technical) metrics, measuring how good the model is technically rather than business value.
